# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]** What is a context-free grammar? Explain the four components with an example.

> **Model Answer (3 marks):**
>
> A **Context-Free Grammar (CFG)** is a formal notation used to describe the syntax of programming languages. It is a 4-tuple $G = (V, T, P, S)$ where:
>
> - **$V$** — Finite set of **non-terminals** (syntactic variables written in italics / uppercase, e.g., $E$, $T$).
> - **$T$** — Finite set of **terminals** (the actual tokens, e.g., `id`, `+`, `*`).
> - **$P$** — Finite set of **production rules** of the form $A \rightarrow \alpha$ where $A \in V$ and $\alpha \in (V \cup T)^{*}$.
> - **$S$** — The **start symbol**, a distinguished element of $V$.
>
> **Example:** $G$ for arithmetic expressions:
> $$E \rightarrow E + T \mid T, \quad T \rightarrow T * F \mid F, \quad F \rightarrow (E) \mid \text{id}$$
> Here $V = \{E, T, F\}$, $T = \{+, *, (, ), \text{id}\}$, and $S = E$. **(3 marks)**

---

### Q2. **[KTU University Exam — May 2024]** Define LL(1) parser. State the condition a grammar must satisfy to be LL(1).

> **Model Answer (3 marks):**
>
> An **LL(1) parser** is a top-down, table-driven predictive parser that reads input **L**eft-to-right, produces a **L**eftmost derivation, and uses **1** lookahead symbol to decide which production to apply. **(1 mark)**
>
> **Condition for a grammar to be LL(1):** For every non-terminal $A$ with two or more productions $A \rightarrow \alpha_1 \mid \alpha_2 \mid \dots \mid \alpha_n$:
> $$\text{FIRST}(\alpha_i) \cap \text{FIRST}(\alpha_j) = \emptyset \text{ for all } i \neq j$$
> Additionally, if $\epsilon \in \text{FIRST}(\alpha_i)$, then $\text{FIRST}(\alpha_j) \cap \text{FOLLOW}(A) = \emptyset$ for all $j \neq i$. **(2 marks)**
>
> Equivalently, **no cell in the LL(1) parse table should contain more than one production.**

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

### **Question A (14 Marks)** — LL(1) Predictive Parsing Full Workout

**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2022 / Model Paper 2024 Scheme]**

Consider the following grammar:

$$S \rightarrow a \, B \, D \, h$$
$$B \rightarrow b \, B \mid c$$
$$D \rightarrow d \, D \mid \epsilon$$

#### (a) **[7 marks]** Compute the FIRST and FOLLOW sets for all non-terminals.

**Step 1 — FIRST sets**

$F$ computation rule application:

- $S \rightarrow a B D h$: the first symbol is terminal $a$.
  $$\text{FIRST}(S) = \{a\}$$

- $B \rightarrow b B$: RHS begins with $b$.
- $B \rightarrow c$: RHS begins with $c$.
  $$\text{FIRST}(B) = \{b, c\}$$

- $D \rightarrow d D$: begins with $d$.
- $D \rightarrow \epsilon$: adds $\epsilon$.
  $$\text{FIRST}(D) = \{d, \epsilon\}$$

**Step 2 — FOLLOW sets**

Applying the standard rules:

- Start symbol: $\text{FOLLOW}(S) \supseteq \{\$\}$.

- From $S \rightarrow a \, B \, D \, h$: $B$ is followed by $D$, so add $\text{FIRST}(D) \setminus \{\epsilon\} = \{d\}$ to $\text{FOLLOW}(B)$. Since $\epsilon \in \text{FIRST}(D)$, also add $\text{FOLLOW}(D)$ — but this is circular, so we resolve after computing $D$'s follow.
  $$\text{FOLLOW}(B) = \{d\}$$

- $D$ is followed by $h$ in $S \rightarrow a B D h$. So add $h$:
  $$\text{FOLLOW}(D) \supseteq \{h\}$$

- Since $\text{FOLLOW}(D) = \{h\}$ (also need to check if anything follows $D$ at the right-end; $h$ is the only such symbol), we can now finalize:
  $$\text{FOLLOW}(B) = \{d, h\}$$

- For $S$, since nothing follows $S$ in any production, $\text{FOLLOW}(S) = \{\$\}$.

**Final Table (7 marks awarded for correct computation):**

| Non-Terminal | FIRST | FOLLOW |
|---|---|---|
| $S$ | $\{a\}$ | $\{\$\}$ |
| $B$ | $\{b, c\}$ | $\{d, h\}$ |
| $D$ | $\{d, \epsilon\}$ | $\{h\}$ |

---

#### (b) **[7 marks]** Construct the LL(1) parse table and verify the grammar is LL(1).

**Construction rules:** For each production $A \rightarrow \alpha$, place it in $M[A, a]$ for every $a \in \text{FIRST}(\alpha)$; if $\epsilon \in \text{FIRST}(\alpha)$, place it in $M[A, b]$ for every $b \in \text{FOLLOW}(A)$.

| Production | FIRST(RHS) / FOLLOW | Table entries |
|---|---|---|
| $S \rightarrow aBDh$ | $\{a\}$ | $M[S, a] = S \rightarrow aBDh$ |
| $B \rightarrow bB$ | $\{b\}$ | $M[B, b] = B \rightarrow bB$ |
| $B \rightarrow c$ | $\{c\}$ | $M[B, c] = B \rightarrow c$ |
| $D \rightarrow dD$ | $\{d\}$ | $M[D, d] = D \rightarrow dD$ |
| $D \rightarrow \epsilon$ | $\epsilon$; FOLLOW($D$) = $\{h\}$ | $M[D, h] = D \rightarrow \epsilon$ |

**LL(1) Parse Table:**

| | $a$ | $b$ | $c$ | $d$ | $h$ | $\$$ |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **$S$** | $S \rightarrow aBDh$ | — | — | — | — | — |
| **$B$** | — | $B \rightarrow bB$ | $B \rightarrow c$ | — | — | — |
| **$D$** | — | — | — | $D \rightarrow dD$ | $D \rightarrow \epsilon$ | — |

> [!NOTE]
> **Verification:** No cell has more than one entry. Hence the grammar **is LL(1)** and the predictive parser can be built successfully. **(7 marks)**

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** — Alternative Choice: LR Parsing and Ambiguity

**[KTU University Exam — May 2023]**

#### (a) **[7 marks]** Explain the differences between LL and LR parsers. Discuss why LR parsers are more powerful than LL parsers.

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | LL Parser | LR Parser |
|---|---|---|
| Build direction | Top-down (root → leaves) | Bottom-up (leaves → root) |
| Derivation | Constructs leftmost derivation | Constructs reverse of rightmost derivation |
| Lookahead | 1 token (LL(1)) | 1 token (LR(1)) or more |
| Conflicts | FIRST/FIRST or FIRST/FOLLOW | Shift/Reduce or Reduce/Reduce |
| Implementation | Recursive or table-driven | Always table-driven (DFA) |
| Power | Recognizes LL(1) grammars | Recognizes all deterministic CFLs |

> **Why LR is more powerful (7 marks):**
>
> 1. **Class coverage:** LR(1) recognizes the full class of **deterministic context-free languages**, while LL(1) recognizes only a strict subset. For every LL(1) grammar, an equivalent LR(1) grammar exists, but **not vice-versa**.
> 2. **No left-recursion problem:** LL parsers **cannot handle left-recursive grammars**, requiring grammar rewriting. LR parsers naturally handle left-recursion.
> 3. **Error detection:** LR parsers detect errors as soon as possible (on the earliest shift that leads to a dead state), whereas LL parsers may delay detection.
> 4. **Common grammars are LR:** Most programming language constructs (expressions, statements) are naturally **right-associative** and LR, not LL.
> 5. **Example:** The grammar $S \rightarrow S a \mid b$ is left-recursive and **not LL(1)** but is **LR(1)**. LR handles it directly; LL requires rewriting to $S \rightarrow b S'$, $S' \rightarrow a S' \mid \epsilon$.

---

#### (b) **[7 marks]** Show with a parse tree that the grammar $E \rightarrow E + E \mid E * E \mid (E) \mid \text{id}$ is ambiguous. Rewrite it to obtain an unambiguous grammar.

**Ambiguity Proof:**
Consider the input string: `id + id * id`

**Parse Tree 1** (with `*` binding tighter):

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    E1((E)) --> E2((E))
    E1 --> plus((+))
    E1 --> E3((E))
    E2 --> id1((id))
    E3 --> E4((E))
    E3 --> mul((*))
    E3 --> E5((E))
    E4 --> id2((id))
    E5 --> id3((id))
```

**Parse Tree 2** (with `+` binding tighter):

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    E1((E)) --> E2((E))
    E1 --> mul((*))
    E1 --> E3((E))
    E2 --> E4((E))
    E2 --> plus((+))
    E2 --> E5((E))
    E3 --> id1((id))
    E4 --> id2((id))
    E5 --> id3((id))
```

Since the same string has **two distinct parse trees**, the grammar is **ambiguous**. **(3 marks)**

**Unambiguous Rewrite (4 marks):**
Enforce precedence — `*` higher than `+`, both **left-associative**:

$$E \rightarrow E + T \mid T$$
$$T \rightarrow T * F \mid F$$
$$F \rightarrow (E) \mid \text{id}$$

This grammar:
- Gives `*` higher precedence (handled at $T$ level).
- Forces **left-associativity** via left-recursive rules $E \rightarrow E + T$ and $T \rightarrow T * F$.
- Recognizes the **same language** but with **exactly one parse tree** for every string.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — How Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Forgetting $\epsilon$ handling** in FOLLOW sets when a non-terminal appears at the end of a production — this is the #1 mark-deduction cause.
> 2. **Confusing FIRST and FOLLOW:** FIRST is for **what a string can start with**; FOLLOW is for **what can follow a non-terminal**.
> 3. **In parse table construction,** students often place a production in cells based on **all** FIRST of the LHS non-terminal, not the RHS — this is a critical error.
> 4. **For LL(1) verification,** simply listing productions is not enough; you **must check for multi-entry cells**.
> 5. **For ambiguity proofs,** one derivation is not a proof — you must produce **two distinct parse trees** for the **same** string.
> 6. **In LR parsing,** mixing up **LR(0) items, SLR(1), and LALR(1)** item formats leads to wrong automaton construction.
> 7. **Skipping the $ sign** in FOLLOW($S$) costs 1 mark easily.
> 8. **In recursive descent,** forgetting to order alternatives by **FIRST sets** to avoid backtracking.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist for KTU 2024 Scheme — Syntax and Analysis Parsing**

- **Syntax vs Semantics:** Syntax = form; Semantics = meaning.
- **Grammar $G = (V, T, P, S)$:** Non-terminals, Terminals, Productions, Start symbol.
- **Two derivation types:** Leftmost (LMD — used in top-down) and Rightmost (RMD — used in bottom-up).
- **Parse Tree:** Full concrete tree with all grammar symbols; **AST:** Compressed essential structure.
- **LL(1) Condition:** For every non-terminal $A$, no two distinct productions $A \rightarrow \alpha$ and $A \rightarrow \beta$ may share a token in $\text{FIRST}(\alpha)$ and $\text{FIRST}(\beta)$. If $\epsilon$ is involved, also check against $\text{FOLLOW}(A)$.
- **FIRST Computation:** Apply iteratively until no change. Always include $\epsilon$ if reachable.
- **FOLLOW Computation:** Always seed with `$\text{FOLLOW}(S) = \{\$\}$`. Propagate right-to-left through productions.
- **Parse Table:** Rows = non-terminals; Columns = terminals + `$\text{}$`. Multiple entries in a cell = **NOT LL(1)**.
- **Predictive Parser Stack Convention:** Top of stack on the **right**; matching terminals are popped; non-terminals are expanded using $M[X, a]$.
- **LR Hierarchy (power increasing):** LR(0) ⊂ SLR(1) ⊂ LALR(1) ⊂ LR(1).
- **SLR(1) uses FOLLOW sets** to decide reductions; LALR(1) merges LR(1) states with same cores.
- **YACC / Bison** generate LALR(1) parsers by default.
- **Ambiguity** is proven by exhibiting **two parse trees for the same string**, not by a single example.
- **Dangling-else** is the textbook C/Java ambiguity; resolved by matching `else` with the nearest unmatched `if`.
- **Left-recursive grammars** are **not LL(1)**; use left-factoring to remove **common prefixes** and rewrite to eliminate **left recursion** before predictive parsing.
- **Operators in expressions:** Lower in grammar hierarchy ⇒ lower precedence. Left-recursive rules give **left-associativity**; right-recursive rules give **right-associativity**.
- **Tools to remember:** **YACC / Bison** (LALR), **ANTLR** (ALL(*) — stronger than LL(k)), **Flex / Lex** (lexer), **CUP** (Java LALR).
- **Error Recovery Strategies:** Panic mode (skip tokens), phrase level (insert/delete), error productions, global correction.
- **Compiler Phase Order:** Lexical → Syntax → Semantic → Intermediate Code → Optimization → Code Generation.

> **Final Mantra:** Master **FIRST, FOLLOW, Parse Table Construction, and Parse Tree Drawing** — these four topics account for **>70%** of marks in KTU 2024 Scheme Module 1 examinations on Syntax and Analysis Parsing.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Lexical Structure of Programming Languages
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PECST758 - PROGRAMMING LANGUAGES > - **Module:** Module Module 1 > - **Topic:** Lexical Structure of Programming Languages ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition The **Lexical Structure** of a programming language is the set of basic rules that define how source code characters are grouped into the smallest meaningful units called **lexemes**, which are subsequently classified into **tokens**. It is the lowest level of syntactic organization, defining identifiers, keywords, literals, operators, punctuation, and comments. In compiler architecture, **Lexical Analysis** (also called **scanning** or **tokenization**) is the **first phase** of compilation, where the stream of input characters is read left-to-right and grouped into tokens. Each token is described by a **token name** (an abstract symbol) and an **optional attribute value** (lexeme). > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 1):** Lexical Structure forms the foundation of the Language Processor pipeline. It is the mandatory prerequisite for understanding **Syntax Analysis**, **Parsing**, and **Code Generation** in later modules. ### 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy Think of a **legal contract document** written in English: - A **scanner** reads the document character by character. - It groups characters into **words** (analogous to lexemes). - It then classifies each word into a **legal category** such as *Name*, *Date*, *Clause*, *Definition*, or *Signature* (analogous to tokens). Just as a paralegal scans a contract and tags each meaningful piece of text before a lawyer interprets the document's overall meaning, a **lexical analyzer** scans source code and produces tokens before the parser (the "lawyer") interprets the program's logic. ### 1.3 Core Terminology Snapshot | Term | Meaning | Example (in `int score = 95;`) | |---|---|---| | **Lexeme** | A sequence of characters matching a pattern | `int`, `score`, `=`, `95`, `;` | | **Token** | A category of lexemes (abstract class) | `KEYWORD`, `IDENTIFIER`, `OPERATOR`, `NUMBER`, `PUNCTUATION` | | **Pattern** | A rule (typically a regular expression) describing a set of lexemes | `[a-zA-Z_][a-zA-Z_0-9]*` for identifiers | | **Alphabet (Σ)** | The finite set of allowed input characters | `{a–z, A–Z, 0–9, _, =, ;, +, ...}` | > [!NOTE] > **One Pattern → Many Lexemes → One Token Class.** > Example: All of `score`, `total`, `count`, `x` match the identifier pattern and belong to the token class `IDENTIFIER`. --- ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 Five Standard Token Categories Every modern programming language recognizes these five lexical categories: 1. **Keywords (Reserved Words)** — Predefined words with fixed meaning. They **cannot** be used as identifiers. - Examples: `if`, `else`, `while`, `return`, `int`, `float` (C/Java), `def`, `lambda` (Python). 2. **Identifiers** — Programmer-defined names for variables, functions, classes. - Pattern: `[A-Za-z_][A-Za-z0-9_]*` 3. **Literals (Constants)** — Fixed numeric/string/character values. - Numeric: `42`, `3.14`, `0xFF` - String: `"hello"`, `'a'` 4. **Operators** — Symbols denoting computation/relationship. - Arithmetic: `+`, `-`, `*`, `/`, `%` - Relational: `<`, `>`, `==`, `!=` - Logical: `&&`, `||`, `!` 5. **Punctuation / Separators** — Delimiters and structural markers. - `;`, `,`, `(`, `)`, `{`, `}`, `[`, `]` ### 2.2 Why is Lexical Analysis Required? - **Simplicity:** Parser operates on tokens, not raw characters → drastically reduces parser complexity. - **Efficiency:** Single-pass scanning using fast finite automata → O(n) time. - **Encapsulation:** Removes language-specific character details (whitespace, comments) from syntax rules. - **Reusability:** Generic token interface enables porting parsers across languages. ### 2.3 Pattern → Automaton → Token Mapping The standard KTU-grade derivation follows this exact pipeline: $$ \text{Pattern (Regex)} \;\xrightarrow{\text{Thompson's Construction}}\; \text{NFA} \;\xrightarrow{\text{Subset Construction}}\; \text{DFA} \;\xrightarrow{\text{Minimization}}\; \text{Minimal DFA} $$ ### 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet | Symbol / Concept | Definition / Formula | Use Case | |---|---|---| | **Regular Expression Operators** | `|` (union), `·` (concatenation), `*` (Kleene closure) | Define token patterns | | **Kleene Star** | $r^* = \bigcup_{i=0}^{\infty} r^i$ | Zero or more repetitions | | **Positive Closure** | $r^+ = r \cdot r^*$ | One or more repetitions | | **Optional** | $r? = r \mid \varepsilon$ | Zero or one occurrence | | **DFA Transition** | $\delta: Q \times \Sigma \rightarrow Q$ | Deterministic state move | | **NFA Transition** | $\delta: Q \times \Sigma_\varepsilon \rightarrow \mathcal{P}(Q)$ | Non-deterministic move | | **Lexeme Count** | $L(n) \leq n$ where $n$ = input length | Upper bound on tokens produced | | **Buffer Lookahead** | Two-buffer scheme: $(B_1 \mid B_2)$ | Forward pointer `forward` | | **Lexical Error Recovery** | Panic mode / deletion / insertion / replacement | Resilient scanning | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Critical Engineering Note:** Lexical Analysis runs in **O(n)** time where $n$ is the source length, due to the deterministic nature of minimal DFAs. This is why modern compilers (GCC, Clang, javac) can process millions of lines per second. ### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility - **Compiler Construction:** Used in GCC, LLVM-Clang, MSVC, javac, Python's `compile()`. - **Static Analyzers:** Tools like *SonarQube*, *Coverity*, *Linters* (ESLint, Pylint) rely on token streams. - **IDE Features:** Syntax highlighting, autocomplete, refactoring → all driven by tokenization. - **Domain-Specific Languages (DSLs):** SQL parsers, YAML/JSON processors, configuration file readers. --- ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Worked Example — Designing a Token Recognizer **Problem:** Design the lexical grammar and minimal DFA to recognize the following tokens from a mini-language: - Identifier: `[A-Za-z][A-Za-z0-9]*` - Integer literal: `[0-9]+` - Assignment operator: `=` - Plus operator: `+` - Semicolon: `;` #### Step 1 — Write Regular Expressions for Each Token $$ \begin{aligned} \text{IDENT} &= [A-Za-z][A-Za-z0-9]^{*} \\ \text{NUM} &= [0-9]^{+} \\ \text{ASSIGN} &= `=' \\ \text{PLUS} &= `+' \\ \text{SEMI} &= `;' \end{aligned} $$ #### Step 2 — Concatenate All Patterns Using Union $$ r_{\text{combined}} = \text{IDENT} \mid \text{NUM} \mid \text{ASSIGN} \mid \text{PLUS} \mid \text{SEMI} $$ #### Step 3 — Construct the NFA (Thompson's Construction) Each pattern becomes its own NFA, then a new **start state** $\epsilon$-connects them. For instance, the NFA for $\text{IDENT}$: $$ \begin{aligned} q_0 \xrightarrow{A\text{-}Z \text{ or } a\text{-}z} q_1 \\ q_1 \xrightarrow{A\text{-}Z \text{ or } a\text{-}z \text{ or } 0\text{-}9} q_1 \quad \text{(self-loop)} \end{aligned} $$ Accepting state: $q_1$ with token = `IDENT`. #### Step 4 — Apply Subset Construction (NFA → DFA) For every subset of NFA states reachable via $\epsilon$-transitions, create one DFA state. The transition function $\delta$ is computed as: $$ \delta_D(\{q_i, q_j, \ldots\}, a) = \bigcup_{q \in \{q_i,q_j,\ldots\}} \varepsilon\text{-closure}(\delta_N(q, a)) $$ #### Step 5 — DFA Minimization (Hopcroft's Algorithm) Partition states into **accepting** vs **non-accepting**, then iteratively split partitions where transitions on some input lead to different partitions. Result is the **Minimal DFA** with the fewest possible states. ### 3.2 Analytical Decision Matrix — How the Scanner Chooses a Token When the DFA is in an **accept state** AND `forward` points to a character that **cannot extend** the current lexeme, the longest-match (**Maximal Munch**) rule fires: $$ \text{Selected Token} = \arg\max_{t \in \text{AcceptStates}} \big(\text{length}(\text{lexeme}_t)\big) $$ If two patterns match equal lengths, the **rule priority order** (often specified in the lex specification) breaks the tie. ### 3.3 Numerical Demonstration **Input String:** `total = num1 + 100;` | Step | `forward` reads | Lexeme Accumulated | Current State | Action | |---|---|---|---|---| | 1 | `t` | `t` | start | accept letter → in-IDENT | | 2 | `o` | `to` | in-IDENT | continue | | 3 | `t` | `tot` | in-IDENT | continue | | 4 | `a` | `tota` | in-IDENT | continue | | 5 | `l` | `total` | accept(IDENT) | lookahead ` ` breaks lexeme | | 6 | ` ` | — | — | skip whitespace | | 7 | `=` | `=` | accept(ASSIGN) | emit `` | | 8 | ` ` | — | — | skip whitespace | | 9–11 | `n`,`u`,`m`,`1` | `num1` | accept(IDENT) | emit `` | | 12 | ` ` | — | — | skip whitespace | | 13 | `+` | `+` | accept(PLUS) | emit `` | | 14 | ` ` | — | — | skip whitespace | | 15–17 | `1`,`0`,`0` | `100` | accept(NUM) | emit `` | | 18 | `;` | `;` | accept(SEMI) | emit `` | **Final Token Stream:** $$ \langle \text{IDENT},\text{"total"} \rangle, \langle \text{ASSIGN},\text{"="} \rangle, \langle \text{IDENT},\text{"num1"} \rangle, \langle \text{PLUS},\text{"+"} \rangle, \langle \text{NUM},\text{100} \rangle, \langle \text{SEMI},\text{";"} \rangle $$ ### 3.4 Buffering & Lookahead Strategy Most languages require **two-buffer lookahead** for token boundary detection: $$ \text{Input Stream} = \underbrace{B_1}_{\text{size} = N} \;\vert\; \underbrace{B_2}_{\text{size} = N} $$ $$ \text{forward pointer moves:} \quad \text{forward} \leftarrow (\text{forward}+1) \bmod (2N) $$ When `forward` reaches the midpoint of $B_2$, the next buffer block is loaded into $B_1$. This guarantees a **single-pass O(n)** scan. --- ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Functional Architecture of a Lexical Analyzer ```mermaid flowchart LR A[Source Program
Character Stream] --> B[Input Buffer
Two-Buffer Scheme] B --> C[DFA Engine
State Transition Table] C --> D{Accept State
Reached?} D -- No --> E[Error Handler
Panic Mode Recovery] E --> B D -- Yes --> F[Maximal Munch
Longest Match Logic] F --> G{Ambiguous
Match?} G -- Yes --> H[Priority Rule
Lookup Table] G -- No --> I[Token Output
Token Tuple] H --> I I --> J[Symbol Table
Insert Identifier] J --> K[Token Stream
to Syntax Analyzer] ``` ### 4.2 State Machine for Identifier vs Keyword Recognition ```mermaid stateDiagram-v2 [*] --> S0 S0 --> S1: receive [A-Za-z_] S1 --> S1: receive [A-Za-z0-9_] S1 --> S2: receive non-alphanumeric S2 --> S3: lookup in Keyword Table S2 --> S4: lookup in Symbol Table S3 --> [*]: emit KEYWORD token S4 --> [*]: emit IDENTIFIER token S1 --> E1: illegal char detected E1 --> E2: panic mode recovery E2 --> S0 ``` ### 4.3 Token Classification Hierarchy ```mermaid graph TD Root[Lexical Input
Character Stream] --> T1[Token Group 1
Keywords Reserved] Root --> T2[Token Group 2
Identifiers] Root --> T3[Token Group 3
Literals] Root --> T4[Token Group 4
Operators] Root --> T5[Token Group 5
Punctuation] T3 --> L1[Integer Literal] T3 --> L2[Float Literal] T3 --> L3[Char Literal] T3 --> L4[String Literal] T4 --> O1[Arithmetic Op] T4 --> O2[Relational Op] T4 --> O3[Logical Op] T4 --> O4[Assignment Op] ``` --- ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each) > **Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]** > Differentiate between **Token**, **Lexeme**, and **Pattern** with a suitable example. **Model Answer:** - **Token** is the abstract category or class of a lexical unit (e.g., `IDENTIFIER`, `KEYWORD`). - **Lexeme** is the actual sequence of characters matched from the input source. - **Pattern** is the rule (typically a regular expression) describing the set of all possible lexemes belonging to a token class. **Example:** For the statement `float price = 99.95;` - Pattern: `[a-zA-Z_][a-zA-Z0-9_]*` - Lexeme: `price` - Token: `IDENTIFIER` --- > **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** > What is the role of a **Symbol Table** in lexical analysis? Mention any two entries stored in it. **Model Answer:** The Symbol Table is a data structure maintained by the lexical analyzer (and later phases) that stores information about each identifier appearing in the source program. **Two Typical Entries:** 1. **Identifier Name** — The lexeme string (e.g., `price`). 2. **Attributes** — Type, scope, memory address, line number of declaration. Additional fields: kind (variable/function/array), size, parameter list, return type. --- ### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks — Internal Choice) > **Question A — [KTU University Exam – July 2024]** > > **(a)** Describe the **different phases** involved in the working of a compiler with a neat block diagram. Explain in detail the role of the **Lexical Analyzer** as the front-end of the pipeline. **(7 Marks)** > > **(b)** Construct a **minimal DFA** that recognizes the language consisting of strings over the alphabet $\Sigma = \{a, b\}$ that contain the substring `aba`. **(7 Marks)** #### Model Solution **(a) Compiler Phases:** 1. **Lexical Analysis** — Converts character stream into tokens. 2. **Syntax Analysis** — Builds parse tree using CFG. 3. **Semantic Analysis** — Type checking, scope resolution. 4. **Intermediate Code Generation** — Produces three-address code / TAC. 5. **Code Optimization** — Improves IR efficiency. 6. **Target Code Generation** — Produces assembly/machine code. 7. **Symbol Table Manager** — Maintains cross-phase identifier information. 8. **Error Handler** — Detects and reports errors at each phase. **Role of Lexical Analyzer:** - Reads the source program character by character. - Groups characters into lexemes using patterns. - Produces token tuples ``. - Strips whitespace and comments. - Inserts identifiers into the symbol table. - Reports lexical errors (illegal characters, unterminated strings). **(b) Minimal DFA Construction for strings containing `aba`:** **Step 1:** Identify states representing progress in matching `aba`. - $S_0$: Initial — no prefix matched. - $S_1$: `a` matched (seen prefix `a`). - $S_2$: `ab` matched (seen prefix `ab`). - $S_3$: `aba` matched — **accepting state**. **Step 2:** Define transition function $\delta$: $$ \begin{aligned} \delta(S_0, a) &= S_1, \quad \delta(S_0, b) = S_0 \\ \delta(S_1, a) &= S_1, \quad \delta(S_1, b) = S_2 \\ \delta(S_2, a) &= S_3, \quad \delta(S_2, b) = S_0 \\ \delta(S_3, a) &= S_3, \quad \delta(S_3, b) = S_3 \end{aligned} $$ **Step 3:** Verify minimality — All 4 states are distinguishable; no further minimization possible. **Result:** Minimal DFA with 4 states correctly recognizes $\Sigma^*$ containing `aba`. --- > **Question B — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** > > **(a)** Explain **Regular Expressions** for lexical analysis. Write regex for: identifiers in C, unsigned integers, and relational operators. **(7 Marks)** > > **(b)** What is the **Maximal Munch** rule? Trace the lexical analyzer step-by-step for the input string `x123 = x + 10;` and produce the token stream. **(7 Marks)** #### Model Solution **(a) Regular Expressions for Common Tokens:** $$ \begin{aligned} \text{Identifier (C-style)} &= [A\text{-}Za\text{-}z\_][A\text{-}Za\text{-}z0\text{-}9\_]^{*} \\ \text{Unsigned Integer} &= [0\text{-}9]^{+} \\ \text{Floating-Point Literal} &= [0\text{-}9]^{+}\.[0\text{-}9]^{*} \mid [0\text{-}9]^{*}\.[0\text{-}9]^{+} \\ \text{Relational Operator} &= `<' \mid `>' \mid `<=' \mid `>=' \mid `==' \mid `!=' \\ \text{Assignment Operator} &= `=' \\ \text{Arithmetic Operator} &= `+' \mid `-' \mid `*' \mid `/' \mid `\%' \\ \text{Whitespace (skip)} &= (\text{space} \mid \text{tab} \mid \text{newline})^{+} \end{aligned} $$ **(b) Maximal Munch Rule:** The scanner always returns the **longest possible lexeme** that matches any pattern, ensuring deterministic tokenization. **Step-by-Step Trace for `x123 = x + 10;`:** | Step | Lexeme | Token | Action | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | `x123` | `` | longest match starting with letter/digit | | 2 | ` ` | — | whitespace skipped | | 3 | `=` | `` | single-letter identifier | | 6 | ` ` | — | whitespace skipped | | 7 | `+` | `` | arithmetic op | | 8 | ` ` | — | whitespace skipped | | 9 | `10` | `` | unsigned integer | | 10 | `;` | `` | statement terminator | **Final Token Stream:** $$ \langle \text{ID},\text{"x123"} \rangle \;\; \langle \text{ASSIGN},\text{"="} \rangle \;\; \langle \text{ID},\text{"x"} \rangle \;\; \langle \text{PLUS},\text{"+"} \rangle \;\; \langle \text{NUM},\text{10} \rangle \;\; \langle \text{SEMI},\text{";"} \rangle $$ --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:** > 1. **Confusing lexeme with token** — Examiners deduct 1 mark if you call `price` a "token" instead of a "lexeme" of type `IDENTIFIER`. > 2. **Forgetting the optional `+` operator** in regex for identifiers — C requires `+` after the first character class. > 3. **Missing whitespace handling** — Always mention that whitespace/comments are stripped by the lexer, not the parser. > 4. **Wrong maximal munch** — Students often stop at the *first* match; remember to extend until no more characters fit. > 5. **Not showing symbol table insertion** — When asked about identifiers, you *must* mention their entry into the symbol table. --- ### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember - **Lexical Structure** = Rules defining how source characters form tokens. - **Lexical Analysis** = First compilation phase producing tokens from input stream. - **Token** = Abstract category; **Lexeme** = Actual matched string; **Pattern** = Regex defining a token class. - **Five token classes:** Keywords, Identifiers, Literals, Operators, Punctuation. - **Pipeline:** Pattern → NFA (Thompson) → DFA (Subset Construction) → Minimal DFA (Hopcroft). - **DFA transition:** $\delta: Q \times \Sigma \rightarrow Q$ (deterministic, single next state). - **NFA transition:** $\delta: Q \times \Sigma_\varepsilon \rightarrow \mathcal{P}(Q)$ (subset of next states). - **Maximal Munch Rule:** Always return the *longest* matching lexeme. - **Time Complexity:** $O(n)$ — single left-to-right scan. - **Two-Buffer Input Scheme:** Guarantees lookahead without re-reading input. - **Symbol Table:** Stores identifier lexemes + attributes (type, scope, address). - **Error Recovery:** Panic mode, deletion, insertion, substitution, character repair. - **Whitespace & Comments** are stripped at the lexical level, never reach the parser. - **Lookahead Required** for tokens like `<` vs `<=` vs `<<` — hence buffered scanning. ---

topics.title: Context-Free Grammars and BNFs
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECST758 - PROGRAMMING LANGUAGES
> - **Module:** Module Module 1
> - **Topic:** Context-Free Grammars and BNFs

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is a Context-Free Grammar (CFG)?

A **Context-Free Grammar (CFG)** is a formal mathematical notation used to describe the syntax of programming languages. It consists of a finite set of **production rules** where every rule transforms a single non-terminal symbol into a string of terminals and/or non-terminals, **without regard to the surrounding context** in which that symbol appears.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal Definition (KTU Syllabus Standard):**
> A Context-Free Grammar **G** is defined as a **4-tuple**:
> $$G = (V, T, P, S)$$
> where:
> - $V$ = Finite set of **non-terminals** (variables / syntactic categories)
> - $T$ = Finite set of **terminals** (alphabet / tokens of the language)
> - $P$ = Finite set of **production rules** of the form $A \rightarrow \alpha$, where $A \in V$ and $\alpha \in (V \cup T)^{*}$
> - $S$ = Designated **start symbol**, where $S \in V$

---

## 1.2 What is Backus-Naur Form (BNF)?

The **Backus-Naur Form (BNF)** is the original meta-language used to represent **Context-Free Grammars** for programming languages. It was invented by **John Backus** (1959) and refined by **Peter Naur** (1960) for defining the syntax of the **ALGOL 60** language. BNF provides a compact, readable textual notation for expressing CFG production rules.

> [!NOTE]
> **BNF is essentially a notational convention for writing CFGs.** Every language definable by a BNF is a context-free language. The notation differs from formal CFG only in the symbols used to denote productions.

### BNF Symbol Conventions:

| Symbol | Meaning | Example |
|---|---|---|
| `< >` | Non-terminal symbol (variable) | `<expression>` |
| `::=` | "is defined as" (production arrow) | `<A> ::= a <B>` |
| `\|` | Logical OR (alternative) | `<A> ::= a \| b` |

---

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy: The Recipe Book

> [!TIP]
> **Think of a CFG as a Recipe Book for Sentences.**
>
> Imagine a chef who wants to teach cooking in a structured way. He writes a book where:
> - **Non-terminals (`<Dish>`, `<Ingredient>`, `<Step>`)** are like *category headings* — they describe what kind of thing you are making.
> - **Terminals (`"salt"`, `"onion"`, `"fry"`, `"boil"`)** are the actual *raw ingredients and actions* — final words that cannot be broken down further.
> - **Productions (`<Dish> ::= <Ingredient> + <Step>`)** are the *recipes themselves*, telling you how to combine categories into other categories until you reach the actual ingredients.
> - **The Start Symbol (`<MainCourse>`)** is the *title of the book* — every recipe starts by replacing this top-level heading.
>
> To "parse" a sentence, you keep applying recipes (rewriting non-terminals) until only ingredients (terminals) remain. The final cooked dish is the **derived sentence** in the language!

---

## 1.4 Why Are CFGs/BNFs Important in Programming Languages?

- **Compiler Design**: The front-end of every modern compiler (lexical + syntax analysis) uses CFG-based parsing. Tools like **YACC (Yet Another Compiler-Compiler)**, **ANTLR**, and **Bison** are built directly on CFG foundations.
- **Language Specification**: Languages such as **Pascal**, **C**, **Java**, and **Python** have their syntax formally described using BNF or its variants (EBNF, ABNF).
- **Automatic Parser Generation**: Given a CFG, parser generators can automatically produce parsers — eliminating manual coding.
- **Language Theory Foundation**: Defines the boundaries of what *can* and *cannot* be expressed in syntax (e.g., balanced parentheses require CFGs; matching `a^n b^n c^n` requires more powerful formalisms).

---

## 1.5 Key Terminology (Highlighted for Quick Recall)

- **Terminal**: A basic symbol (token) that cannot be further decomposed — e.g., `id`, `+`, `(`, `)`.
- **Non-Terminal**: A syntactic variable representing a phrase or construct — e.g., `<expression>`, `<statement>`.
- **Production Rule**: A rewriting rule of the form $A \rightarrow \alpha$.
- **Derivation**: A sequence of replacements applying productions to reach a string of terminals.
- **Sentential Form**: Any string derivable from $S$ (may contain non-terminals).
- **Sentence**: A sentential form containing *only* terminals (a valid program fragment).
- **Language $L(G)$**: The set of all sentences derivable from $S$.
- **Ambiguity**: A grammar is ambiguous if some string has two or more distinct parse trees.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Anatomy of a Context-Free Grammar

A CFG is fully characterized by its four components $(V, T, P, S)$. Let us analyze each component in detail.

### 2.1.1 Non-Terminals ($V$)

Non-terminals are **placeholders** representing groups of terminal strings. They always appear on the **left-hand side (LHS)** of at least one production.

> [!NOTE]
> Conventionally, non-terminals are written in **uppercase letters** ($A, B, S, E$) or **angle-bracketed names** (`<expression>`) in BNF.

### 2.1.2 Terminals ($T$)

Terminals are the **atomic symbols** of the language — the actual tokens produced by the lexer. They **never** appear on the LHS of any production.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Conventionally, terminals are written in **lowercase letters** ($a, b, c$), **boldface** (`id`, `num`), or **quoted strings** (`"if"`, `"while"`).

### 2.1.3 Productions ($P$)

Each production has the form:
$$A \rightarrow \alpha$$
where $A \in V$ and $\alpha \in (V \cup T)^{*}$.

The symbol **$\rightarrow$** (or `::=` in BNF) is read as *"can be replaced by"* or *"derives"*.

### 2.1.4 Start Symbol ($S$)

$S$ is a **distinguished non-terminal** from which all derivations begin. It defines the *root* of any parse tree.

---

## 2.2 BNF Notation Rules and Variants

### 2.2.1 Standard BNF

The original BNF introduced by Naur for ALGOL 60 uses these conventions:

- Non-terminals enclosed in angle brackets: `<expression>`
- The "is defined as" symbol: `::=`
- Alternatives separated by vertical bar: `|`
- Concatenation by juxtaposition: `<expr> <op> <term>`

### 2.2.2 Extended BNF (EBNF)

EBNF adds **convenience notation** for repetition and optional elements:

| EBNF Construct | Meaning | Equivalent BNF |
|---|---|---|
| `{ x }` | Zero or more repetitions of $x$ | $A \rightarrow A\,x \mid \epsilon$ |
| `[ x ]` | Optional occurrence of $x$ (0 or 1) | $A \rightarrow x \mid \epsilon$ |
| `( x \| y )` | Grouping with alternation | Use of parentheses |
| `x ... y` | Special repetition (used in some dialects) | Repetition rule |

### 2.2.3 Augmented BNF (ABNF) — used in Internet RFCs

ABNF (used in HTTP, SIP, etc.) is similar to BNF but uses different terminal quoting conventions.

---

## 2.3 Derivation Concepts

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Derivation** is the process of applying production rules sequentially, starting from $S$, to obtain a terminal string.

### 2.3.1 Types of Derivations

Given $A \rightarrow \gamma$ and a sentential form $\alpha A \beta$:

1. **Leftmost Derivation ($\Rightarrow_{lm}$)**: The **leftmost non-terminal** in the current sentential form is replaced at each step.
2. **Rightmost Derivation ($\Rightarrow_{rm}$)**: The **rightmost non-terminal** in the current sentential form is replaced at each step.
3. **Mixed Derivation**: Any non-terminal may be chosen at each step.

> [!NOTE]
> A grammar is **ambiguous** if *any* terminal string has two or more distinct parse trees — equivalently, two or more distinct leftmost (or rightmost) derivations.

### 2.3.2 Recursive Productions

A production of the form $A \rightarrow \alpha A \beta$ makes the grammar **recursive**.

- **Left-Recursive**: $A \rightarrow A \alpha$ — dangerous for top-down parsers; must be eliminated.
- **Right-Recursive**: $A \rightarrow \alpha A$ — preferred for top-down parsers.
- **Indirectly Recursive**: $A \rightarrow B \alpha$, $B \rightarrow A \beta$ — recursive through a chain.

---

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Concept | Formula / Notation | Description |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | CFG Tuple | $G = (V, T, P, S)$ | Four components of a CFG |
| 2 | Production Rule | $A \rightarrow \alpha$ | $A \in V$, $\alpha \in (V \cup T)^{*}$ |
| 3 | Direct Derivation | $\alpha A \beta \Rightarrow \alpha \gamma \beta$ | One-step replacement using $A \rightarrow \gamma$ |
| 4 | Multi-step Derivation | $\alpha \Rightarrow^{*} \beta$ | Zero or more derivation steps |
| 5 | Language Defined by $G$ | $L(G) = \{w \in T^{*} \mid S \Rightarrow^{*} w\}$ | All terminal strings reachable from $S$ |
| 6 | Kleene Star (Repetition) | $(V \cup T)^{*}$ | All finite strings over $V \cup T$ |
| 7 | Epsilon Production | $A \rightarrow \epsilon$ | $A$ can derive the empty string |
| 8 | BNF Derives | $::=$ or $::=$ | Equivalent to $\rightarrow$ in CFG |
| 9 | Length of Derivation | $\vert \text{derivation} \vert$ | Number of derivation steps |
| 10 | Parse Tree | Rooted ordered tree | Leaves form the derived terminal string |
| 11 | Ambiguity Condition | $\exists w : 2+$ parse trees for $w$ | Grammar is ambiguous |
| 12 | Left-Recursion | $A \rightarrow A \alpha$ | Causes infinite recursion in top-down parsing |
| 13 | Right-Recursion | $A \rightarrow \alpha A$ | Safe for top-down recursive descent parsing |
| 14 | EBNF Repetition | $\{ x \}$ | Equivalent to $X \rightarrow x X \mid \epsilon$ |
| 15 | EBNF Optional | $[ x ]$ | Equivalent to $X \rightarrow x \mid \epsilon$ |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Pitfall:** In the exam, do **NOT** confuse terminals (lowercase, e.g., `a`, `id`) with non-terminals (uppercase, e.g., `A`, `E`). This single mistake can invalidate an entire derivation!

---

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Domain | Application of CFG/BNF |
|---|---|
| **Compiler Design** | Parser generation tools (YACC, ANTLR, Bison, Lark) |
| **Software Engineering** | Defining DSLs (Domain-Specific Languages) like SQL, HTML, JSON |
| **Network Protocols** | HTTP, SMTP, SIP message formats defined using ABNF |
| **Bioinformatics** | Modeling RNA secondary structure, protein folding patterns |
| **Natural Language Processing** | Parsing English & multilingual grammar (CFG-induced parse trees) |
| **Formal Verification** | Model checking of recursive systems (e.g., pushdown automata equivalence) |
| **Markup Languages** | XML DTD, JSON Schema (CFG-inspired validation) |
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Worked Example 1: Arithmetic Expression Grammar

### Given Grammar $G_1$ (in CFG form):

$$E \rightarrow E + T \mid T$$
$$T \rightarrow T * F \mid F$$
$$F \rightarrow (E) \mid id$$

Here, $V = \{E, T, F\}$, $T = \{+, *, (, ), id\}$, $S = E$.

### Task: Derive the string `id + id * id` using leftmost derivation.

We show every single step explicitly:

> **Step 1:** Start with the start symbol.
> $$E$$

> **Step 2:** Apply $E \rightarrow E + T$ (we choose this rule because the target string begins with `id`, which $E$ will eventually produce, and the target contains `+`).
> $$E \Rightarrow E + T$$

> **Step 3:** Apply $E \rightarrow T$ to the leftmost non-terminal.
> $$E \Rightarrow E + T \Rightarrow T + T$$

> **Step 4:** Apply $T \rightarrow T * F$ (we anticipate `id * id` later in the right side).
> $$T + T \Rightarrow T * F + T$$

> **Step 5:** Apply $T \rightarrow F$ to the leftmost non-terminal.
> $$T * F + T \Rightarrow F * F + T$$

> **Step 6:** Apply $F \rightarrow id$ (leftmost non-terminal).
> $$F * F + T \Rightarrow id * F + T$$

> **Step 7:** Apply $F \rightarrow id$.
> $$id * F + T \Rightarrow id * id + T$$

> **Step 8:** Apply $T \rightarrow F$.
> $$id * id + T \Rightarrow id * id + F$$

> **Step 9:** Apply $F \rightarrow id$.
> $$id * id + F \Rightarrow id * id + id$$

**Final derived string:** $\mathbf{id + id * id}$ ✓

The complete leftmost derivation in compact form:

$$
\begin{aligned}
E &\Rightarrow E + T \\
&\Rightarrow T + T \\
&\Rightarrow T * F + T \\
&\Rightarrow F * F + T \\
&\Rightarrow id * F + T \\
&\Rightarrow id * id + T \\
&\Rightarrow id * id + F \\
&\Rightarrow id * id + id
\end{aligned}
$$

---

## 3.2 Worked Example 2: BNF for a Mini Language — If-Statement

### Given BNF Specification:

```
<stmt>     ::= <if-stmt> | <assign>
<if-stmt>  ::= if ( <cond> ) <stmt> | if ( <cond> ) <stmt> else <stmt>
<assign>   ::= id = <expr> ;
<cond>     ::= <expr> <relop> <expr>
<expr>     ::= <expr> + <term> | <term>
<term>     ::= <term> * <factor> | <factor>
<factor>   ::= ( <expr> ) | id | num
<relop>    ::= < | > | == | !=
```

### Task: Derive the sentence: `if (id > num) id = id + num ;`

**Step-by-step leftmost derivation:**

> **Step 1:** Start symbol.
> `<stmt>`

> **Step 2:** Apply `<stmt> → <if-stmt>`.
> `<stmt> ⇒ <if-stmt>`

> **Step 3:** Apply `<if-stmt> → if ( <cond> ) <stmt>` (choose the no-else alternative first).
> `<if-stmt> ⇒ if ( <cond> ) <stmt>`

> **Step 4:** Apply `<cond> → <expr> <relop> <expr>`.
> `⇒ if ( <expr> <relop> <expr> ) <stmt>`

> **Step 5:** Apply `<expr> → <term>`.
> `⇒ if ( <term> <relop> <expr> ) <stmt>`

> **Step 6:** Apply `<term> → <factor>` and then `<factor> → id`.
> `⇒ if ( id <relop> <expr> ) <stmt>`

> **Step 7:** Apply `<relop> → >`.
> `⇒ if ( id > <expr> ) <stmt>`

> **Step 8:** Apply `<expr> → <term>` and then `<term> → <factor>` and then `<factor> → num`.
> `⇒ if ( id > num ) <stmt>`

> **Step 9:** Apply `<stmt> → <assign>`.
> `⇒ if ( id > num ) <assign>`

> **Step 10:** Apply `<assign> → id = <expr> ;`.
> `⇒ if ( id > num ) id = <expr> ;`

> **Step 11:** Apply `<expr> → <expr> + <term>`.
> `⇒ if ( id > num ) id = <expr> + <term> ;`

> **Step 12:** Apply `<expr> → <term>` and `<term> → <factor>` and `<factor> → id`.
> `⇒ if ( id > num ) id = id + <term> ;`

> **Step 13:** Apply `<term> → <factor>` and `<factor> → num`.
> `⇒ if ( id > num ) id = id + num ;`

**Final derived sentence:** ✓ matches the target string.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Distinguishing Key Concepts

| Dimension | Context-Free Grammar (CFG) | Backus-Naur Form (BNF) | Regular Grammar |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Power** | Powerful (Type-2 in Chomsky hierarchy) | Same as CFG | Less powerful (Type-3) |
| **LHS of Rule** | Exactly one non-terminal | One non-terminal | One non-terminal |
| **RHS of Rule** | Any string of $V \cup T$ | Any string with `<>` and literals | Restricted form |
| **Notation** | $A \rightarrow \alpha$ | `<A> ::= α` | $A \rightarrow aB$ or $A \rightarrow a$ |
| **Recognizer** | Pushdown Automaton | Pushdown Automaton | Finite Automaton |
| **Can describe** | Nested/recursive structures (palindromes, balanced parens) | Same as CFG | Only linear patterns (no nesting) |
| **Used in** | Theory of computation | Real language specs (ALGOL, Pascal, Java) | Lexical analysis |
| **Ambiguity** | Can be ambiguous | Inherits ambiguity | Mostly unambiguous |

---

## 3.4 Derivation Length Calculation

For the grammar $G_1$ in Example 3.1, the derivation of `id + id * id` has **8 derivation steps** (each `⇒` is one step). The **yield** (string read at leaves) of the parse tree equals the derived sentence.

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Quick Check:** If a derivation takes $n$ steps and each step applies one production, the **derivation length** is $n$. The **derivation tree depth** equals the maximum nested non-terminals during derivation.

---

## 3.5 Worked Example 3: Eliminating Ambiguity

### Original Ambiguous Grammar:

$$S \rightarrow S + S \mid S * S \mid (S) \mid id$$

> **Demonstrating ambiguity** for the string `id + id + id`:

**Parse Tree 1 (left-associative `+`):**
- $S \Rightarrow S + S \Rightarrow S + S + S \Rightarrow id + S + S \Rightarrow id + id + S \Rightarrow id + id + id$

**Parse Tree 2 (right-associative `+`):**
- $S \Rightarrow S + S \Rightarrow id + S \Rightarrow id + S + S \Rightarrow id + id + S \Rightarrow id + id + id$

Both derivations produce the **same string** but have **different parse tree shapes** → **grammar is ambiguous**.

### Unambiguous Equivalent Grammar:

$$
\begin{aligned}
E &\rightarrow E + T \mid T \\
T &\rightarrow T * F \mid F \\
F &\rightarrow (E) \mid id
\end{aligned}
$$

This grammar forces `+` and `*` to be left-associative, eliminating ambiguity.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Exam Note:** Disambiguating a grammar by introducing precedence levels is a frequent 7-mark question. Remember the standard pattern: $E \rightarrow E + T \mid T$, $T \rightarrow T * F \mid F$, $F \rightarrow id$.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram: Components of a Context-Free Grammar

The following Mermaid block diagram illustrates the four components of a CFG and their interactions:

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    CFG["CFG = V T P S"]:::core
    V["V Non-Terminals"]:::nt
    T["T Terminals"]:::term
    P["P Production Rules"]:::prod
    S["S Start Symbol"]:::start
    L["Language L G"]:::lang
    V1["Example E T F"]:::ntEx
    T1["Example id plus star paren"]:::termEx
    P1["A implies alpha"]:::prodEx
    S1["Example E expression"]:::startEx
    CFG --> V
    CFG --> T
    CFG --> P
    CFG --> S
    S --> L
    V --> V1
    T --> T1
    P --> P1
    S --> S1
    classDef core fill:#1e3a8a,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px,color:#fff
    classDef nt fill:#fef3c7,stroke:#92400e,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef term fill:#d1fae5,stroke:#065f46,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef prod fill:#fee2e2,stroke:#991b1b,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef start fill:#ede9fe,stroke:#5b21b6,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef lang fill:#fce7f3,stroke:#9d174d,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef ntEx fill:#fef9c3,stroke:#854d0e,stroke-width:1px,color:#000,font-size:10px
    classDef termEx fill:#dcfce7,stroke:#166534,stroke-width:1px,color:#000,font-size:10px
    classDef prodEx fill:#fecaca,stroke:#7f1d1d,stroke-width:1px,color:#000,font-size:10px
    classDef startEx fill:#ddd6fe,stroke:#4c1d95,stroke-width:1px,color:#000,font-size:10px
```

---

## 4.2 Parse Tree for `id + id * id`

Using grammar $G_1$, the parse tree for the string `id + id * id` is shown below:

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    ROOT["E"]:::nodeLevel1
    L1["E"]:::nodeLevel2
    PLUS["PLUS"]:::nodeLevel3
    R1["T"]:::nodeLevel2
    LL1["T"]:::nodeLevel3
    MUL["STAR"]:::nodeLevel3
    R2["F"]:::nodeLevel3
    LL2["F"]:::nodeLevel4
    R3["id"]:::nodeLevel5
    R4["id"]:::nodeLevel5
    R5["id"]:::nodeLevel5
    ROOT --> L1
    ROOT --> PLUS
    ROOT --> R1
    L1 --> LL1
    L1 --> MUL
    L1 --> R2
    LL1 --> LL2
    LL1 --> R3
    R2 --> R4
    R1 --> R5
    classDef nodeLevel1 fill:#1e40af,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px,color:#fff
    classDef nodeLevel2 fill:#3b82f6,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px,color:#fff
    classDef nodeLevel3 fill:#93c5fd,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef nodeLevel4 fill:#dbeafe,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef nodeLevel5 fill:#fef3c7,stroke:#92400e,stroke-width:1px,color:#000
```

> **Tree Reading Guide:**
> - **Internal nodes** = Non-terminals ($E$, $T$, $F$)
> - **Leaf nodes** = Terminals (`id`, `+`, `*`)
> - **In-order traversal of leaves** = derived string: `id + id * id`

---

## 4.3 Derivation Flow: Leftmost Derivation Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A["Step 0 S"]:::stepA
    B["Step 1 alpha 1"]:::stepB
    C["Step 2 alpha 2"]:::stepC
    D["Step 3 alpha 3"]:::stepD
    E["Step n w terminals"]:::stepE
    R1["Production 1"]:::rule
    R2["Production 2"]:::rule
    R3["Production 3"]:::rule
    Rn["Production n"]:::rule
    A --R1--> B
    B --R2--> C
    C --R3--> D
    D --Rn--> E
    classDef stepA fill:#7c2d12,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px,color:#fff
    classDef stepB fill:#9a3412,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px,color:#fff
    classDef stepC fill:#c2410c,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px,color:#fff
    classDef stepD fill:#ea580c,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px,color:#fff
    classDef stepE fill:#16a34a,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px,color:#fff
    classDef rule fill:#fde68a,stroke:#92400e,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
```

---

## 4.4 BNF Production Rule Anatomy (Block-Level Schematic)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    LHS["LHS Non Terminal A"]:::lhs
    ARROW["DERIVES"]:::arrow
    RHS["RHS String alpha"]:::rhs
    ALT["OR"]:::alt
    OPT["EBNF OPTIONAL x"]:::opt
    REP["EBNF REPETITION x times"]:::rep
    LHS --> ARROW --> RHS
    RHS -.-> ALT
    RHS -.-> OPT
    RHS -.-> REP
