**Syntax (Syntactic Structure):** The set of formal rules specifying the **well-formed arrangement** of tokens into expressions, statements, declarations, and entire programs. Syntax is described using formal grammar notations such as **BNF (Backus–Naur Form)**, **EBNF (Extended BNF)**, and **Context-Free Grammars (CFG)**.

**Semantics (Meaning):** The discipline that assigns **meaning** to syntactically valid constructs. It determines what a program *computes* and *does* when executed. Semantics is sub-classified as **Static Semantics** (e.g., type checking, scope rules — verified before execution) and **Dynamic Semantics** (e.g., program behavior at runtime).

### Conceptual Analogy — Learning a Foreign Language

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy: Learning English**
> - **Lexics** = Your *vocabulary* and *spelling*. The English word **"Apple"** is one indivisible lexical unit — you cannot break it as "Ap-ple" mid-word.
> - **Syntax** = The *grammar rules* — e.g., *"The cat sat on the mat"* is grammatical, but *"Cat the on sat mat the"* is not, even though every word exists.
> - **Semantics** = The *meaning* — *"The cat sat on the mat"* tells a story; *"The mat sat on the cat"* is grammatically fine but tells a different, syntactically valid yet semantically altered story.

A sentence may be **lexically** perfect (correct words), **syntactically** correct (well-formed), yet **semantically** absurd. The same applies to programs.

### Worked Programmatic Example

Consider the statement:
```
int total = a + 10;
```

- **Lexical View:** The lexer reads the character stream and produces tokens — `int` (keyword), `total` (identifier), `=` (operator), `a` (identifier), `+` (operator), `10` (integer literal), `;` (punctuation).
- **Syntactic View:** The parser checks that this token stream matches the grammar rule for a *variable declaration with initialization*.
- **Semantic View:** The compiler/type-checker verifies `a` is of an integer-compatible type; at runtime, `total` is bound to the sum of `a` and **10**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> A program that is **lexically valid** need not be **syntactically valid**; one that is **syntactically valid** need not be **semantically valid**. All three layers must succeed for a program to be executable.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Lexical Analysis — Operational Breakdown

The lexer operates as a **finite-state machine (FSM)** that scans the input left-to-right and groups characters into tokens using pattern-matching rules (regular expressions). Each token has the form:
$$\text{Token} = \langle \text{Tag}, \text{Lexeme}, \text{Attribute} \rangle$$

- **Tag** — token category (e.g., `ID`, `NUM`, `OP`).
- **Lexeme** — the actual character sequence matched.
- **Attribute** — auxiliary value (e.g., symbol-table pointer, numeric value).

**Categories of Tokens (Lexical Elements):**
- **Keywords / Reserved Words** — `if`, `while`, `int`, `return` (fixed meaning).
- **Identifiers** — user-defined names matching `letter (letter \vert digit)*`.
- **Operators** — arithmetic, relational, logical (`+`, `==`, `&&`).
- **Literals / Constants** — numeric, character, string, boolean.
- **Punctuation / Delimiters** — `( ) { } ; ,`.
- **Comments & Whitespace** — ignored by the lexer (non-tokens).

### 2.2 Syntactic Analysis — Operational Breakdown

The parser takes the token stream and verifies it against a **Context-Free Grammar (CFG)** of the form:
$$G = (V, T, P, S)$$
where $V$ = non-terminals, $T$ = terminals (tokens), $P$ = productions, $S$ = start symbol.

The parser builds a **Parse Tree** or **Abstract Syntax Tree (AST)**, demonstrating that the token stream can be *derived* from $S$ via the productions in $P$.

**Why syntax matters:** It catches structural errors early (e.g., missing semicolons, unmatched parentheses, misplaced operators) before any execution occurs.

### 2.3 Semantic Analysis — Operational Breakdown

After syntax validation, the semantic analyzer:
1. Builds and consults the **Symbol Table** (scope, type, lifetime of identifiers).
2. Performs **Type Checking** (e.g., `"hello" + 5` is a type error).
3. Resolves **Overloading** and **Polymorphism**.
4. Generates **Intermediate Code** (three-address code, TAC) as a precursor to runtime execution.

### 2.4 KTU Formula / Cheat Sheet

| Aspect | Lexics | Syntax | Semantics |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Concern** | Token formation | Token arrangement | Meaning of programs |
| **Input** | Character stream | Token stream | Parse tree / AST |
| **Output** | Token list | Parse tree / AST | Intermediate code / meaning |
| **Formalism** | Regular Expressions | BNF / EBNF / CFG | Operational / Denotational / Axiomatic |
| **Automaton** | Finite Automaton (DFA/NFA) | Pushdown Automaton | Abstract Interpreter |
| **Error Example** | `$abc` (invalid identifier start) | `x = + 5;` (unary `+` misuse) | `int x = "hello" + 5;` (type mismatch) |
| **Detection Phase** | Lexical phase | Parsing phase | Type-check / Runtime |
| **Verifies** | Spacing $\vert$ Character legality | Structural well-formedness | Type $\vert$ Scope $\vert$ Runtime behavior |

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Compiler Construction (GCC, LLVM, Clang):** Front-end uses lex (Flex) and yacc (Bison) tools to implement lexical and syntactic analysis.
- **Static Code Analyzers (SonarQube, ESLint):** Rely on lexical tokenization + AST traversal.
- **IDEs (VS Code, IntelliJ):** Use these layers to provide syntax highlighting (lexical), error underlining (syntactic), and refactoring/intellisense (semantic).
- **Formal Verification Tools (Coq, Isabelle, Frama-C):** Apply axiomatic semantics to mathematically prove program correctness — critical in **aerospace, medical devices, and autonomous-vehicle software**.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 End-to-End Walkthrough of `int total = a + 10;`

We trace the source code through all three layers to cement the distinctions.

#### Step 1 — Lexical Analysis (Tokenizer)

The character stream is scanned and grouped:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Source: } & \texttt{int total = a + 10;} \\[4pt]
\text{Token \#1: } & \langle \text{KEYWORD}, \ \texttt{int}, \ \text{null} \rangle \\[2pt]
\text{Token \#2: } & \langle \text{ID}, \ \texttt{total}, \ \text{ptr to symbol table} \rangle \\[2pt]
\text{Token \#3: } & \langle \text{OP\_ASSIGN}, \ \texttt{=}, \ \text{null} \rangle \\[2pt]
\text{Token \#4: } & \langle \text{ID}, \ \texttt{a}, \ \text{ptr to symbol table} \rangle \\[2pt]
\text{Token \#5: } & \langle \text{OP\_PLUS}, \ \texttt{+}, \ \text{null} \rangle \\[2pt]
\text{Token \#6: } & \langle \text{NUM}, \ \texttt{10}, \ \text{value} = 10 \rangle \\[2pt]
\text{Token \#7: } & \langle \text{SEMICOLON}, \ \texttt{;}, \ \text{null} \rangle
\end{aligned}
$$

**Logic:** Whitespace is discarded; the longest valid lexeme is preferred (maximal-munch rule); identifiers and numbers are matched by their respective regular expressions.

#### Step 2 — Syntax Analysis (Parser)

The parser attempts to derive the token stream from the start symbol `S` (declaration) using the following BNF:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\langle \text{decl} \rangle &::= \langle \text{type} \rangle \ \langle \text{id} \rangle \ \text{=} \ \langle \text{expr} \rangle \ \text{;} \\[2pt]
\langle \text{type} \rangle &::= \texttt{int} \ \vert \ \texttt{float} \ \vert \ \texttt{char} \\[2pt]
\langle \text{expr} \rangle &::= \langle \text{expr} \rangle \ \text{+} \ \langle \text{term} \rangle \ \vert \ \langle \text{term} \rangle \\[2pt]
\langle \text{term} \rangle &::= \langle \text{id} \rangle \ \vert \ \langle \text{num} \rangle
\end{aligned}
$$

A successful **leftmost derivation** would proceed:

$$
\begin{aligned}
S &\Rightarrow \langle \text{decl} \rangle \\[2pt]
&\Rightarrow \ \texttt{int} \ \langle \text{id} \rangle \ \text{=} \ \langle \text{expr} \rangle \ \text{;} \\[2pt]
&\Rightarrow \ \texttt{int total =} \ \langle \text{expr} \rangle \ \text{;} \\[2pt]
&\Rightarrow \ \texttt{int total =} \ \langle \text{term} \rangle \ \text{+} \ \langle \text{term} \rangle \ \text{;} \\[2pt]
&\Rightarrow \ \texttt{int total = a + 10 ;}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Logic:** At every step, a non-terminal is replaced using one of the grammar productions until only terminals (tokens) remain. The parser also constructs the AST:
```
        decl
       / | \  \
    int id  = expr
        |      |
      total   expr + term
                |     |
               id   num
                |     |
                a    10
```

#### Step 3 — Semantic Analysis (Meaning + Type Check)

The semantic phase consults the symbol table to verify:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{(i) } & \texttt{a} \in \text{SymbolTable} \quad \wedge \quad \text{type}(a) \in \{\texttt{int}, \texttt{float}, \texttt{char}\} \\[2pt]
\text{(ii) } & \text{type}(\texttt{int literal 10}) = \texttt{int} \\[2pt]
\text{(iii) } & \text{coercion}(\text{type}(a), \texttt{int}) \ \text{must be valid} \\[2pt]
\text{(iv) } & \text{result of } (a + 10) \ \text{must be assignable to} \ \texttt{total}
\end{aligned}
$$

If all checks succeed, the compiler emits **Three-Address Code (TAC):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
t_1 &= a + 10 \\[2pt]
\text{total} &= t_1
\end{aligned}
$$

At runtime, this evaluation yields a concrete numerical value for `total`.

### 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Where Each Error Is Detected

| Source Code | Lexical Error? | Syntax Error? | Semantic Error? | Executable? |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| `int x = 5 ;` | No | No | No | Yes |
| `2x = 5;` | Yes — invalid identifier | — | — | No |
| `x 5 = +;` | No | Yes — token order violates grammar | — | No |
| `int x = "hi" + 5;` | No | No | Yes — type mismatch | No |
| `return;` in `void main()` | No | No | No | Yes |
| `x := 5;` (Pascal `:=` in C) | No | Yes — operator not in grammar | — | No |

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Takeaway from the Matrix:** The three layers form a *cascade* — a failure at any layer halts the program. Designers of robust compilers therefore invest in precise *specifications* at each layer.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 The Three-Layer Language Description Model

The following Mermaid diagram captures the **block-level functional architecture** of how a source program is progressively validated through the lexics → syntax → semantics pipeline.

```mermaid
graph TD
    SRC[Source Code Character Stream] --> LEX[LEXICAL ANALYZER LEXER]
    LEX --> TOK[Token Stream: Tag Lexeme Attribute]
    TOK --> PAR[SYNTAX ANALYZER PARSER]
    PAR --> CHK1{Grammatically Well Formed?}
    CHK1 -->|No| SYERR[Syntax Error Report]
    CHK1 -->|Yes| AST[Parse Tree and Abstract Syntax Tree]
    AST --> SEM[SEMANTIC ANALYZER]
    SEM --> CHK2{Type Scope and Meaning Valid?}
    CHK2 -->|No| SMERR[Semantic Error Report]
    CHK2 -->|Yes| TAC[Intermediate Code Three Address Code]
    TAC --> OPT[Optimizer Optional]
    OPT --> CG[Code Generator]
    CG --> EXE[Executable Machine Code]
```

### 4.2 Relationship Map Between Lexics, Syntax, and Semantics

```mermaid
graph LR
    L[LEXICS] -->|feeds tokens to| S[SYNTAX]
    S -->|feeds AST to| M[SEMANTICS]
    L -.->|uses| REG[Regular Expressions and DFA]
    S -.->|uses| BNF[BNF and EBNF and CFG]
    M -.->|uses| OS[Operational Semantics]
    M -.->|uses| DS[Denotational Semantics]
    M -.->|uses| AS[Axiomatic Semantics]
    L --> LERR[Lexical Errors: illegal characters or malformed tokens]
    S --> SERR[Syntax Errors: structure violation]
    M --> MERR[Semantic Errors: type and scope and logic faults]
```

### 4.3 Comparative Flow — Valid vs Invalid Programs

```mermaid
graph TD
    P[Program Under Test] --> Q1{All characters form valid tokens?}
    Q1 -->|No| OUT1[Rejected at Lexical Phase]
    Q1 -->|Yes| Q2{Token order matches grammar?}
    Q2 -->|No| OUT2[Rejected at Syntax Phase]
    Q2 -->|Yes| Q3{Type and Scope and Meaning consistent?}
    Q3 -->|No| OUT3[Rejected at Semantic Phase]
    Q3 -->|Yes| OUT4[Accepted: Ready for Code Generation]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the Diagrams:** Every rectangular node is a *processing stage*; every diamond node is a *decision gate*; the leaf rectangles at the far right represent terminal outcomes. The cascade structure visually enforces the principle that *lexics precedes syntax, which precedes semantics*.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. Differentiate between syntax and semantics with a suitable programming example.**  `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **Syntax** refers to the *structural rules* governing how tokens are combined to form valid program statements.  *(1 mark)*
- **Semantics** refers to the *meaning* or *behavior* that syntactically valid programs exhibit during execution.  *(1 mark)*
- **Example:** `int x = 5 / 0;` is **syntactically correct** (it follows the grammar `type id = expr ;`), but **semantically undefined** (division by zero causes a runtime error).  *(1 mark)*

---

**Q2. What is a token? List any four categories of tokens recognized by a lexical analyzer.**  `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
A **token** is the smallest indivisible lexical unit produced by the lexer, represented as a tuple $\langle \text{Tag}, \text{Lexeme}, \text{Attribute} \rangle$.  *(1 mark)*

Four categories:  *(2 marks — 0.5 each)*
1. **Keywords** (e.g., `if`, `while`)
2. **Identifiers** (e.g., `count`, `x1`)
3. **Operators** (e.g., `+`, `==`)
4. **Literals/Constants** (e.g., `42`, `"hello"`)

---

### 5.2 Part B — Module Internal Choice (14 Marks Each)

---

#### ⭐ Question A (14 Marks)  `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024]`

**(a)** Explain the various categories of tokens used in lexical analysis with examples.  *(7 marks)*

**(b)** Discuss the different approaches used to specify the semantics of programming languages.  *(7 marks)*

---

#### ✅ Model Solution — Question A (a): Token Categories (7 marks)

The **lexical analyzer (lexer)** classifies source characters into the following token categories:

1. **Keywords / Reserved Words** *(1.5 marks)* — Predefined identifiers with fixed syntactic and semantic meaning. Examples: `if`, `else`, `while`, `return`, `int`, `float`. They cannot be redefined by the programmer.

2. **Identifiers** *(1.5 marks)* — User-defined names for variables, functions, classes, etc. Defined by the regular expression:
$$\text{identifier} = (\text{letter} \ \vert \ \_ ) \ (\text{letter} \ \vert \ \text{digit} \ \vert \ \_)^{*}$$
Example: `studentName`, `_count`, `sum1`.

3. **Operators** *(1.5 marks)* — Symbols denoting computations or comparisons.
   - *Arithmetic:* `+`, `-`, `*`, `/`, `%`
   - *Relational:* `<`, `>`, `<=`, `>=`, `==`, `!=`
   - *Logical:* `&&`, `\vert\vert`, `!`
   - *Assignment:* `=`, `+=`, `-=`

4. **Literals / Constants** *(1 mark)* — Fixed values appearing in source code: integer (`42`), floating-point (`3.14`), character (`'A'`), string (`"hello"`), boolean (`true`, `false`).

5. **Punctuation / Delimiters** *(0.5 mark)* — Symbols used to demarcate structure: `( ) { } [ ] ; , . :`.

6. **Comments and Whitespace** *(1 mark — context)* — Stripped by the lexer; not delivered as tokens but used to separate tokens.

---

#### ✅ Model Solution — Question A (b): Approaches to Semantics (7 marks)

The principal formal approaches to specify **dynamic semantics** are:

1. **Operational Semantics** *(2.5 marks)* — Defines program meaning by specifying the **sequence of state transitions** on an abstract machine. The program is interpreted step-by-step. *Example:* The C language can be specified operationally via a stack-based abstract machine. Each statement transforms the program state $(memory, environment)$.

2. **Denotational Semantics** *(2.5 marks)* — Each program fragment is mapped to a **mathematical function** (its *denotation*) from inputs to outputs. Compositionality is central — the meaning of a whole is built from the meaning of its parts using functions over domains and codomains. *Example:* A `while` loop is mapped to a least fixed-point of a function. Pioneered by **Christopher Strachey** and **Dana Scott**.

3. **Axiomatic Semantics** *(2 marks)* — Specifies semantics using **logical assertions** (preconditions and postconditions) and rules of inference (Hoare triples of the form $\{P\} \ S \ \{Q\}$). Used extensively in **formal verification** of programs. *Example:* $\{x > 0\} \ \ x := x + 1 \ \ \{x > 1\}$.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Question A (b)**
> Students often *omit* the distinction between **static semantics** (compile-time checks: type, scope) and **dynamic semantics** (runtime behavior). Always mention BOTH. Failing to cite **Hoare triples** in axiomatic semantics costs a full mark band.

---

#### ⭐ Question B (14 Marks)  `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`

**(a)** With a suitable example, explain how **BNF (Backus–Naur Form)** is used to describe the syntax of a programming language.  *(7 marks)*

**(b)** Compare and contrast **operational, denotational, and axiomatic semantics** with neat diagrams or tables.  *(7 marks)*

---

#### ✅ Model Solution — Question B (a): BNF Notation (7 marks)

**BNF (Backus–Naur Form)** is a formal metalanguage introduced by **John Backus** and **Peter Naur** (for **ALGOL 60**). It is a notation for **context-free grammars** comprising:
- **Non-terminals** — written in angle brackets, e.g., $\langle \text{expr} \rangle$.
- **Terminals** — actual tokens, written in quotes or as themselves.
- **Productions** — of the form $\langle \text{NT} \rangle ::= \text{body}$ where $::$ means *is defined as*.
- **Alternation** — `$\vert$` separates alternatives.

**Example BNF for a simple arithmetic expression language:**  *(5 marks — 2 for BNF, 1 for sample derivation, 1 for parse tree sketch, 1 for significance)*

$$
\begin{aligned}
\langle \text{expr} \rangle &::= \langle \text{expr} \rangle \ \text{"+"} \ \langle \text{term} \rangle \ \vert \ \langle \text{expr} \rangle \ \text{"-"} \ \langle \text{term} \rangle \ \vert \ \langle \text{term} \rangle \\[2pt]
\langle \text{term} \rangle &::= \langle \text{term} \rangle \ \text{"*"} \ \langle \text{factor} \rangle \ \vert \ \langle \text{factor} \rangle \\[2pt]
\langle \text{factor} \rangle &::= \ \text{"("} \ \langle \text{expr} \rangle \ \text{")"} \ \vert \ \langle \text{id} \rangle \ \vert \ \langle \text{num} \rangle
\end{aligned}
$$

**Derivation of `x + 5 * y`:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\langle \text{expr} \rangle &\Rightarrow \langle \text{expr} \rangle \ \text{"+"} \ \langle \text{term} \rangle \\[2pt]
&\Rightarrow \langle \text{term} \rangle \ \text{"+"} \ \langle \text{term} \rangle \ \text{"*"} \ \langle \text{factor} \rangle \\[2pt]
&\Rightarrow \langle \text{id} \rangle \ \text{"+"} \ \langle \text{factor} \rangle \ \text{"*"} \ \langle \text{id} \rangle \\[2pt]
&\Rightarrow \ \text{"x + 5 * y"}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Significance:**  *(1 mark)* BNF gives a precise, unambiguous specification that compiler generators (Yacc, Bison, ANTLR) consume to produce parsers automatically. It is the cornerstone of formal language theory taught in KTU Module 1.

---

#### ✅ Model Solution — Question B (b): Comparative Study of Semantics (7 marks)

| Feature | Operational | Denotational | Axiomatic |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Origin** | Machine-based simulation | Mathematical functions | Logic / Hoare Logic |
| **Meaning Defined As** | Sequence of state transitions | Function over abstract domains | Pre/Post-condition triples |
| **Mathematical Basis** | State machines | Lambda calculus, fixed-point theory | Predicate logic, inference rules |
| **Tooling** | Abstract interpreters | Domain-theoretic models | Verification condition generators (Frama-C, Dafny) |
| **Strengths** | Intuitive, easy to follow | Highly rigorous, compositional | Excellent for proving correctness |
| **Weaknesses** | Less rigorous, machine-dependent | Mathematically heavy | Hard to use for all language constructs |
| **Best Used For** | Specifying low-level constructs | Defining full language semantics | Verifying critical code (aerospace, medical) |
| **Example** | Java Virtual Machine (JVM) spec | Scott-Strachey model of LISP | Loop invariants, partial correctness |

**Diagrammatic Analogy:** *(1 mark)*

```mermaid
graph LR
    OPS[OPERATIONAL: Program is a Recipe for a Robot Chef] --> RES1[Result: Plate of Food]
    DEN[DENOTATIONAL: Program is a Pure Mathematical Function] --> RES2[Result: Output Value in Math Domain]
    AXI[AXIOMATIC: Program is a Logical Theorem to Prove] --> RES3[Result: Correctness Proof Certificate]
```

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Question B (b)**
> - Do NOT write a *bullet list* without a comparison axis — you will lose **3 marks**.
> - Always include **at least one real-world example** (JVM, LISP, Frama-C).
> - Mention **Christopher Strachey & Dana Scott** for denotational; **Tony Hoare** for axiomatic — naming pioneers scores an extra evaluator's mark.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Lexics vs. Syntax vs. Semantics**
> - **Lexics** = tokens (regex/DFA). Concerned with character → lexeme grouping.
> - **Syntax** = structure (BNF/CFG/parse tree). Concerned with token arrangement.
> - **Semantics** = meaning (operational/denotational/axiomatic). Concerned with what the program *does*.
> - Tokens come in **5 main categories**: keywords, identifiers, operators, literals, punctuation.
> - **BNF** uses `::=`, alternation $\vert$, and $\langle \ \rangle$ for non-terminals.
> - **Static semantics** (type, scope) is checked before execution; **dynamic semantics** describes runtime behavior.
> - Compiler pipeline order: **Lexical → Syntax → Semantic → Intermediate Code Generation → Optimization → Code Generation**.
> - **Hoare triple** $\{P\} \ S \ \{Q\}$ is the axiomatic-semantics cornerstone.
> - **Denotational semantics** relies on **least fixed points** for loops/recursion.
> - The three layers form a *cascade* — failure at any layer halts compilation.
> - **Common pitfall:** confusing a *syntax error* (compile-time, structural) with a *semantic error* (often runtime, meaning-related).
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Case Study: Building a Syntax Analyzer for TinyAda
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECST758 - PROGRAMMING LANGUAGES
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Case Study: Building a Syntax Analyzer for TinyAda

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syntax Analyzer (Parser):** A syntax analyzer, or **parser**, is the second phase of a compiler that takes the linear stream of tokens produced by the lexical analyzer and verifies their arrangement against the **grammar rules** of the source programming language. It constructs a hierarchical structure — typically a **Parse Tree** or **Abstract Syntax Tree (AST)** — which represents the grammatical structure of the source program.

**TinyAda** is a pedagogical, drastically reduced subset of the **Ada programming language**, commonly used in compiler construction textbooks (notably Watt & Brown's *Programming Language Processors in Java*). TinyAda retains Ada's characteristic block structure, declarative sections, and statement forms but strips away generics, tasks, packages, and most type machinery. Studying its syntax analyzer offers a microcosm of real-world compiler construction.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Grammar Police at the Sentence Gate

Imagine you are a strict English grammar teacher standing at a gate. Students bring you sentences word-by-word (tokens from the lexical analyzer). Your job is **not** to check spelling (that was the scanner's job) — your job is to check whether the words, when arranged in this order, form a *grammatically valid sentence*.

For example:

- ✅ "The **cat** *sat* on the **mat**." → Valid (Subject → Verb → Preposition → Object)
- ❌ "Cat the on mat sat." → Invalid (nonsense arrangement)

A **syntax analyzer for TinyAda** is exactly this gatekeeper, but for Ada-like programs. It accepts token streams like:

```
procedure  Hello  is
begin
  Put ( "Hi" ) ;
end  Hello ;
```

…and rejects malformed ones. Crucially, it **also** builds a tree-shaped map of how the tokens fit together — this map (the **AST**) is what later compiler phases (semantic analysis, code generation) actually consume.

### 1.3 Why TinyAda Specifically?

| Feature | Real Ada | TinyAda |
|---|---|---|
| Block structure (`begin … end`) | ✓ | ✓ |
| Declarative parts | ✓ | ✓ (simplified) |
| Strong typing | ✓ | ✗ (mostly implicit) |
| Packages / generics | ✓ | ✗ |
| Tasks / protected types | ✓ | ✗ |
| Exceptions | ✓ | ✗ |

This minimalism makes TinyAda ideal for a **case study**: the entire grammar fits on one or two pages, allowing students to implement a full recursive-descent parser in well under 500 lines of code, while still demonstrating production-quality techniques like error recovery and AST construction.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (PECST758 Module 1):** The case study of TinyAda is the canonical vehicle for illustrating *language specification → grammar → parser implementation*. Expect direct questions on grammar design, parse tree construction, and predictive parsing tables.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Three Logical Layers of a TinyAda Syntax Analyzer

A TinyAda syntax analyzer is conventionally decomposed into **three cooperating layers**:

**Layer 1 — The Grammar (Specification Layer)**
The grammar is written in **EBNF (Extended Backus–Naur Form)**. It declares every legal syntactic construct as a *production rule*. Example:

```
program     ::= "procedure" identifier "is" 
                declarative_part
                "begin"  statement_part  "end" identifier ";" .
declarative_part ::= { declaration } .
statement_part    ::= { statement } .
```

**Layer 2 — The Lexical Interface (Token Stream Layer)**
The parser pulls tokens one at a time from a `getToken()` function supplied by the lexical analyzer. Tokens carry both a **class** (e.g., `IDENT`, `INT_LIT`, `IF`, `SEMI`) and, for identifiers/literals, a **spelling** (the actual lexeme).

**Layer 3 — The Recognizer + Tree Builder (Parsing Layer)**
Each EBNF non-terminal becomes a **parsing function**. Each function:
1. Inspects the current token.
2. Either matches (and consumes) it, or recursively calls the function for a sub-non-terminal, **or** reports a syntax error.
3. Allocates AST nodes and links them.

### 2.2 Predictive Parsing Theory — The Math Behind the Recognizer

For TinyAda, a **recursive-descent parser** with one-token lookahead (LL(1)) is the standard implementation technique. The decision *which alternative to pick* is driven by two sets computed from the grammar:

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Definition — FIRST(α):** For any grammar symbol string α, FIRST(α) is the set of terminal symbols that can begin a string derivable from α. If α can derive ε (empty), then ε ∈ FIRST(α).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Definition — FOLLOW(A):** For any non-terminal A, FOLLOW(A) is the set of terminal symbols that can immediately follow A in some sentential form. A special end-of-file marker **\$** is added to FOLLOW(start_symbol).

### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Concept / Rule | Formula / Definition | Usage |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **FIRST set computation** | If X is terminal → FIRST(X) = {X}; If X → ε is a production → add ε to FIRST(X) | Decides which tokens may begin a non-terminal |
| 2 | **FOLLOW set computation** | Add \$ to FOLLOW(S); If A → αBβ → add FIRST(β) − {ε} to FOLLOW(B); If A → αB or A → αBβ with ε ∈ FIRST(β) → add FOLLOW(A) to FOLLOW(B) | Decides which tokens may legally follow a non-terminal |
| 3 | **LL(1) Grammar Condition** | For every non-terminal A with alternatives A → α₁ ∣ α₂ ∣ … ∣ αₙ, the sets FIRST(αᵢ) must be pairwise disjoint | Guarantees deterministic predictive parsing |
| 4 | **EBNF Repetition** | `{ X }` ≡ zero-or-more occurrences | Models optional lists like `{declaration}` |
| 5 | **Parse Tree Depth** | Depth of tree = number of derivation steps from root to leaf | Used to bound parser recursion |
| 6 | **Empty Derivation** | ε-production permits a non-terminal to derive nothing | Encodes optional constructs |
| 7 | **End-of-Input Marker** | \$ is appended to FOLLOW(start_symbol) | Sentinel for end of program |

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

Predictive parsing and AST-based compiler architectures (the exact pattern used in TinyAda) underpin:
- **GCC** and **Clang/LLVM** — use LL/LR techniques for C/C++.
- **VHDL/Ada front-ends** (e.g., GNAT) — descend from Ada's grammar lineage.
- **IDE language servers** (LSP) — use incremental parsers to power real-time error squiggles.

> [!NOTE]
> **Real engineering analogy:** A TinyAda parser is to a real Ada compiler (GNAT) what a 1:64 scale model car is to a production sedan — same engineering principles, smaller scope.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Computing FIRST and FOLLOW Sets for a TinyAda Sub-Grammar

Consider this fragment of the TinyAda grammar (statement_part):

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{stmt}   &\rightarrow \text{if\_stmt} \;\vert\; \text{assign\_stmt} \;\vert\; \text{null\_stmt} \\
\text{if\_stmt}   &\rightarrow \text{"if"} \; \text{cond} \; \text{"then"} \; \text{stmt\_list} \; \text{"end" "if" ";"} \\
\text{assign\_stmt} &\rightarrow \text{identifier} \; \text{":="} \; \text{expr} \; \text{";"} \\
\text{null\_stmt}    &\rightarrow \text{"null" ";"} \\
\text{cond}   &\rightarrow \text{identifier} \\
\text{expr}   &\rightarrow \text{identifier} \;\vert\; \text{int\_literal} \\
\text{stmt\_list}   &\rightarrow \text{stmt} \; \{\text{stmt}\}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 1 — Compute FIRST sets for terminals and literals**

Terminals have trivial FIRST sets: FIRST(`"if"`) = {`if`}, FIRST(`identifier`) = {`IDENT`}, etc.

**Step 2 — Compute FIRST(non-terminals) using production rules**

For `if_stmt`, the first symbol is the terminal `"if"`, so FIRST(`if_stmt`) = {`if`}.

For `assign_stmt`, the first symbol is `identifier`, so FIRST(`assign_stmt`) = {`IDENT`}.

For `null_stmt`, the first symbol is `"null"`, so FIRST(`null_stmt`) = {`null`}.

Therefore, for `stmt` (with three alternatives):

$$
\text{FIRST}(\text{stmt}) = \{\, \text{"if"},\;\text{IDENT},\;\text{"null"} \,\}
$$

These three sets are **pairwise disjoint** ⟹ the grammar satisfies the **LL(1) condition** for this non-terminal.

**Step 3 — Compute FOLLOW sets**

We begin by adding the end-of-input marker:

$$
\text{FOLLOW}(\text{program}) \ni \$
$$

Since `stmt` appears inside `stmt_list`, and `stmt_list` is followed by `"end"`, we add `"end"` to FOLLOW(`stmt`). Also, within `stmt_list` the recursive `{stmt}` means a `stmt` can be followed by another `stmt`'s first token. Therefore:

$$
\text{FOLLOW}(\text{stmt}) = \{\, \text{IDENT},\;\text{"if"},\;\text{"null"},\;\text{"end"} \,\}
$$

**Step 4 — Verify LL(1) Table Entries**

For each alternative of `stmt`, we consult FIRST of its right-hand side to populate the predictive parsing table. Because the three FIRST sets are disjoint, no conflict arises. The parsing table is **deterministic**.

### 3.2 Analytical Matrix: EBNF Construct → Parser Action

This matrix is **high-yield** for KTU exam short-answer questions linking grammar to implementation.

| EBNF Construct | Meaning | Parser Action (Pseudo-code) |
|---|---|---|
| `X` (terminal) | A literal token | `accept(X)` — check current token, consume if match, else error |
| `X` (non-terminal) | A sub-construct | Call `parse_X()` |
| `[ X ]` | Optional (0 or 1) | `if (current ∈ FIRST(X)) parse_X();` |
| `{ X }` | Repetition (0 or more) | `while (current ∈ FIRST(X)) parse_X();` |
| `( X )` | Grouping | Just for precedence / clarity — no runtime effect |
| `A ∣ B` | Alternative | `if (current ∈ FIRST(A)) parse_A(); else parse_B();` |
| `;` | EBNF terminator (not Ada) | Marks end of production — runtime no-op |
| `.` | End of grammar rule | Marks end of production — runtime no-op |

### 3.3 Worked Example: Parsing a TinyAda `if` Statement

Source input (after lexing):

$$
[\,\text{"if"},\;\text{IDENT}(x),\;\text{"then"},\;\text{IDENT}(y),\;\text{":="},\;\text{INT\_LIT}(5),\;\text{";"},\;\text{"end"},\;\text{"if"},\;\text{";"}\,]
$$

**Recursive descent trace:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
&\textbf{parseStmt():} && \text{current token = "if" → matches FIRST(if\_stmt)} \\
& && \textbf{parseIfStmt():} \\
& && \quad \text{accept("if")  → consumes "if"} \\
& && \quad \textbf{parseCond():} \; \text{expect IDENT → consumes IDENT}(x) \\
& && \quad \text{accept("then") → consumes "then"} \\
& && \quad \textbf{parseStmtList():} \\
& && \quad\quad \text{current = IDENT → matches FIRST(assign\_stmt)} \\
& && \quad\quad \textbf{parseAssignStmt():} \\
& && \quad\quad\quad \text{accept(IDENT)} \rightarrow y \\
& && \quad\quad\quad \text{accept(":=")} \\
& && \quad\quad\quad \textbf{parseExpr():} \text{ current = INT\_LIT → accept INT\_LIT(5)} \\
& && \quad\quad\quad \text{accept(";")} \\
& && \quad\quad \text{current = "end" → not in FIRST(any stmt) → exit while} \\
& && \quad \text{accept("end")} \;\text{accept("if")}\;\text{accept(";")} \\
& && \textbf{RETURN AST:}\; \text{IfNode(cond="x", body=AssignNode(y, 5))}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Resulting AST (textual representation):**

```
        IF
       / | \
     cond then  end
      |    |
      x    ASSIGN
           / | \
          y  :=  5
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this matters in KTU exams:** Tracing a recursive-descent parse by hand, given an input token stream, is one of the most frequently asked 14-mark questions. Practice drawing both the AST and the call-return trace.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Architecture of the TinyAda Syntax Analyzer (Mermaid Block-Level Functional Flow)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    srcA[Source Program Text] --> lexA[Lexical Analyzer]
    lexA --> tokA[Token Stream]

    subgraph parserCore["Syntax Analyzer Core"]
        dirA[Parser Driver parseProgram]
        dirB[parseDeclarativePart]
        dirC[parseStatementPart]
        dirD[parseStmt]
        dirE[parseIfStmt]
        dirF[parseAssignStmt]
        dirG[parseExpr]
    end

    tokA --> dirA
    dirA --> dirB
    dirA --> dirC
    dirC --> dirD
    dirD --> dirE
    dirD --> dirF
    dirD --> dirG

    dirA --> astA[Abstract Syntax Tree AST]
    dirB --> astA
    dirC --> astA
    dirD --> astA
    dirE --> astA
    dirF --> astA
    dirG --> astA

    astA --> symA[Symbol Table]
    astA --> errA[Error Reporter]
    errA --> outA[Diagnostics to User]

    getTokA[getToken from Lexer] -. supplied to .-> dirA
    getTokA -. supplied to .-> dirB
    getTokA -. supplied to .-> dirC
    getTokA -. supplied to .-> dirD
```

**Reading the diagram:**
- Solid arrows denote **data flow** (tokens / AST nodes).
- Dotted arrows denote **service invocation** (`getToken()` callbacks into the lexer).
- The five `parse*` functions inside the `parserCore` subgraph are mutually recursive — the parser stack grows and shrinks with nesting depth.

### 4.2 Grammar-to-Parser Mapping Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    ebnfA[EBNF Production Rule] --> funcA[Java Function parseX]
    firstA[FIRST set of production] --> tblA[Predictive Parsing Table cell M X t]
    followA[FOLLOW set of non terminal] --> tblA
    tblA --> dispA[Dispatcher if else chain in parseX]

    ebnfA --> nodeA[AST Node class NodeX]
    funcA --> nodeA
    nodeA --> astOutA[Final AST]

    tblA --> conflictCheckA{LL 1 conflict?}
    conflictCheckA -- No --> okA[Grammar accepted]
    conflictCheckA -- Yes --> rejectA[Left factor or eliminate left recursion]
```

### 4.3 TinyAda Parse Tree Example

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    rootN["program"]
    procN["procedure Proc is"]
    declN["declarative_part (empty)"]
    beginN["begin"]
    stmtN["statement_part"]

    rootN --> procN
    rootN --> declN
    rootN --> beginN
    rootN --> stmtN

    stmtN --> s1N["stmt"]
    s1N --> ifN["if_stmt"]
    ifN --> ifK["if"]
    ifN --> condN["cond"]
    ifN --> thenK["then"]
    ifN --> sListN["stmt_list"]
    ifN --> endK["end if ;"]

    condN --> idX["IDENT x"]
    sListN --> asgN["assign_stmt"]
    asgN --> idY["IDENT y"]
    asgN --> assOp[":="]
    asgN --> exprN["expr"]
    exprN --> lit5["INT_LIT 5"]
    asgN --> semi1[";"]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram note:** In strict Mermaid syntax, node labels avoid reserved words. Here `end` is embedded inside quoted text labels only (e.g., `"end if ;"`), not as a standalone node ID, satisfying the node-identifier alpha rule.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024]**
*Define a syntax analyzer. What is the role of FIRST and FOLLOW sets in predictive parsing?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A **syntax analyzer** (or parser) is the compiler phase that takes the token stream from the lexical analyzer and verifies whether the tokens can be generated by the source language's grammar. It produces a **parse tree** or **AST** as output.

**FIRST sets** identify which terminal tokens may legally appear at the *beginning* of a string derived from a given grammar symbol. **FOLLOW sets** identify which terminal tokens may legally appear *immediately after* a non-terminal in some valid sentence. Together they populate the **predictive parsing table** that drives a recursive-descent parser to make deterministic, one-token-lookahead decisions. *(3 marks)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024]**
*Why is TinyAda chosen as a case study for illustrating compiler front-ends? List any two reasons.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

1. **Minimal yet representative grammar** — TinyAda retains Ada's block structure (`procedure … is … begin … end …`) and statement forms but omits generics, packages, and tasks, allowing the entire grammar to fit on one page and the parser to be coded in under 500 lines.
2. **Realistic engineering patterns** — despite its size, TinyAda exercises production-grade techniques such as recursive-descent parsing, AST construction, and error recovery, making the case study directly transferable to real Ada compilers like GNAT.
3. *(Optional third point)* **Pedagogical clarity** — the absence of syntactic sugar lets students focus on core concepts like FIRST/FOLLOW and LL(1) tables. *(3 marks)*

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

---

**Question A (14 Marks)**
**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Style]**

Consider the following TinyAda grammar fragment for statements:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{stmt} &\rightarrow \text{"if"} \; \text{cond} \; \text{"then"} \; \text{stmt\_list} \; \text{"end" "if" ";"} \\
&\;\;\vert\; \text{identifier} \; \text{":="} \; \text{expr} \; \text{";"} \\
&\;\;\vert\; \text{"null" ";"} \\
\text{cond} &\rightarrow \text{identifier} \\
\text{expr} &\rightarrow \text{int\_literal} \;\vert\; \text{identifier}
\end{aligned}
$$

**(a)** Compute the **FIRST** and **FOLLOW** sets for `stmt` and show that the grammar satisfies the LL(1) condition. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Trace the recursive-descent parse for the input `if x then y := 5 ; end if ;` showing the AST produced. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution:**

**(a) FIRST and FOLLOW computation (7 marks)**

**Step 1 — FIRST sets of right-hand-side alternatives:**

- FIRST(`"if" cond …`) = {`"if"`}
- FIRST(`identifier ":=" expr ";"`) = {`IDENT`}
- FIRST(`"null" ";"`) = {`"null"`}

Therefore:

$$
\text{FIRST}(\text{stmt}) = \{\, \text{"if"},\;\text{IDENT},\;\text{"null"} \,\}
$$

The three sets are pairwise disjoint ⟹ **LL(1) condition satisfied** for this non-terminal. **(4 marks)**

**Step 2 — FOLLOW(stmt):**
Since `stmt` appears inside `stmt_list` which is followed by `"end"`, and `stmt_list` is a repetition of `stmt`, a `stmt` may be followed by another `stmt`'s start token:

$$
\text{FOLLOW}(\text{stmt}) = \{\, \text{IDENT},\;\text{"if"},\;\text{"null"},\;\text{"end"} \,\}
$$

Note: FIRST(stmt) ∩ FOLLOW(stmt) = ∅ ⟹ no conflict at the closure of alternatives. **(3 marks)**

---

**(b) Parse trace and AST (7 marks)**

Token stream: [`if`, IDENT(x), `then`, IDENT(y), `:=`, INT_LIT(5), `;`, `end`, `if`, `;`]

Trace:
1. `parseStmt()` — sees `"if"` → dispatches to `parseIfStmt()`. *(1 mark)*
2. `parseIfStmt()` — `accept("if")`; `parseCond()` matches IDENT(x); `accept("then")`. *(1 mark)*
3. `parseStmtList()` — current token IDENT ∈ FIRST(assign) → `parseAssignStmt()`. *(1 mark)*
4. `parseAssignStmt()` — accepts IDENT(y), `:=`, `parseExpr()` consumes INT_LIT(5), accepts `;`. *(1 mark)*
5. Loop test: current = `"end"` ∉ FIRST(stmt) → exit loop. *(1 mark)*
6. `parseIfStmt()` finishes: accept `"end"`, `"if"`, `";"`. *(1 mark)*

**Resulting AST (1 mark):**

```
       IfStmt
      /  |   \
   cond then  end
    |    |
    x   Assign
         /|\
        y := 5
```

---

**Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**
**[KTU University Exam — July 2024 Style]**

**(a)** Explain the architecture of a TinyAda syntax analyzer. Draw a block diagram showing the interaction between the lexical analyzer, parser driver, parsing functions, and the AST. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Differentiate between a **parse tree** and an **Abstract Syntax Tree (AST)**. Using the statement `a := b + 3 ;` show how each is represented. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Architecture (7 marks):**

The TinyAda syntax analyzer is organized in three cooperating layers. **(1 mark)**
- **Lexical interface layer** — supplies tokens via `getToken()`. **(1 mark)**
- **Parser driver** — `parseProgram()` is the entry point that invokes `parseDeclarativePart()` and `parseStatementPart()`. **(1 mark)**
- **Per-non-terminal parsing functions** — one function per EBNF non-terminal (e.g., `parseIfStmt`, `parseAssignStmt`), mutually recursive, each using **predictive parsing** based on FIRST/FOLLOW. **(2 marks)**
- **AST builder** — each parsing function constructs and returns an AST node, which is linked to its parent. **(1 mark)**
- **Error reporter** — invokes `reportError()` with line/column information when `accept()` fails. **(1 mark)**

(Refer to the Mermaid architecture diagram in Section 4.1 for the visual.)

---

**(b) Parse Tree vs AST (7 marks):**

| Aspect | Parse Tree (Concrete) | Abstract Syntax Tree |
|---|---|---|
| Granularity | Every grammar symbol is a node | Only semantically meaningful constructs remain |
| Punctuation | Includes `;`, `:=`, `end`, `if`, etc. as nodes | These are stripped |
| Size | Large | Compact |
| Use | Grammar verification | Semantic analysis, code generation |
| One-to-one with grammar? | Yes | No |

**(2 marks for differentiation)**

For the statement `a := b + 3 ;` (assuming `+` is allowed in expr for illustration):

**Parse Tree (2 marks):**
```
        stmt
         |
     assign_stmt
    /  |   \   \   \
  ID  :=  expr  ;
  a       / | \
        term  +
        / \  / \
        b  *  3   ε
```

**AST (2 marks):**
```
     Assign
    /  |   \
   a  :=  Add
            / \
           b   3
```

**Conclusion (1 mark):** The AST is the preferred intermediate representation because it discards syntactic noise, making downstream phases (semantic analysis, optimization, code generation) simpler and more efficient.

---

### 5.3 ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common places where students lose marks on TinyAda / syntax-analyzer questions:**
> 1. **Confusing parse tree with AST** — the parse tree has *every* grammar symbol as a node; the AST drops punctuation. Examiners allocate separate marks for the distinction.
> 2. **Forgetting the end-of-input marker \$** when computing FOLLOW of the start symbol. This single omission can invalidate an entire FIRST/FOLLOW derivation.
> 3. **Not verifying LL(1) disjointness explicitly** — simply listing FIRST sets is not enough; you must state that the alternatives' FIRST sets are *pairwise disjoint* and that FIRST/FOLLOW do not clash.
> 4. **Skipping the dispatcher logic** in the parser — KTU expects you to show the `if (current ∈ FIRST(α₁)) … else if …` decision chain, not just the function call.
> 5. **Omitting error recovery** in long answers — even a one-line mention of `synchronize()` based on FOLLOW sets earns credit.
> 6. **Wrong indentation of AST drawings** in the exam sheet — examiners cannot award full marks for an illegible tree. Use a ruler and clear parent-child alignment.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- ✅ **Syntax analyzer** = parser = verifies token arrangement against grammar + builds parse tree/AST.
- ✅ **TinyAda** = a minimal Ada subset used as a pedagogical case study; retains block structure and declarative sections.
- ✅ **EBNF extensions** over BNF: `[ ]` (optional), `{ }` (repetition), `( )` (grouping).
- ✅ **FIRST(X)** = terminals that may *begin* a string from X.
- ✅ **FOLLOW(A)** = terminals that may *follow* A; always include \$ for the start symbol.
- ✅ **LL(1) condition** = FIRST sets of alternatives are pairwise disjoint *and* disjoint from FOLLOW when ε-productions exist.
- ✅ **Recursive descent parser** = one parsing function per non-terminal, driven by predictive parsing table.
- ✅ **Parse tree** = concrete, every symbol is a node. **AST** = abstract, only meaningful constructs remain.
- ✅ **Architecture**: Lexer → Token stream → Parser driver → per-non-terminal functions → AST → (later) Semantic analysis.
- ✅ **Error recovery** uses FOLLOW-based synchronization tokens.
- ✅ Real-world engines built on these principles: **GCC, Clang/LLVM, GNAT, ANTLR-generated parsers**.
- ✅ For KTU 14-mark questions, always pair a **grammar definition** with a **hand-traced parse** and a **drawn AST**.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Initial Static Semantic Analysis of TinyAda
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECST758 - PROGRAMMING LANGUAGES
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Initial Static Semantic Analysis of TinyAda

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition: Static Semantic Analysis

**Static Semantic Analysis** is the phase of compilation that validates the *context-sensitive* and *non-syntactic* properties of a source program **without executing it**. Unlike syntax analysis (which is governed by a context-free grammar), semantic analysis enforces rules that cannot be expressed by a pure CFG — for example: *"every identifier must be declared before use,"* *"the type of an expression must be compatible with its usage context,"* and *"subprogram calls must match the declared parameter profile."*

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Definition (Board-Exam Standard):**
> Static semantic analysis is the intermediate translation phase, situated between syntax analysis and intermediate code generation, that checks declarations, types, scopes, and other context-dependent constraints by consulting a **Symbol Table** and producing annotated Abstract Syntax Trees (ASTs) or decorated parse trees.

In the context of **TinyAda** — a deliberately small subset of the Ada programming language designed for teaching compiler construction (popularized by Kenneth C. Louden) — the *initial* static semantic pass focuses on:

1. **Identification & Declaration Tracking**
2. **Scope Resolution & Visibility**
3. **Type Equivalence & Compatibility**
4. **Parameter Mode Validation**
5. **Overload Resolution**

---

## 1.2 What is TinyAda?

TinyAda is a **mini-language** distilled from Ada to expose the essential declarative and block-structured features that make static semantics non-trivial:

- Nested **begin … end** blocks with their own declarative parts.
- Strong **static typing** with user-defined types.
- **Subprogram declarations** (procedures and functions) with `in`, `out`, `in out` parameter modes.
- **Overloading** of subprogram names.
- **Packages** with visible/private parts (in extended versions).

A representative TinyAda skeleton:

```ada
procedure Main is
   X : Integer;
   Y : Float;
   
   function Square(N : in Integer) return Integer is
   begin
      return N * N;
   end Square;
   
begin
   X := 5;
   Y := 2.0;
   X := Square(X);          -- Valid call
   Y := Square(X);          -- Type mismatch
end Main;
```

---

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The Blueprint Inspector

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Intuition:** Think of the compiler as a **municipal building inspector** reviewing architectural blueprints.
> 
> - **Syntax analysis** checks that the *grammar* of the blueprint is valid (walls are drawn as lines, doors as rectangles).
> - **Static semantic analysis** checks that every **room labelled "Bedroom-2"** has been **defined elsewhere in the blueprint**, that the **electrical load** of the kitchen matches the **circuit rating**, and that a **door labelled "Exit"** actually leads somewhere defined.
> 
> The **Symbol Table** is the inspector's *clipped notebook* — a structured ledger where every declared entity is recorded along with its attributes (type, scope, mode). The inspector flips back through the notebook to answer *"What did the programmer mean by X?"* before approving the design.

---

## 1.4 Static vs. Dynamic Semantics — A Sharp Distinction

| Aspect | Static Semantics | Dynamic Semantics |
|---|---|---|
| **When checked** | At **compile time** | At **run time** |
| **Requires execution?** | **No** | Yes |
| **Examples in TinyAda** | Undeclared identifiers, type mismatch, scope violations, wrong parameter mode | Division by zero, array index out of range, null pointer dereference |
| **Tool used** | Symbol Table + Attribute Grammar / Visitor pattern | Run-time system, virtual machine, OS traps |

---

## 1.5 Position in the Compilation Pipeline

> [!NOTE]
> TinyAda's initial static semantic analyzer sits **after** lexical and syntax analysis but **before** intermediate code generation. Its output is typically a **decorated AST** in which every identifier reference is linked (via pointers or line-number tags) to its declaration in the symbol table.

```
Source Program  ──►  Lexical Analyzer  ──►  Syntax Analyzer
                                              │
                                              ▼
                                   Static Semantic Analyzer
                                       (THIS TOPIC)
                                              │
                                              ▼
                                   Decorated AST + Updated
                                        Symbol Table
                                              │
                                              ▼
                                  Intermediate Code Generator
```
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Symbol Table: The Heart of Semantic Analysis

The **Symbol Table** (often abbreviated *ST*) is a **scope-aware dictionary** mapping identifiers to their semantic attributes. In TinyAda, because of nested blocks, the symbol table is structured as a **stack of scopes** (also called a *chained symbol table* or *hierarchical symbol table*).

### 2.1.1 Scope Levels in TinyAda

A TinyAda program contains (from outermost to innermost):

1. **Library / Predefined Scope** — built-in types (`Integer`, `Float`, `Boolean`, `Character`) and standard subprograms (`Put_Line`, `Get`).
2. **Package / Subprogram Scope** — top-level procedures and functions.
3. **Block Scope** — each `declare … begin … end` introduces a new local scope.
4. **Record Scope** — fields of a record type (in extended TinyAda).

### 2.1.2 Core Operations on the Symbol Table

Let the symbol table be denoted $ST$, and let the current scope be $S_{current}$.

> **Operation 1 — `lookup(id, scope)`:**
> Search the scope chain starting at $S_{current}$, moving outward through enclosing scopes until the predefined scope. Return the entry if found, or signal an *"undeclared identifier"* error.

> **Operation 2 — `insert(id, attrs, scope)`:**
> Add the identifier with its attribute record to the *innermost* currently open scope. This corresponds to processing a declaration.

> **Operation 3 — `enter_scope(name)`:**
> Push a new empty scope onto the scope stack. The new scope becomes $S_{current}$.

> **Operation 4 — `leave_scope()`:**
> Pop the current scope. The enclosing scope becomes $S_{current}$. All identifiers declared in the popped scope become inaccessible.

> **Operation 5 — `find_in_current_scope(id)`:**
> Look up an identifier *only* in $S_{current}$. Used to detect duplicate declarations within the same scope.

These can be expressed formally using scope-chain notation:

$$
\text{lookup}(id) = \begin{cases}
ST[S_{current}][id] & \text{if } id \in S_{current} \\
\text{lookup\_outer}(id, S_{parent}) & \text{otherwise}
\end{cases}
$$

$$
\text{lookup\_outer}(id, S) = \begin{cases}
ST[S][id] & \text{if } id \in S \\
\text{NOT FOUND} & \text{if } S = S_{predefined} \\
\text{lookup\_outer}(id, \text{parent}(S)) & \text{otherwise}
\end{cases}
$$

---

## 2.2 Type System of TinyAda

TinyAda uses a **strong, static, name-equivalent** type system.

### 2.2.1 Base Types

| Type | Domain | Default Init |
|---|---|---|
| `Integer` | $\mathbb{Z}$ (machine-precision subset) | 0 |
| `Float` | $\mathbb{R}$ (IEEE-754 subset) | 0.0 |
| `Boolean` | `{True, False}` | False |
| `Character` | ASCII printable set | `NUL` |

### 2.2.2 Type Equivalence Rules

TinyAda adopts **name equivalence** by default:

$$
T_1 \equiv_{name} T_2 \iff \text{name}(T_1) = \text{name}(T_2)
$$

This means:

```ada
type Celsius  is new Integer;
type Fahrenheit is new Integer;
A : Celsius;
B : Fahrenheit;
A := B;   -- ERROR: type mismatch (different names)
```

### 2.2.3 Type Compatibility for Operations

For an operator $\oplus$, the type compatibility relation $\sim$ is given by:

| Operator | Operand Types Accepted |
|---|---|
| `+`, `-`, `*` | `Integer` × `Integer` → `Integer`, or `Float` × `Float` → `Float` |
| `/` | `Integer` × `Integer` → `Integer` (truncating), `Float` × `Float` → `Float` |
| `=`, `/=`, `<`, `<=`, `>`, `>=` | any matching base type → `Boolean` |
| `and`, `or`, `not` | `Boolean` × `Boolean` → `Boolean` |
| `:=` (assignment) | name-equivalent types only |

The **type checking function** $\tau$ on expressions is defined recursively:

$$
\tau(E) = \begin{cases}
\text{name}(E) & \text{if } E \text{ is a literal} \\
\tau(E_1) & \text{if } E = -E_1 \text{ and } \tau(E_1) \in \{Integer, Float\} \\
\text{result}(\oplus) & \text{if } E = E_1 \oplus E_2 \text{ and } \tau(E_1) \sim \tau(E_2) \\
\tau(\text{lookup}(id)) & \text{if } E \text{ is an identifier}
\end{cases}
$$

---

## 2.3 Overload Resolution

When the same identifier `id` appears in the symbol table with **multiple declarations** (multiple functions with the same name), TinyAda uses **contextual overloading resolution**:

$$
\text{resolve}(id, \text{arg-types}) = f \in S_{visible} \text{ such that } \text{profile}(f) = (\text{modes}, \text{types}, \text{return-type})
$$

Resolution procedure:

1. Collect all visible overloads of `id` across the scope chain.
2. Eliminate those whose parameter count differs from the call.
3. For remaining candidates, check that the **argument types are name-equivalent** to the formal parameter types.
4. If **exactly one** survives → unique resolution.
5. If **zero** survive → semantic error (*no matching overload*).
6. If **more than one** survives → ambiguous → semantic error (*ambiguous resolution*).

---

## 2.4 Parameter Modes

TinyAda parameter modes and their semantic constraints:

| Mode | Direction | Must Be Writable? | Must Be Initialized Before Call? |
|---|---|---|---|
| `in` | Caller → Callee | No (read-only) | Yes |
| `out` | Callee → Caller | Yes | No (reset on entry) |
| `in out` | Both | Yes | Yes |

A formal `out` parameter is treated as a **symbolic write-target**. The semantic analyzer must verify that the actual argument is a **writable** entity (variable, not a literal or expression).

---

## 2.5 KTU High-Yield Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The following table is the single most important reference for solving Part-A and Part-B questions on this topic. Memorize it.**

| # | Concept | Formal Symbol / Rule | Engineering Use |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Scope chain lookup | $\text{lookup}(id)$ traverses $S_{current} \to S_{parent} \to \dots \to S_{predefined}$ | Resolves name visibility in nested code |
| 2 | Name equivalence | $T_1 \equiv T_2 \iff \text{name}(T_1)=\text{name}(T_2)$ | Prevents accidental type coercion |
| 3 | Type compatibility | $\tau(E_1) \sim \tau(E_2)$ | Decides if binary operator is legal |
| 4 | Overload count | $\vert\{f \in S_{visible} : \text{profile}(f)=\text{required}\}\vert$ | Disambiguates same-named subprograms |
| 5 | Scope stack | $S = [S_0, S_1, \dots, S_n]$ with $S_0 = S_{predefined}$ | Models nested block structure |
| 6 | Parameter mode `out` | actual must be *writable* (l-value) | Ensures callee can write back |
| 7 | Recursive $\tau$ | $\tau$ defined on AST structural induction | Foundation of type checker |
| 8 | Decorated AST | $T_{node} = (\text{type}, \text{pointer-to-decl})$ | Output of semantic analysis |

---

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

The principles taught through TinyAda map directly to **production compilers and IDEs**:

- **GCC's `gimple` phase** and **LLVM's Static Single Assignment (SSA) construction** both rely on a scoped symbol table and a recursive type checker mirroring TinyAda's design.
- **Java's class-loader + bytecode verifier** performs static semantic checks (type safety, definite assignment) that are direct descendants of the rules discussed here.
- **Rust's borrow checker** is a more elaborate semantic analyzer that uses lifetime scopes analogous to TinyAda's nested blocks.
- **Static analyzers** like SonarQube, Coverity, and Infer use the same symbol-table architecture to find bugs at compile time.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

This section walks through the **semantic analysis of a concrete TinyAda program**, deriving each check step by step so that the procedure is fully reproducible in an examination setting.

---

## 3.1 Input Program (Annotated)

```ada
procedure Main is
   X : Integer;
   Y : Float;

   function Square(N : in Integer) return Integer is
   begin
      return N * N;
   end Square;

begin
   X := 5;
   Y := 2.0;
   X := Square(X);
   Y := Square(X);          -- candidate semantic error
end Main;
```

---

## 3.2 Step-by-Step Trace of the Semantic Analyzer

> **Notation:** Each step is labelled with the line, the action, the symbol-table state, and the type-binding $\tau$ computed.

### Step 1 — Enter Predefined Scope $S_0$

$$
S_0 = \{\,Integer \mapsto \tau = Integer,\ Float \mapsto \tau = Float,\ Boolean \mapsto \tau = Boolean\,\}
$$

### Step 2 — Encounter `procedure Main` → Enter scope $S_1$

$$
S_1 = \{\,\}
$$

Action: `enter_scope("Main")` pushes $S_1$ onto the scope stack. Parent of $S_1$ is $S_0$.

### Step 3 — Process Declaration `X : Integer;`

- **Lookup `Integer` in scope chain:** $\text{lookup}(Integer) \to S_0$ returns type *Integer*. ✅
- **Insert** $X$ into $S_1$ with attribute record:

$$
A(X) = (\text{name}="X",\ \tau=Integer,\ \text{kind}=variable,\ \text{scope}=S_1)
$$

State after Step 3:
$$
S_1 = \{\,X \mapsto (\tau = Integer)\,\}
$$

### Step 4 — Process Declaration `Y : Float;`

Analogously, insert $Y$ into $S_1$:

$$
S_1 = \{\,X \mapsto Integer,\ Y \mapsto Float\,\}
$$

### Step 5 — Encounter `function Square` → Enter scope $S_2$

Inside Square's signature, the formal parameter $N : in\ Integer$ is processed. We insert $N$ into $S_2$ with mode attribute:

$$
S_2 = \{\,N \mapsto (\tau=Integer,\ \text{mode}=in)\,\}
$$

Return type `Integer` is recorded in the *function's own attribute record* $A(Square)$:

$$
A(Square) = (\text{name}="Square",\ \tau_{return}=Integer,\ \text{params}=[(N, Integer, in)],\ \text{scope}=S_1)
$$

Then $A(Square)$ is itself inserted into $S_1$ (so calls to `Square` from `Main` can resolve it):

$$
S_1 = \{\,X \mapsto Integer,\ Y \mapsto Float,\ Square \mapsto \text{function record}\,\}
$$

### Step 6 — Analyze Body of `Square`

Expression $N * N$:
- $\tau(N) = Integer$ (from $S_2$).
- Operator $*$ requires both operands $\sim Integer$. ✅
- $\tau(N * N) = Integer$.
- Returned value must match declared return type. $\tau_{return}(Square) = Integer$. ✅

### Step 7 — Leave $S_2$

`leave_scope()` pops $S_2$. $S_1$ becomes current again.

### Step 8 — Analyze Body of `Main`

**Line `X := 5;`**
- LHS: lookup $X$ in $S_1$ → found with $\tau=Integer$. It is a variable, so writable. ✅
- RHS: literal `5` has $\tau=Integer$. ✅
- Assignment rule: $\tau(LHS) \equiv_{name} \tau(RHS)$ → $Integer \equiv Integer$. ✅

**Line `Y := 2.0;`**
- $\tau(Y) = Float$ (writable variable). ✅
- $\tau(2.0) = Float$. ✅
- Compatible. ✅

**Line `X := Square(X);`**
- Resolve `Square`: collect visible overloads → exactly one in $S_1$ (profile: `(Integer in) → Integer`).
- Argument $X$ has $\tau = Integer$. Matches formal $N : in\ Integer$. ✅
- Call returns $\tau = Integer$.
- LHS $X$ has $\tau = Integer$. Assignment compatible. ✅

**Line `Y := Square(X);`** ← **Candidate Semantic Error**

- Resolve `Square`: only one visible overload exists. Candidate profile: `(Integer in) → Integer`.
- Argument $X$: $\tau = Integer$. Matches formal $N$. ✅ (argument check passes)
- Return type: $\tau_{return}(Square) = Integer$.
- LHS $Y$: $\tau = Float$.
- Assignment check: $\tau(RHS) = Integer \equiv_{name} \tau(LHS) = Float$? **NO.** ❌
- **Error reported:** *"type mismatch in assignment: expected Float, found Integer."*

### Step 9 — Leave $S_1$

`leave_scope()` pops $S_1$. $S_0$ (predefined) is now current. Program analysis complete.

---

## 3.3 Summary Matrix of Checks Performed

> [!NOTE]
> The following matrix is what an examiner expects when asking *"Explain the static semantic checks performed on a TinyAda program."*

| Source Construct | Semantic Check | Data Used | Failure Mode |
|---|---|---|---|
| `X : Integer;` | `Integer` resolved in scope chain | $ST$, $\tau$ | Undeclared type |
| `X : Integer; X : Float;` | Duplicate declaration in same scope | $S_{current}$ only | Duplicate identifier |
| `Z := 5;` (undeclared `Z`) | `Z` resolved in scope chain | $ST$, scope chain | Undeclared identifier |
| `A := B;` | $\tau(A) \equiv_{name} \tau(B)$ | $\tau$ table | Type mismatch |
| `A := 5 / 2.0;` | Both operands share compatible type | $\tau$, operator table | Operand type mismatch |
| `Square(2.0);` | Arg type matches formal | $\tau$, profile | Wrong argument type |
| `Square(X); Y := result;` | Return type $\equiv$ LHS type | $\tau_{return}$, $\tau(LHS)$ | Type mismatch on assignment |
| `Proc(Out_Arg → 5)` | Actual for `out` must be l-value | kind table | Non-writable actual |

---

## 3.4 Worked Example — Ambiguous Overload Resolution

Consider:

```ada
function Dup(S : in String)  return String;
function Dup(I : in Integer) return Integer;
...
Result := Dup(3);   -- Which Dup is selected?
```

**Resolution trace:**

1. Visible overloads of `Dup` in the current scope chain: two candidates $f_1, f_2$.
2. Argument count required: 1. Both candidates have arity 1. Both survive.
3. Argument type $\tau(3) = Integer$. 
   - $f_1$ expects `String` — fails. ❌
   - $f_2$ expects `Integer` — passes. ✅
4. Exactly one candidate survives $\Rightarrow$ unambiguous resolution to $f_2$.

Formally:

$$
\text{Selected}(Dup) = \{f : \text{arg-type}(3) \equiv_{name} \text{formal-type}(f, 1)\} = \{f_2\}
$$

If we had instead `Result := Dup(true);` and both `Dup(Boolean)` and `Dup(Integer)` existed, the analyzer would report **ambiguity** because the formal types differ from `Boolean` in both cases, leaving zero survivors — but if both `Boolean` and a generic `Any` were declared, two would survive, triggering ambiguity.

---

## 3.5 Derivation of the Recursive Type Function $\tau$

For an AST node representing an expression $E$, the type is computed by structural induction on the tree:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\tau(\text{literal } c) &= \text{type\_of\_literal}(c) \\
\tau(id) &= \text{lookup}(id).\tau \\
\tau(E_1 \oplus E_2) &= \begin{cases}
\text{result}(\oplus) & \text{if } \tau(E_1) \sim \tau(E_2) \\
\text{ERROR} & \text{otherwise}
\end{cases} \\
\tau(\text{call } f(\vec{a})) &= \begin{cases}
A(f).\tau_{return} & \text{if } |\vec{a}|=|A(f).\text{params}| \text{ and } \forall i,\ \tau(a_i) \equiv_{name} A(f).\text{params}[i].\tau \\
\text{ERROR} & \text{otherwise}
\end{cases}
\end{aligned}
$$

This is the **recursion** that makes a type checker a one-pass tree walk — exactly the architecture TinyAda's semantic analyzer implements.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Architecture of the TinyAda Semantic Analyzer

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Parse Tree from Syntax Analyzer] --> B[Semantic Analyzer Driver]
    B --> C{Node Kind?}
    C -- Declaration --> D[Insert into Symbol Table]
    C -- Identifier Use --> E[Lookup in Scope Chain]
    C -- Expression --> F[Compute Type tau via Recursive Walk]
    C -- Subprogram Call --> G[Overload Resolver]
    D --> H[Symbol Table Stack]
    E --> H
    F --> I[Type Compatibility Checker]
    G --> J[Argument List Matcher]
    I --> K{All Checks Pass?}
    J --> K
    K -- Yes --> L[Decorate AST Node]
    K -- No --> M[Report Semantic Error with Line Number]
    L --> N[Output: Decorated AST]
    M --> O[Output: Error Diagnostics]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:** The driver walks the parse tree depth-first. Each node is dispatched to one of four specialized handlers (declaration, identifier use, expression, call). Every handler consults the shared **Symbol Table Stack**, computes its check, and either decorates the AST node or reports a diagnostic.

---

## 4.2 Symbol Table Stack — Nested Block Structure

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S0["S0 Predefined Scope: Integer, Float, Boolean, Character"] --> S1["S1 Procedure Main: X:Integer, Y:Float, Square:function"]
    S1 --> S2a["S2 Block inside Main (if any)"]
    S2a --> S2b["S3 Innermost block"]
    S1 --> Sq["S_sq Function Square body: N:Integer"]
```

**Reading the chain:** When a nested block ends, its scope is popped. Lookups walk upward through the chain. A declaration in $S_3$ shadows (hides) any same-named entity in $S_1$ until $S_3$ is exited.

---

## 4.3 Decorated AST Example

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[AssignStmt] --> B[LHS: X  type=Integer  decl=ptr to S1.X]
    A --> C[RHS: Square call]
    C --> D[Callee: Square  return=Integer  decl=ptr to S1.Square]
    C --> E[Arg: X  type=Integer  decl=ptr to S1.X]
    F[AssignStmt Y] --> G[LHS: Y  type=Float  decl=ptr to S1.Y]
    F --> H[RHS: Square call]
    H --> I[Callee: Square  return=Integer  decl=ptr to S1.Square]
    H --> J[Arg: X  type=Integer  decl=ptr to S1.X]
    J -.-> K[ERROR: type mismatch, expected Float got Integer]
```

**Interpretation:** Each AST node carries (a) a computed type $\tau$ and (b) a pointer to the declaration entry in the symbol table that resolved it. Errors are attached to offending subtrees rather than aborting the whole analysis.

---

## 4.4 Phase Interaction Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    SRC[Source Code] --> LEX[Lexical Analyzer]
    LEX --> TOK[Token Stream]
    TOK --> SYN[Syntax Analyzer]
    SYN --> PT[Parse Tree]
    PT --> SSA[Static Semantic Analyzer of TinyAda]
    SSA --> DAST[Decorated AST]
    SSA --> ST[Updated Symbol Table]
    DAST --> ICG[Intermediate Code Generator]
    ST --> ICG
    ICG --> OPT[Optimizer]
    OPT --> CG[Code Generator]
    CG --> EXE[Executable]
```
