| Arithmetic | `+`, `-`, `*` | INTEGER $\times$ INTEGER | INTEGER | Both operands static → result static | `3 + 4 * 5 = 23` |
| Arithmetic | `/` | INTEGER $\times$ INTEGER | INTEGER (truncated) | Both static → result static | `7 / 2 = 3` |
| Arithmetic | `+`, `-`, `*`, `/` | FLOAT $\times$ FLOAT | FLOAT | Both static → result static | `3.0 * 2.5 = 7.5` |
| Mixed Numeric | `+`, `-`, `*`, `/` | INTEGER $\times$ FLOAT | FLOAT (coerced) | Both static → result static | `2 + 3.0 = 5.0` |
| Unary | `-`, `+`, `abs` | INTEGER or FLOAT | Same as operand | Operand static → result static | `-(5) = -5` |
| Relational | `=`, `/=`, `<`, `<=`, `>`, `>=` | Same / compatible | BOOLEAN | All operands static → result static | `5 < 10 = TRUE` |
| Logical | `and`, `or`, `xor` | BOOLEAN $\times$ BOOLEAN | BOOLEAN | Both static → result static | `TRUE and FALSE = FALSE` |
| Logical | `not` | BOOLEAN | BOOLEAN | Operand static → result static | `not TRUE = FALSE` |
| String | `&` (concatenation) | STRING or CHARACTER | STRING | Both static → result static | `"A" & "B" = "AB"` |
| Attribute | `'FIRST`, `'LAST`, `'SIZE` | Discrete type | INTEGER (or universal) | Always static | `INTEGER'FIRST = -2**31` |
| Conversion | `T(expr)` | T is the target type | T | Operand static → result static (with range check) | `FLOAT(5) = 5.0` |

**Boundary Conditions & Universally True Statements:**

$$
\text{Static}(c) = \texttt{true} \quad \forall \text{ literal constants } c
$$

$$
\text{Static}(\text{unary\_op}(E)) = \text{Static}(E)
$$

$$
\text{Static}(E_1 \oplus E_2) = \text{Static}(E_1) \wedge \text{Static}(E_2)
$$

$$
\text{Static}(\text{variable}) = \texttt{false}
$$

**Engineering Utility:** Static expression evaluation enables **code-size reduction**, **dead-code elimination** of branch conditions that always evaluate to `TRUE` or `FALSE`, and **predictable embedded systems** where pre-computed constants eliminate runtime arithmetic unit (ALU) cycles — a critical concern in **automotive ECU firmware, aerospace flight control software, and IoT sensor firmware**.

## 2.3 Real-World Compiler Implementations

| Production Compiler | Optimization Equivalent |
|---|---|
| GCC (`-O2`, `-O3`) | `-fcprop-registers`, `-floop-im`, constant folding pass |
| LLVM | `InstructionCombining` and `ConstantFolding` passes |
| MSVC | `/Og` (deprecated) → enabled by default in `/O1` and above |
| AdaCore GNAT | `GNATprove` (static analysis for SPARK subset) |
| TinyAda (educational) | The exact mechanism studied in Module 3 |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Note:** When examiners ask "What optimization is enabled by static expression evaluation?", the canonical answer is **constant folding**, optionally followed by **constant propagation** and **dead-code elimination**.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Worked Example 1 — Pure Constant Folding

Evaluate the TinyAda static expression:

$$
E = 2 + 3 \times 4 - 5
$$

Assume the precedence follows the standard convention where `*` binds tighter than `+` and `-`.

**Step 1 — Construct the AST respecting precedence:**

The expression parses to a left-associative tree:

$$
\begin{aligned}
E &= \big((2 + (3 \times 4)) - 5\big) \\
\text{AST root} &: \text{subtraction} \\
\text{Left child} &: \text{addition} \\
\text{Left-left} &: 2 \\
\text{Left-right} &: \text{multiplication} \\
\text{Right-right-left} &: 3 \\
\text{Right-right-right} &: 4 \\
\text{Right child of root} &: 5
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Post-order traversal (left, right, root) of the AST:**

Visit the leftmost leaf first.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Step 2a:} \quad &\text{Visit } 2 \Rightarrow \text{Return } (\text{value}=2, \text{type}=\text{INTEGER}, \text{static}=\text{TRUE}) \\[4pt]
\text{Step 2b:} \quad &\text{Visit } 3 \Rightarrow \text{Return } (3, \text{INTEGER}, \text{TRUE}) \\[4pt]
\text{Step 2c:} \quad &\text{Visit } 4 \Rightarrow \text{Return } (4, \text{INTEGER}, \text{TRUE}) \\[4pt]
\text{Step 2d:} \quad &\text{Apply } \times : (3, \text{TRUE}) \times (4, \text{TRUE}) \Rightarrow (12, \text{INTEGER}, \text{TRUE}) \\[4pt]
\text{Step 2e:} \quad &\text{Apply } + : (2, \text{TRUE}) + (12, \text{TRUE}) \Rightarrow (14, \text{INTEGER}, \text{TRUE}) \\[4pt]
\text{Step 2f:} \quad &\text{Visit } 5 \Rightarrow \text{Return } (5, \text{INTEGER}, \text{TRUE}) \\[4pt]
\text{Step 2g:} \quad &\text{Apply } - : (14, \text{TRUE}) - (5, \text{TRUE}) \Rightarrow (9, \text{INTEGER}, \text{TRUE})
\end{aligned}
$$

**Final Result:**

$$
E_{\text{evaluated}} = 9 \quad \text{with} \quad \text{Static}(E) = \texttt{TRUE}
$$

The compiler replaces the entire subtree with the constant `9` in the intermediate representation (IR), achieving **constant folding**.

---

## 3.2 Worked Example 2 — Mixed Static and Non-Static Sub-Expressions

Evaluate:

```ada
A : constant INTEGER := 10;
B : constant INTEGER := A + 5;       -- refers to a previous constant
X : INTEGER;                          -- a variable, not a constant
Y : constant INTEGER := X + 1;        -- ERROR: non-static in constant init
```

**Symbol Table State Before Evaluating `B`'s Initializer:**

| Identifier | Kind | Value | Type | Static? |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| `A` | constant | `10` | INTEGER | TRUE |
| `X` | variable | *undefined at compile time* | INTEGER | FALSE |

**Step 1 — Evaluating `A + 5`:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Lookup } A &\Rightarrow (10, \text{INTEGER}, \text{TRUE}) \\
\text{Literal } 5 &\Rightarrow (5, \text{INTEGER}, \text{TRUE}) \\
\text{Apply } + &\Rightarrow (15, \text{INTEGER}, \text{TRUE}) \\
\therefore \quad B_{\text{value}} &= 15
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Evaluating `X + 1` for `Y`'s initializer:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Lookup } X &\Rightarrow (\text{undefined}, \text{INTEGER}, \text{FALSE}) \\
\text{Literal } 1 &\Rightarrow (1, \text{INTEGER}, \text{TRUE}) \\
\text{Apply } + &:
\begin{cases}
\text{Operand 1 static?} & \text{FALSE} \\
\text{Operand 2 static?} & \text{TRUE} \\
\text{Conjunction} & \text{FALSE} \\
\end{cases} \\
\Rightarrow \text{Expression is non-static} \\
\Rightarrow \text{COMPILE-TIME ERROR: constant initializer must be static}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Conclusion:** The declaration of `Y` is **rejected by the compiler** with an error such as *"initializer expression for constant `Y` is not static"*.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Operator × Operand Staticity

The following matrix shows whether the result of applying a binary operator $\oplus$ to operands $L$ and $R$ can be statically computed. $S$ = static, $D$ = dynamic (non-static), $\checkmark$ = result static, $\times$ = result dynamic.

| $L \backslash R$ | $S$ (Static) | $D$ (Dynamic) |
|---|---|---|
| **$S$ (Static)** | $\checkmark$ (Static) | $\times$ (Dynamic) |
| **$D$ (Dynamic)** | $\times$ (Dynamic) | $\times$ (Dynamic) |

**Formal Boolean Rule:**

$$
\text{Static}(L \oplus R) = \text{Static}(L) \;\wedge\; \text{Static}(R)
$$

This is logically equivalent to a logical AND truth table. A single non-static operand poisons the entire expression.

---

## 3.4 Worked Example 3 — Type Compatibility with Mixed Numeric Promotion

Evaluate:

$$
E = 2 + 3.0 \times 4
$$

**Step 1 — Identify types of literals:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
2 &\Rightarrow \text{INTEGER literal} \\
3.0 &\Rightarrow \text{FLOAT literal} \\
4 &\Rightarrow \text{INTEGER literal}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Apply the higher-precedence operator first (`*`):**

The sub-expression `3.0 × 4` involves mixed types. TinyAda's type rules mandate **implicit conversion of the lower-precision operand to the higher-precision type**.

$$
\begin{aligned}
3.0 \times 4 &\Rightarrow 3.0 \times 4.0 \quad \text{(4 promoted to FLOAT)} \\
&\Rightarrow 12.0 \quad \text{(FLOAT result, static)}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Apply the lower-precedence operator (`+`):**

The sub-expression `2 + 12.0` again involves mixed types:

$$
\begin{aligned}
2 + 12.0 &\Rightarrow 2.0 + 12.0 \quad \text{(2 promoted to FLOAT)} \\
&\Rightarrow 14.0 \quad \text{(FLOAT result, static)}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Final Type and Value:**

$$
E_{\text{value}} = 14.0, \quad E_{\text{type}} = \text{FLOAT}, \quad \text{Static}(E) = \texttt{TRUE}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Type Promotion Rule (KTU Favorite):** When INTEGER and FLOAT operands meet at a binary arithmetic operator, the INTEGER is implicitly converted to FLOAT. The result is always FLOAT. This is called **mixed-mode arithmetic** and is a high-yield 3-mark question.

---

## 3.5 Worked Example 4 — Boolean Short-Circuit and Constant Branch Folding

Consider:

```ada
DEBUG : constant BOOLEAN := FALSE;
...
if DEBUG and (X > 0) then  -- X is a runtime variable
    Put_Line("Debug mode active");
end if;
```

**Static Evaluation:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{DEBUG} &\Rightarrow (\text{FALSE}, \text{BOOLEAN}, \text{TRUE}) \\
X > 0 &\Rightarrow \text{non-static (depends on runtime input)} \\
\text{DEBUG and (X > 0)} &\Rightarrow \text{FALSE and (X > 0)}
\end{aligned}
$$

TinyAda's static evaluator, knowing that `DEBUG = FALSE`, applies the **absorption identity** $F \wedge P \equiv F$. The entire boolean expression folds to `FALSE` at compile time. The compiler then performs **dead-code elimination**, removing the `Put_Line` call from the generated executable.

This is a real-world example of how static expression evaluation cascades into multiple optimization passes.

---

## 3.6 Full Worked Example 5 — Static Expression with Named Constants and Attributes

```ada
type DAY_TYPE is range 1..31;
PI      : constant FLOAT   := 3.14159;
RADIUS  : constant FLOAT   := 5.0;
AREA    : constant FLOAT   := PI * RADIUS ** 2;
MAX_DAY : constant INTEGER := DAY_TYPE'LAST;
```

**Evaluation of `AREA`:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{PI} &\Rightarrow 3.14159 \quad \text{(FLOAT, static)} \\
\text{RADIUS} &\Rightarrow 5.0 \quad \text{(FLOAT, static)} \\
\text{RADIUS ** 2} &\Rightarrow 5.0 ** 2 = 25.0 \\
\text{PI * 25.0} &\Rightarrow 3.14159 \times 25.0 = 78.53975 \\
\therefore \text{AREA} &= 78.53975
\end{aligned}
$$

**Evaluation of `MAX_DAY`:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{DAY\_TYPE'LAST} &\Rightarrow 31 \quad \text{(INTEGER, static — attributes are always static)} \\
\therefore \text{MAX\_DAY} &= 31
\end{aligned}
$$

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 High-Level Static Expression Evaluation Pipeline

The following Mermaid flowchart depicts the lifecycle of a static expression as it moves through the TinyAda compiler's semantic analysis phase.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Parser produces AST node for expression] --> B{AST Node Type}
    B -->|Literal| C[Return literal value and type, mark as static]
    B -->|Identifier| D[Symbol Table Lookup]
    D --> E{Is it a constant?}
    E -->|Yes| F[Retrieve folded value and type from ST]
    E -->|No| G[Mark as non-static, return dynamic flag]
    B -->|Operator Node| H[Recursively evaluate left operand]
    H --> I[Recursively evaluate right operand]
    I --> J{All operands static?}
    J -->|Yes| K[Apply operator type rules and compute folded value]
    J -->|No| L[Emit runtime evaluation code, mark expression dynamic]
    K --> M{Type compatibility check}
    M -->|Pass| N[Perform range and constraint check]
    M -->|Fail| O[Emit compile-time type error]
    N -->|Pass| P[Replace AST subtree with constant node]
    N -->|Fail| Q[Emit range constraint violation error]
    L --> R[Pass modified AST to code generator]
    P --> R
    G --> R
    O --> S[Halt compilation with diagnostic]
    Q --> S
    C --> R
    F --> R
```

## 4.2 Block Diagram — Static Expression Evaluator Components

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph IN[Input Subsystem]
        A1[AST Traversal Engine]
    end
    subgraph CORE[Evaluation Core]
        B1[Literal Resolver]
        B2[Symbol Table Interface]
        B3[Operator Dispatcher]
        B4[Type Checker Module]
    end
    subgraph OUT[Output Subsystem]
        C1[Constant Folder]
        C2[Error Reporter]
        C3[IR Generator]
    end
    A1 --> B1
    A1 --> B2
    A1 --> B3
    B1 --> B4
    B2 --> B4
    B3 --> B4
    B4 --> C1
    B4 --> C2
    C1 --> C3
```

## 4.3 Decision Tree — Is This Expression Static?

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start[Expression E arrives at evaluator] --> Q1{Is E a literal?}
    Q1 -->|Yes| Static1[Mark E as static]
    Q1 -->|No| Q2{Is E an identifier?}
    Q2 -->|No| Q3{Is E an operator expression?}
    Q2 -->|Yes| Q4{Is the identifier declared as constant?}
    Q4 -->|Yes| Q5{Was its initializer static?}
    Q5 -->|Yes| Static2[Return folded value of constant]
    Q5 -->|No| Error1[Semantic error: non-static initializer]
    Q4 -->|No| Dynamic1[Mark E as dynamic, variable reference]
    Q3 -->|Yes| Q6{All sub-operands static?}
    Q6 -->|Yes| Q7{Operator type-compatible with operands?}
    Q7 -->|Yes| Static3[Apply operator, mark E static with computed value]
    Q7 -->|No| Error2[Type mismatch error]
    Q6 -->|No| Dynamic2[Mark E as dynamic]
    Q3 -->|No| Error3[Unknown expression form]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the Diagrams:** Every green-path (static) outcome terminates either at a folded constant or at a generated runtime instruction. Every red-path (error) outcome halts compilation with a precise diagnostic referencing the source line number, identifier name, and the rule that was violated.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: Define a *static expression* in the TinyAda language. Give two examples and one counter-example. **[KTU University Exam - May 2024]**

**Model Answer:**

A **static expression** in TinyAda is an expression whose value can be determined at *compile time* without executing the program. Every operand in the expression must be a literal, a named constant with a static initializer, an attribute of a static type, or a result of operators applied to other static sub-expressions.

**Examples of static expressions:**
- `1024 * 8` → value `8192` (pure literal arithmetic)
- `MAX_SIZE - 16` (where `MAX_SIZE` is a previously declared constant)

**Counter-example:** `A + B`, where `A` and `B` are program variables (not constants). This expression is **non-static** because its value depends on runtime input.

---

### Question 2: What is **constant folding**? How is it related to static expression evaluation? **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]**

**Model Answer:**

**Constant folding** is a compiler optimization technique in which expressions composed entirely of compile-time constants are evaluated during compilation, and the resulting constant is substituted in place of the original expression in the generated code.

**Relationship to Static Expression Evaluation:**

Static expression evaluation is the *mechanism*; constant folding is the *outcome* (optimization). The static evaluator traverses the AST, determines that an expression is static, computes its value, and the constant folder then replaces the AST subtree with a single constant node. This eliminates runtime arithmetic instructions, reducing both code size and execution time.

| Static Expression Evaluation | Constant Folding |
|---|---|
| Determines *whether* a value is computable at compile time | Performs the actual *replacement* of expressions with constants |
| Semantic analysis phase | Optimization phase |
| May produce folded values | Consumes those folded values for IR rewriting |

---

## 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark Questions (Module 3 Internal Choice)

### Question A: Comprehensive Analysis of Static Expression Evaluation in TinyAda

**(a)** *[7 Marks]* Explain the algorithm used by the TinyAda compiler to compute the values of static expressions. Discuss the role of post-order traversal, the symbol table, and the staticity propagation rule. Use a labeled diagram in your explanation.

**(b)** *[7 Marks]* Consider the following TinyAda program fragment. Identify whether each declaration is legal, and for every legal constant, compute its static value. Show every step of your reasoning.

```ada
type SMALL_INT is range 1..100;
A : constant INTEGER       := 25;
B : constant SMALL_INT     := A + 10;
C : constant FLOAT         := A / 2;
D : constant INTEGER       := 3.0 + 4;
E : constant SMALL_INT     := B * 2;
F : constant CHARACTER     := 'A';
G : constant BOOLEAN       := (A < B) and TRUE;
H : constant STRING        := "Hello" & ", " & "World!";
I : constant INTEGER       := SMALL_INT'LAST;
J : constant SMALL_INT     := B + 150;
```

---

#### Model Solution to Question A

**Part (a) — Algorithm Explanation [7 Marks]:**

The TinyAda compiler evaluates static expressions through a recursive **post-order traversal** of the AST. The algorithm proceeds in three conceptual phases:

**Phase 1 — Leaf Node Resolution:**

For each leaf node (literal or identifier), the algorithm returns a 3-tuple `(value, type, isStatic)`. Literals are trivially static. Identifiers require a **symbol table lookup**: if the identifier is a `constant` whose initializer was previously folded, its stored value is returned; if it is a `variable` or an uninitialized constant, the algorithm marks the result as non-static.

**Phase 2 — Internal Node Combination:**

For operator nodes, the algorithm recursively evaluates all operands first (hence *post-order*). It then applies the **staticity propagation rule**: an expression is static if and only if **all** its sub-expressions are static. Formally:

$$
\text{Static}(E_1 \oplus E_2) \iff \text{Static}(E_1) \wedge \text{Static}(E_2)
$$

**Phase 3 — Operator Application and Type Checking:**

If the operands are static, the algorithm applies the operator's semantic rule (e.g., integer addition, floating-point multiplication, boolean AND) and verifies type compatibility. On success, the subtree is replaced with a constant node holding the folded value. On failure, a compile-time error is emitted with a precise diagnostic.

*Diagram: Refer to Section 4.1 of these notes for the complete pipeline flowchart.*

**Role of Each Component:**
- **Post-order traversal** ensures children are evaluated before parents, allowing bottom-up folding.
- **Symbol table** stores the folded value of each named constant, enabling transitive constant resolution (`A` referencing `B` referencing `C`).
- **Staticity propagation rule** determines the boundary between compile-time and runtime evaluation.

**Part (b) — Worked Solution [7 Marks]:**

| Declaration | Legality | Computed Value | Reasoning |
|---|---|---|---|
| `A : constant INTEGER := 25` | ✅ Legal | `25` | Pure literal — trivially static. |
| `B : constant SMALL_INT := A + 10` | ✅ Legal | `35` | `A` resolves to `25` (static); `25 + 10 = 35`; range check: `1 ≤ 35 ≤ 100` passes. |
| `C : constant FLOAT := A / 2` | ✅ Legal | `12.5` | `A / 2` where `A` is `INTEGER` and `2` is `INTEGER` → integer division? **No** — context is `FLOAT`, so `A` and `2` are promoted to `FLOAT`; result `25.0 / 2.0 = 12.5`. |
| `D : constant INTEGER := 3.0 + 4` | ❌ Illegal | — | **Type mismatch**: `3.0` is `FLOAT`, `4` is `INTEGER`. The result type is `FLOAT` (mixed-mode rule), but the constant is declared as `INTEGER`. No implicit narrowing allowed. Compile error. |
| `E : constant SMALL_INT := B * 2` | ✅ Legal | `70` | `B = 35` (static); `35 * 2 = 70`; range check: `1 ≤ 70 ≤ 100` passes. |
| `F : constant CHARACTER := 'A'` | ✅ Legal | `'A'` | Character literal — static. |
| `G : constant BOOLEAN := (A < B) and TRUE` | ✅ Legal | `TRUE` | `A = 25`, `B = 35`; `25 < 35 = TRUE`; `TRUE and TRUE = TRUE` (static). |
| `H : constant STRING := "Hello" & ", " & "World!"` | ✅ Legal | `"Hello, World!"` | String concatenation of static literals — static. |
| `I : constant INTEGER := SMALL_INT'LAST` | ✅ Legal | `100` | Attribute of a static type is always static. `SMALL_INT'LAST = 100`. |
| `J : constant SMALL_INT := B + 150` | ❌ Illegal | — | `B = 35`; `35 + 150 = 185`; **range check fails**: `185 > 100` violates the constraint of `SMALL_INT`. Compile error. |

**Summary of Errors:**
- `D`: Type mismatch (cannot assign `FLOAT` result to `INTEGER` constant without explicit conversion).
- `J`: Range constraint violation (result `185` exceeds `SMALL_INT`'s upper bound `100`).

---

### Question B: Alternative Comprehensive Analysis

**(a)** *[7 Marks]* Discuss, with suitable examples, the rules that govern the staticity of expressions in TinyAda. Cover at least four operator categories and explain the type promotion rule for mixed-mode arithmetic.

**(b)** *[7 Marks]* Given the TinyAda program below, draw the AST, perform post-order static evaluation, and write the final IR after constant folding. Identify any static expressions in the program.

```ada
N : constant INTEGER := 4;
M : constant INTEGER := N + 1;
P : constant INTEGER := M * M - 2 * N;
Q : constant BOOLEAN := (P > 10) or FALSE;
```

---

#### Model Solution to Question B

**Part (a) — Staticity Rules [7 Marks]:**

An expression in TinyAda is **static** if and only if every operand is statically known. The following rules govern staticity:

1. **Literal Rule:** All literals (integer, real, character, boolean, string) are static by definition. Examples: `42`, `3.14`, `TRUE`, `"abc"`.

2. **Constant Identifier Rule:** A constant identifier is static if its initializer expression is static. Examples: `MAX := 100;` (static), `Y := X + 1;` (where `X` is a variable → non-static).

3. **Attribute Rule:** Attributes of static types (`'FIRST`, `'LAST`, `'SIZE`, `'SUCC`, `'PRED`) are always static. Example: `INTEGER'LAST` is static.

4. **Unary Operator Rule:** If `E` is static, then `-E`, `+E`, `abs E`, and `not E` are static. If `E` is non-static, the result is non-static.

5. **Binary Operator Rule (General):** If $E_1$ and $E_2$ are both static, then $E_1 \oplus E_2$ is static for any operator $\oplus$ defined in TinyAda. If either is non-static, the result is non-static.

**Operator Categories with Examples:**

| Category | Operators | Operand Type | Example (static evaluation) |
|---|---|---|---|
| Arithmetic | `+`, `-`, `*`, `/`, `mod`, `rem`, `**` | Numeric | `7 mod 3 = 1`, `2 ** 10 = 1024` |
| Relational | `=`, `/=`, `<`, `<=`, `>`, `>=` | Same or compatible | `5 /= 3 = TRUE` |
| Logical | `and`, `or`, `xor`, `not` | BOOLEAN | `TRUE xor TRUE = FALSE` |
| Concatenation | `&` | String or 1-D array | `"Hi" & "!" = "Hi!"` |

**Type Promotion Rule (Mixed-Mode Arithmetic):**

When an `INTEGER` operand and a `FLOAT` operand meet at a binary arithmetic operator, the compiler implicitly converts the `INTEGER` to `FLOAT` before applying the operator. The result type is always `FLOAT`. Example: `2 + 3.5` → `2.0 + 3.5 = 5.5`. The promotion is **lossless** in one direction; no implicit narrowing from `FLOAT` to `INTEGER` is permitted.

**Part (b) — AST, Post-Order Evaluation, and Final IR [7 Marks]:**

**Step 1 — Draw the AST for `P := M * M - 2 * N`:**

Assuming `*` has higher precedence than `-`, the AST is:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Root} &: \text{subtraction} (-) \\
\text{Left child} &: \text{multiplication} (*) \\
\text{Left-left} &: M \\
\text{Left-right} &: M \\
\text{Right child} &: \text{multiplication} (*) \\
\text{Right-left} &: 2 \\
\text{Right-right} &: N
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Post-Order Traversal and Static Evaluation:**

Known constants: `N = 4`, `M = 5` (from `M := N + 1`).

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Visit } M \text{ (left of left subtree)} &\Rightarrow (5, \text{INTEGER}, \text{TRUE}) \\
\text{Visit } M \text{ (right of left subtree)} &\Rightarrow (5, \text{INTEGER}, \text{TRUE}) \\
\text{Apply } * : 5 * 5 &\Rightarrow (25, \text{INTEGER}, \text{TRUE}) \\[4pt]
\text{Visit } 2 &\Rightarrow (2, \text{INTEGER}, \text{TRUE}) \\
\text{Visit } N &\Rightarrow (4, \text{INTEGER}, \text{TRUE}) \\
\text{Apply } * : 2 * 4 &\Rightarrow (8, \text{INTEGER}, \text{TRUE}) \\[4pt]
\text{Apply } - : 25 - 8 &\Rightarrow (17, \text{INTEGER}, \text{TRUE})
\end{aligned}
$$

Therefore, `P = 17`.

**Step 3 — Evaluate `Q := (P > 10) or FALSE`:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
P > 10 &\Rightarrow 17 > 10 = \text{TRUE} \\
\text{TRUE or FALSE} &\Rightarrow \text{TRUE} \\
\therefore Q &= \text{TRUE}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Final IR After Constant Folding:**

```ada
N : constant INTEGER := 4;
M : constant INTEGER := 5;
P : constant INTEGER := 17;
Q : constant BOOLEAN := TRUE;
```

**Static Expressions Identified:** `N`, `M`, `P`, and `Q` are all fully static. Every literal and every constant reference resolved to a compile-time known value.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls in Static Expression Evaluation Questions:**
> 1. **Forgetting to mention "post-order traversal"** — Examiners explicitly award 1 mark for this term. Generic phrases like "the compiler checks the expression" lose this mark.
> 2. **Confusing *static* with *constant*** — A constant is a *declaration kind*; a static expression is a *property of an expression*. They are related but not identical.
> 3. **Missing the type promotion rule** — When asked about mixed `INTEGER + FLOAT`, students often write "the result is INTEGER" or "error". The correct answer is **implicit conversion to FLOAT**.
> 4. **Ignoring range checks** — A constant initializer like `SMALL_INT := 200` is rejected not because of staticity but because of a **range constraint violation**. Always mention this separately.
> 5. **Missing the staticity propagation rule** — The conjunction $\text{Static}(E_1) \wedge \text{Static}(E_2)$ is the formal heart of the topic. A question asking "when is `E1 + E2` static?" expects this Boolean conjunction, not a vague answer.
> 6. **Omitting the symbol table's role** — Constant identifiers cannot be resolved without symbol table lookup. Examiners want you to say "the compiler consults the symbol table to retrieve the constant's folded value".

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Definition**: A static expression in TinyAda is one whose value is known at compile time; it is composed entirely of literals, static named constants, attributes of static types, and operators applied to static sub-expressions.
- **Algorithm**: Post-order AST traversal → leaf resolution → staticity propagation → operator application → type/range checking → constant folding.
- **Staticity Propagation Rule**: $\text{Static}(E_1 \oplus E_2) = \text{Static}(E_1) \wedge \text{Static}(E_2)$. A single non-static operand makes the whole expression non-static.
- **Type Promotion**: `INTEGER` + `FLOAT` = `FLOAT`. Always promote lower-precision to higher-precision; never narrow implicitly.
- **Range Checks**: Static integer expressions in a constrained subtype must satisfy the subtype's range at compile time.
- **Constant Folding**: The direct optimization outcome of static expression evaluation — replaces AST subtrees with constant nodes.
- **Dead-Code Elimination**: A downstream optimization enabled by folded boolean expressions (e.g., `FALSE and X` → `FALSE`).
- **Symbol Table Dependency**: Static evaluation of named constants requires the symbol table to be populated and queried in declaration order (forward references are illegal in TinyAda constant initializers).
- **Attributes are Always Static**: `'FIRST`, `'LAST`, `'SIZE`, `'SUCC`, `'PRED` of any type are static.
- **Logical Short-Circuit in Constant Context**: `FALSE and X` folds to `FALSE`; `TRUE or X` folds to `TRUE` — the compiler applies Boolean algebra identities.
- **Compilation Halts on Errors**: Type mismatches, range violations, and non-static initializers in constant declarations are all compile-time errors that prevent code generation.
- **Engineering Relevance**: Static expression evaluation underpins constant folding, constant propagation, and dead-code elimination — three of the most impactful compiler optimizations in production systems (GCC, LLVM, GNAT, MSVC).

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Procedures and Environments
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PECST758 - PROGRAMMING LANGUAGES > - **Module:** Module 3 > - **Topic:** Procedures and Environments # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 What is a Procedure Activation? In programming language theory (as defined in the KTU 2024 PECST758 syllabus and drawing from Scott's *Programming Language Pragmatics*), a **procedure activation** (also called a *procedure instance* or *activation record instance*) is the concrete runtime execution of a procedure at a specific point in time, complete with its own copy of parameters, local variables, temporaries, and bookkeeping data needed to resume the caller. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Formal Definition (KTU Standard):** > An **activation** is a concrete execution of a procedure. Each procedure activation has its own **activation record** (a.k.a. **stack frame**), which is a contiguous block of memory that stores all information necessary for the procedure's execution and for returning control to the caller. ## 1.2 What is an Environment? The **environment** in a programming language is the data structure that maps **names to variables (memory locations)** at a given point during program execution. It is a *name-to-location* mapping, distinct from the **state**, which is a *location-to-value* mapping. > [!NOTE] > **Two Distinct Mappings (Scott's Framework):** > 1. **Environment:** `Name → Location` (compile-time / static concept) > 2. **State (Store/Memory):** `Location → Value` (run-time / dynamic concept) > > Together, **Environment + State = Meaning of a Name**. ## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The Office Building Imagine a large office building (the running program). Each office suite (procedure activation) has: - A **nameplate** (the procedure's name and signature) - A **filing cabinet** with local files (local variables) - A **mailbox** holding messages from the boss (parameters) - A **notepad** with the boss's phone extension to call back (return address) - An **escalator down** to the floor it was called from (dynamic link) - A **stairwell** to the floor where it was *defined* (static link) When the procedure finishes, it tears down its suite, files its report, and walks back up the **dynamic link** to resume the caller's work. The **static link** allows it to "go upstairs" to access variables from the lexically enclosing scope — even if that enclosing scope is no longer actively executing on the call stack. ## 1.4 Key Terminology Glossary | Term | Meaning | |---|---| | **Activation Record (AR)** | Stack frame holding all per-call state for one procedure execution | | **Activation Tree** | Tree showing the dynamic nesting of procedure calls at runtime | | **Lexical (Static) Scope** | Scope determined by the textual (program text) position of a declaration | | **Dynamic Scope** | Scope determined by the actual call chain at runtime | | **Static Link (Access Link)** | Pointer to the activation record of the lexically enclosing scope | | **Dynamic Link (Control Link)** | Pointer to the activation record of the caller | | **Display** | Optimised array of pointers to active scope frames for fast non-local access | # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 The Two-Stage Meaning of a Name When the compiler/interpreter sees a reference such as `x`, the meaning is derived in **two distinct stages**: 1. **Environment lookup** — finds the **location** (memory address) bound to `x` in the current environment. 2. **State lookup** — fetches the **value** stored at that location in the current state. $$ \text{Meaning of a name} = \text{Environment}(\text{name}) \rightarrow \text{Location} \rightarrow \text{State}(\text{Location}) = \text{Value} $$ This separation is the conceptual backbone of the *environment model* used in Scheme, ML, and most modern languages. ## 2.2 Components of a Standard Activation Record A typical activation record (growing downward in most architectures) contains the following fields. Their order varies by language and compiler. | Field | Purpose | |---|---| | **Return Address** | Where to resume execution in the caller | | **Dynamic Link (Control Link)** | Pointer to caller's AR — used on return | | **Static Link (Access Link)** | Pointer to lexically enclosing AR — used for non-local access | | **Parameters / Arguments** | Incoming values or references | | **Saved Registers** | Caller-save / callee-save register values | | **Local Variables** | Procedure's own local storage | | **Temporaries** | Intermediate values from expression evaluation | > [!NOTE] > **Conventions for calling protocol** (the "ABIs" of PL semantics): > - **Caller-saved:** Caller pushes the return address; callee saves any caller registers it destroys. > - **Callee-saved:** Callee preserves registers and pushes its own bookkeeping. > - In all cases, the **dynamic link** is the highway for `return`. ## 2.3 Scope Rules: Lexical vs Dynamic ### 2.3.1 Lexical (Static) Scoping The scope of a binding is determined by the **program text** — i.e., the smallest enclosing block or function body in the source code. This is the rule used by C, C++, Java, Python, ML, Haskell, Scheme, etc. ### 2.3.2 Dynamic Scoping The scope of a binding is determined by the **call stack at runtime** — i.e., the most recent active binding on the chain of pending calls. Used by early Lisp, APL, Snobol, and shell-script `export`s. ### 2.3.3 Three Implementation Strategies for Dynamic Scoping (Historical) > [!IMPORTANT] > - **Deep binding** — environment reference is captured at *procedure creation* (closure time). > - **Shallow binding** — environment is the most recent activation's bindings; assignment is fast, lookup is slow. > - **Ad-hoc binding** — environment is the caller's environment at *call time*. ## 2.4 Parameter Passing Mechanisms The "value" of a parameter passed into a procedure depends on the mechanism chosen by the language. | Mechanism | What is passed? | Effect of assignment inside callee | Languages | |---|---|---|---| | **Pass by Value** | A copy of the value | Local to callee; caller unchanged | C, Java (primitives), Pascal (default) | | **Pass by Reference** | A pointer (reference) to the caller's location | Caller's variable is modified | Fortran, Pascal `var`, C++ `&` | | **Pass by Value-Result** | Copy in, copy out | Caller's variable updated on return | Fortran (older) | | **Pass by Name (Thunk)** | A re-evaluated expression (substitution) | Evaluated each access | Algol 60 | | **Pass by Need (Lazy)** | Thunk, but evaluated only once and memoised | Same as by-name, but cached | Haskell, R `lazy` | ## 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula & Concept Sheet > [!NOTE] > The table below is a high-yield cheat sheet for KTU 2024 ESE / Module tests on this topic. **Do not use literal `|` characters inside table cells** — use `\vert` or `\mid` for any absolute-value / set-membership notation. | Symbol / Concept | Definition / Property | Typical Application | |---|---|---| | $\text{Env} : \text{Name} \to \text{Location}$ | Environment mapping | Compile-time name resolution | | $\text{Store} : \text{Location} \to \text{Value}$ | State (memory) mapping | Runtime value retrieval | | $\sigma(\text{var}) = \text{value}$ | State lookup | Variable read | | $\sigma[\text{var} := v]$ | State update | Variable write | | $\rho \vdash e : \tau$ | Expression $e$ has type $\tau$ in env $\rho$ | Type-checking judgement | | $\text{AR}_{\text{size}}$ | $\sum (\text{params} + \text{locals} + \text{temporaries})$ | Stack-depth estimation | | $\text{Deepest nesting} = \text{depth of activation tree}$ | Bound on max live ARs | Space complexity analysis | | $\text{Display}[i] = \text{ptr to AR of scope depth } i$ | Display table | $O(1)$ non-local access | | $\text{Static chain length} \leq \text{static depth}$ | Number of static links to traverse | Worst-case access time | ## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility - **Compiler Design:** Activation-record layout determines stack frame size, calling conventions, and debugging info (DWARF, .pdata). - **Garbage Collection:** Environments as first-class closures require GC to track both the code pointer and the captured environment (e.g., Java's heap-allocated `Method` objects, Python's cell variables). - **OS / Systems Programming:** Context-switch implementations use a similar save-area layout for process control blocks. - **Security & Sandboxing:** Distinguishing *lexical* from *dynamic* scope is critical for understanding variable shadowing, `eval()` injection, and lambda capture leaks (e.g., the famous JavaScript `var i` in a `for` loop bug). # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Activation Tree Construction — Worked Example Consider the following pseudo-code (Pascal-style). We will derive the **activation tree** and the **stack of activation records** as execution proceeds. ```pascal program Main; procedure A; procedure B; begin C; end; procedure C; begin B; (* recursive / mutual call chain *) end; begin C; end; begin A; end. ``` > The call sequence initiated by `Main` is: `Main → A → C → B → C → B → ...` (infinite mutual recursion — useful for illustrating stack growth). ### Step 1 — Identify Lexical (Static) Nesting The **static depth** of each procedure (number of enclosing scopes) is: $$ \text{depth}(\text{Main}) = 0, \quad \text{depth}(\text{A}) = 1, \quad \text{depth}(\text{B}) = 2, \quad \text{depth}(\text{C}) = 2 $$ ### Step 2 — Identify the Dynamic Call Chain (Activation Tree) Each call to a procedure creates a new tree node whose parent is the currently executing activation. $$ \text{Activation tree after } k \text{ calls} = \text{Main} \rightarrow \text{A} \rightarrow \text{C}_1 \rightarrow \text{B}_1 \rightarrow \text{C}_2 \rightarrow \text{B}_2 \rightarrow \cdots $$ ### Step 3 — Stack Frame Snapshot at Peak When the stack contains `Main, A, C, B, C` (the 5th activation), the dynamic links form a downward chain: $$ \begin{aligned} \text{AR}_{\text{C2}}.\text{dynlink} &= \text{AR}_{\text{B1}} \\ \text{AR}_{\text{B1}}.\text{dynlink} &= \text{AR}_{\text{C1}} \\ \text{AR}_{\text{C1}}.\text{dynlink} &= \text{AR}_{\text{A}} \\ \text{AR}_{\text{A}}.\text{dynlink} &= \text{AR}_{\text{Main}} \\ \text{AR}_{\text{Main}}.\text{dynlink} &= \text{null (bottom of stack)} \end{aligned} $$ ### Step 4 — Static Links for the Same Snapshot The static link points to the *most recent* activation of the **lexical parent**: $$ \begin{aligned} \text{AR}_{\text{Main}}.\text{static} &= \text{null} \\ \text{AR}_{\text{A}}.\text{static} &= \text{AR}_{\text{Main}} \\ \text{AR}_{\text{C1}}.\text{static} &= \text{AR}_{\text{A}} \\ \text{AR}_{\text{B1}}.\text{static} &= \text{AR}_{\text{A}} \quad (\text{B is lexically inside A, not inside C}) \\ \text{AR}_{\text{C2}}.\text{static} &= \text{AR}_{\text{B1}}.\text{static} = \text{AR}_{\text{A}} \end{aligned} $$ **Critical observation:** `C2`'s static link is `A` (its lexical grandparent), *not* `B1`. This is the key invariant of lexical scoping: you follow the **static** chain, not the **dynamic** chain, to find non-local variables. ## 3.2 Parameter Passing — Algorithmic Evaluation Consider this C-like snippet and evaluate under each parameter passing mechanism: ```c void p(int x, int y) { x = x + 1; y = y + 2; } int a = 5, b = 7; p(a, b); ``` ### 3.2.1 Pass by Value $$ \begin{aligned} x_{\text{copy}} &= a = 5 \\ y_{\text{copy}} &= b = 7 \\ x_{\text{copy}} &\leftarrow 5 + 1 = 6 \quad (\text{local update}) \\ y_{\text{copy}} &\leftarrow 7 + 2 = 9 \quad (\text{local update}) \\ \end{aligned} $$ **Final state:** $a = 5, \ b = 7$ — caller unchanged. ### 3.2.2 Pass by Reference $$ \begin{aligned} x_{\text{ref}} &= \text{ptr to } a \\ y_{\text{ref}} &= \text{ptr to } b \\ * x_{\text{ref}} &\leftarrow *x_{\text{ref}} + 1 = 6 \\ * y_{\text{ref}} &\leftarrow *y_{\text{ref}} + 2 = 9 \\ \end{aligned} $$ **Final state:** $a = 6, \ b = 9$ — caller modified through pointers. ### 3.2.3 Pass by Value-Result $$ \begin{aligned} x_{\text{copy}} &= a = 5 \\ y_{\text{copy}} &= b = 7 \\ x_{\text{copy}} &\leftarrow 6, \quad y_{\text{copy}} \leftarrow 9 \quad (\text{local}) \\ a &\leftarrow x_{\text{copy}} = 6 \quad (\text{on return, copy out}) \\ b &\leftarrow y_{\text{copy}} = 9 \quad (\text{on return, copy out}) \\ \end{aligned} $$ **Final state:** $a = 6, \ b = 9$ — same as by-reference here, *but* may differ if parameters alias (e.g., `p(a, a)`). ### 3.2.4 Pass by Name (Thunk) The actual argument is *not* evaluated at call time; instead, a thunk is created and **re-evaluated on every reference**. $$ \begin{aligned} x_{\text{thunk}} &= (\text{expr: a, env: caller}) \\ y_{\text{thunk}} &= (\text{expr: b, env: caller}) \\ x_{\text{thunk}}() &\to a = 5 \to 5 + 1 \to 6 \quad (\text{written back to } a) \\ y_{\text{thunk}}() &\to b = 7 \to 7 + 2 \to 9 \quad (\text{written back to } b) \\ \end{aligned} $$ **Final state:** $a = 6, \ b = 9$ — but with pass-by-name, *each access* re-evaluates, so loops and side effects inside the argument can be repeated. ## 3.3 Display Table — O(1) Non-Local Access For deeply nested lexical scopes, traversing the static chain can be $O(d)$ in the nesting depth $d$. A **display** is an array of pointers maintained globally, where: $$ \text{Display}[i] = \text{pointer to the most recent active AR at static depth } i $$ To access a variable at lexical depth $d$, the compiler generates: $$ \text{access} = \text{Display}[d] \rightarrow \text{slot offset} $$ - **Time complexity:** $O(1)$ (one array lookup + one indirection). - **Maintenance:** Whenever a procedure is called or returns, only the entries at depths $\geq$ the new depth need to be saved and restored (typically pushed/popped with the AR). ### Maintenance Protocol (Summary) $$ \begin{aligned} \text{On call at depth } d &: \text{push current Display}[d] \text{ onto AR; set Display}[d] = \text{new AR} \\ \text{On return from depth } d &: \text{Display}[d] \leftarrow \text{saved value from AR (pop)} \end{aligned} $$ ## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Comparing Parameter Passing Mechanisms | Mechanism | Aliasing? | Repeated Eval? | Local Re-assign Visible to Caller? | Cost | |---|---|---|---|---| | Value | No | No | No | 1 copy | | Reference | Yes (if same variable) | No | Yes | 1 pointer | | Value-Result | Only on return | No | Yes (at return) | 2 copies | | Name (Thunk) | No | Yes | Yes (per access) | thunk per arg | | Need (Lazy) | No | No (memoised) | Yes (first access wins) | 1 thunk + cache | # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Activation Record Layout (Generic Block Diagram) ```mermaid flowchart TB A["RETURN ADDRESS
(instr in caller)"]:::hl B["DYNAMIC LINK
(ptr to caller's AR)"]:::hl C["STATIC LINK
(ptr to lexical parent AR)"]:::hl D["SAVED REGISTERS
(callee/caller-save)"]:::reg E["PARAMETERS
(incoming args)"]:::param F["LOCAL VARIABLES
(locals of this proc)"]:::local G["TEMPORARIES
(scratch space)"]:::temp A --> B --> C --> D --> E --> F --> G classDef hl fill:#FFE08A,stroke:#A8741A,color:#000 classDef reg fill:#CFE8FF,stroke:#1B4F8C,color:#000 classDef param fill:#FFD9D9,stroke:#A0322D,color:#000 classDef local fill:#D6F5D6,stroke:#2C7A2C,color:#000 classDef temp fill:#E6D6F5,stroke:#5B2C8A,color:#000 ``` > **Note on layout:** The top of the frame (return address, links) is the *bookkeeping* region; the bottom holds the procedure's *working data*. Stack pointer typically points just above the temporaries. ## 4.2 Activation Tree for the Mutual Recursion Example ```mermaid graph TD M["Main
(static depth 0)"]:::root A["A
(static depth 1)"]:::l1 C1["C - call 1
(static depth 2)"]:::l2 B1["B - call 1
(static depth 2)"]:::l2 C2["C - call 2
(static depth 2)"]:::l2 B2["B - call 2
(static depth 2)"]:::l2 M --> A A --> C1 C1 --> B1 B1 --> C2 C2 --> B2 classDef root fill:#FFD700,stroke:#7A6500,color:#000 classDef l1 fill:#CFE8FF,stroke:#1B4F8C,color:#000 classDef l2 fill:#D6F5D6,stroke:#2C7A2C,color:#000 ``` ## 4.3 Runtime Stack with Static + Dynamic Links ```mermaid flowchart TB TOP["TOP OF STACK
(current activation)"]:::top AR_B2["AR of B - call 2
ret | dynlink --> AR_C2 | static --> AR_A | params | locals"]:::l2 AR_C2["AR of C - call 2
ret | dynlink --> AR_B1 | static --> AR_A | params | locals"]:::l2 AR_B1["AR of B - call 1
ret | dynlink --> AR_C1 | static --> AR_A | params | locals"]:::l2 AR_C1["AR of C - call 1
ret | dynlink --> AR_A | static --> AR_A | params | locals"]:::l2 AR_A["AR of A
ret | dynlink --> AR_Main | static --> AR_Main | params | locals"]:::l1 AR_M["AR of Main
ret | dynlink = NULL | static = NULL | params | locals"]:::root TOP --> AR_B2 --> AR_C2 --> AR_B1 --> AR_C1 --> AR_A --> AR_M classDef top fill:#FF6B6B,stroke:#7A1F1F,color:#FFF classDef root fill:#FFD700,stroke:#7A6500,color:#000 classDef l1 fill:#CFE8FF,stroke:#1B4F8C,color:#000 classDef l2 fill:#D6F5D6,stroke:#2C7A2C,color:#000 ``` > **Reading the diagram:** The *vertical* (downward) pointers are the **dynamic link** — they show "who called me." The *static links* (not shown as arrows here for clarity) point to the lexically enclosing AR, which may be *higher up* in the stack than the caller. ## 4.4 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Scope Resolution Flow ```mermaid flowchart LR SRC["SOURCE CODE
(with name reference x)"]:::src LEX["LEXICAL ANALYSER
tokenize x"]:::phase SCOPE["SCOPE CHECKER
(use current env)"]:::phase ENV["ENVIRONMENT TABLE
Name --> Location"]:::data STORE["STATE STORE
Location --> Value"]:::data VAL["FINAL VALUE of x"]:::out SRC --> LEX --> SCOPE SCOPE -- lookup name --> ENV ENV -- return location --> SCOPE SCOPE -- fetch value at location --> STORE STORE -- return value --> VAL classDef src fill:#FFE08A,stroke:#A8741A,color:#000 classDef phase fill:#CFE8FF,stroke:#1B4F8C,color:#000 classDef data fill:#D6F5D6,stroke:#2C7A2C,color:#000 classDef out fill:#FF6B6B,stroke:#7A1F1F,color:#FFF ``` ## 4.5 Display Table Maintenance — Call / Return Cycle ```mermaid stateDiagram-v2 [*] --> Idle Idle --> Calling: invoke proc P at depth d Calling --> PushingAR: create AR for P PushingAR --> SavingDisplay: save current Display d onto AR SavingDisplay --> UpdatingDisplay: set Display d = new AR pointer UpdatingDisplay --> ExecutingP: execute body of P ExecutingP --> Returning: P returns Returning --> RestoringDisplay: pop saved Display d back to Display d RestoringDisplay --> PoppingAR: deallocate AR of P PoppingAR --> Idle ``` # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) > **[KTU University Exam — Model Question, Module 3, 2024 Scheme]** ### Q1. Define an *environment* and a *state*. Explain how the meaning of a name is derived in two stages. *(3 marks)* **Model Answer:** An **environment** is a mapping from **names to memory locations** (variables) in a program. It is essentially a compile-time / static structure that determines "which storage cell does this name refer to?" A **state** (also called a *store* or *memory*) is a mapping from **locations to their current values**. It is a runtime, dynamic structure that holds the "actual contents of each memory cell." The **meaning** of a name in a program is derived in two stages: $$ \text{Step 1: } \text{Environment} \ (\rho): \text{name} \rightarrow \text{location} $$ $$ \text{Step 2: } \text{State} \ (\sigma): \text{location} \rightarrow \text{value} $$ So `Env(name) → Location → State(Location) = Value`. For example, in a block `{ int x = 5; }`, the environment maps `x` to a stack slot at offset 12, and the state maps that slot to the integer `5`. *(Examiner expects both stages explicitly mentioned.)* --- ### Q2. Differentiate between **lexical (static) scoping** and **dynamic scoping** with one example each. *(3 marks)* **Model Answer:** | Aspect | Lexical (Static) Scoping | Dynamic Scoping | |---|---|---| | **Scope determined by** | Program text — textually enclosing block/function | Call stack — most recent active binding | | **Time of resolution** | Compile time (mostly) | Run time | | **Languages** | C, C++, Java, Python, ML, Scheme, Haskell | Early Lisp, APL, Snobol, Bash `export` | | **Predictability** | Highly predictable, reproducible | Can be surprising; depends on call history | | **Implementation** | Static links / displays | Dynamic chain or A-list | **Example — Lexical:** ```c int x = 1; void p() { printf("%d", x); } // resolves x to global = 1 void q() { int x = 2; p(); } // even though q defines x = 2 main() { q(); } // prints 1 ``` **Example — Dynamic (in pseudo-Lisp):** ```lisp (setq x 1) (defun p () (print x)) (defun q () (setq x 2) (p)) (p) ; with dynamic scope, prints 2 ``` --- ## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice) > **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2024 / Model Paper, Module 3]** ### Question A — 14 Marks (Choose **either** A or B) #### (a) Explain the components of an activation record in detail. Draw the layout of a typical activation record and describe the role of the static and dynamic links. *(7 marks)* **Model Solution:** An **activation record (AR)** is a contiguous region of memory allocated on the **runtime stack** for each active procedure call. Its standard components (top-to-bottom or bottom-to-top depending on architecture) are: 1. **Return Address** — the address of the instruction in the caller to which control must return after the procedure finishes. It is saved by the caller (or by a `CALL` instruction) before the callee begins execution. 2. **Dynamic Link (Control Link)** — a pointer to the activation record of the **caller**. It is used to restore the caller's execution context on return. Following dynamic links from the top of the stack to the bottom gives the **call chain** in reverse order. 3. **Static Link (Access Link)** — a pointer to the activation record of the **lexically enclosing procedure** (the parent in the static scope tree). It is essential for accessing non-local variables in languages with nested procedures and lexical scoping (e.g., Pascal, Algol, Scheme, Python). 4. **Saved Registers** — values of hardware registers that the callee (or caller, depending on convention) is responsible for preserving. Caller-saved registers must be saved by the caller before the call; callee-saved by the callee on entry. 5. **Parameters** — the actual arguments passed to the procedure. They may be values (for pass-by-value), references (for pass-by-reference), or thunks (for pass-by-name). 6. **Local Variables** — names declared inside the procedure body. Their memory is allocated in the AR and deallocated automatically when the procedure returns. 7. **Temporaries** — scratch locations used by the code generator to hold intermediate results of expression evaluation (e.g., when computing `a + b * c`, the result of `b * c` may be stashed in a temporary slot). **Role of Static vs Dynamic Link:** - The **dynamic link** answers the question: *"Who called me, and where do I go back?"* - The **static link** answers the question: *"Where is the variable I need to read/write that is neither local nor global, but defined in my lexically enclosing scope?"* **Diagram (Mermaid):** ```mermaid flowchart TB A["RETURN ADDRESS"]:::b B["DYNAMIC LINK
(caller AR)"]:::b C["STATIC LINK
(lexical parent AR)"]:::b D["SAVED REGISTERS"]:::r E["PARAMETERS"]:::p F["LOCAL VARIABLES"]:::l G["TEMPORARIES"]:::t A --> B --> C --> D --> E --> F --> G classDef b fill:#FFE08A,stroke:#A8741A,color:#000 classDef r fill:#CFE8FF,stroke:#1B4F8C,color:#000 classDef p fill:#FFD9D9,stroke:#A0322D,color:#000 classDef l fill:#D6F5D6,stroke:#2C7A2C,color:#000 classDef t fill:#E6D6F5,stroke:#5B2C8A,color:#000 ``` --- #### (b) What is a *display*? How is it maintained across procedure calls and returns? Why is it faster than a static chain for non-local variable access? *(7 marks)* **Model Solution:** A **display** is a global array (or vector) maintained by the runtime system with one entry per **static nesting depth**. Formally: $$ \text{Display}[i] = \text{pointer to the most recently activated AR at lexical depth } i $$ **Maintenance on a call to procedure P at static depth d:** 1. Before entering P, save the current `Display[d]` value inside the new AR of P. 2. Update `Display[d]` to point to the new AR of P. 3. Execute the body of P. 4. On return from P, restore `Display[d]` from the saved value in P's AR. 5. Deallocate P's AR. **Why faster than a static chain?** - In a **static chain** implementation, accessing a variable at lexical depth $d$ in a procedure currently at depth $k$ requires traversing up to $k - d$ static links — worst case $O(d)$. - With a **display**, the same access is just two memory operations: index into `Display[d]` to get the AR pointer, then add the variable's offset — always $O(1)$. **Trade-off:** the display must be saved/restored on every call/return, but for languages with deeply nested scopes (Pascal, Algol), this is well worth it. *(7 marks complete)* --- ### Question B — 14 Marks (Alternative) #### (a) Compare the five major parameter passing mechanisms: pass-by-value, pass-by-reference, pass-by-value-result, pass-by-name, and pass-by-need. Use a common example and trace the behaviour under each. *(7 marks)* **Model Solution:** **Common Example:** ```c int a = 5, b = 7; p(a, b); void p(int x, int y) { x = x + 1; y = y + 2; } ``` | Mechanism | What is bound to x, y? | Final a | Final b | Notes | |---|---|---|---|---| | **Pass-by-Value** | Local copies of 5, 7 | 5 | 7 | No side-effect on caller | | **Pass-by-Reference** | Pointers to a, b | 6 | 9 | Caller's vars modified | | **Pass-by-Value-Result** | Copies in, copies out on return | 6 | 9 | Aliased params (e.g., p(a,a)) give results differing from by-reference | | **Pass-by-Name** | Thunks re-evaluating `a`, `b` on each access | 6 | 9 | Each access re-evaluates — can cause repeated side-effects | | **Pass-by-Need (Lazy)** | Thunks evaluated once, result cached | 6 | 9 | First access evaluates & caches; subsequent accesses reuse | **Subtle case — pass-by-name vs pass-by-reference when parameters alias:** ```c int i = 1; int a[10]; void p(int x, int y) { x = 2; y = 3; } p(i, a[i]); // pass-by-name: y = a[i] is re-evaluated after x = i, so y = a[2] = 3 // pass-by-ref: y = original a[1] = 3, but result is identical here ``` This demonstrates that **by-name and by-reference can diverge** under aliasing with side-effects. --- #### (b) Explain the *activation tree* of a program. Given a sample program, construct the activation tree and the corresponding stack of activation records showing dynamic and static links. *(7 marks)* **Model Solution:** An **activation tree** is a dynamic, runtime tree in which: - Each **node** represents one activation of a procedure. - The **parent** of a node is the activation that invoked it. - The tree grows during execution and shrinks as procedures return. **Sample Pascal-like program:** ```pascal program Main; procedure A; procedure B; begin B2; end; procedure B2; begin (* body *) end; begin B; end; begin A; end. ``` **Activation tree (single call chain):** ```mermaid graph TD M["Main"]:::root A["A"]:::l1 B["B"]:::l2 B2["B2"]:::l2 M --> A --> B --> B2 classDef root fill:#FFD700,stroke:#7A6500,color:#000 classDef l1 fill:#CFE8FF,stroke:#1B4F8C,color:#000 classDef l2 fill:#D6F5D6,stroke:#2C7A2C,color:#000 ``` **Static nesting depths:** Main = 0, A = 1, B = 2, B2 = 2. **Stack at peak (during B2's execution) — top to bottom:** | Frame (top→bottom) | Return To | Dynamic Link → | Static Link → | |---|---|---|---| | B2 | inside B | B | A (lexical parent of B2 is A) | | B | inside A | A | A (lexical parent of B is A) | | A | inside Main | Main | Main (lexical parent of A is Main) | | Main | — | NULL | NULL | > Note B2's static link skips B and points directly to A, since A is the lexically enclosing scope of B2. *(7 marks complete)* --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Where Students Commonly Lose Marks** > 1. **Confusing "environment" with "state."** Examiners repeatedly deduct 1–2 marks for using these interchangeably. Memorise: *environment = name → location; state = location → value*. > 2. **Wrong static link direction.** Students often point the static link to the *caller* (dynamic link target) instead of the *lexical parent*. This is the #1 error in Pascal/Algol-style questions. > 3. **Ignoring pass-by-name's re-evaluation.** When asked to compare parameter mechanisms, students write "by-name = by-reference," losing 2–3 marks. Show the thunk semantics explicitly. > 4. **Forgetting return addresses.** An activation record without a return address is incomplete; examiners expect all seven components. > 5. **Display vs. static-chain confusion.** The display is a **global array indexed by depth**, not a per-frame chain. Many students draw it as a per-frame structure, losing 2 marks. > 6. **Omitting activation-tree parent.** Every node's parent is the *runtime caller*, not the lexical parent — these often differ and the distinction is frequently tested. --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **Rapid-Revision Checklist — Procedures and Environments** - **Activation Record (Stack Frame):** Created on **call**, destroyed on **return**. Contains: return address, dynamic link, static link, saved registers, parameters, locals, temporaries. - **Dynamic Link** → points to **caller's AR** (used for return). - **Static Link** → points to **lexically enclosing AR** (used for non-local variable access). - **Activation Tree** → runtime tree, parent = *caller*, not lexical parent. - **Environment** = `Name → Location` (compile-time-ish). - **State / Store** = `Location → Value` (runtime). - **Meaning of a name** = `Env(name) → Loc → State(Loc) = Value`. - **Lexical Scope** → determined by **program text** (C, Java, Python, ML, Scheme). - **Dynamic Scope** → determined by **call stack** (early Lisp, Bash). - **Deep binding** for dynamic scope → captured at *closure* creation; **Shallow binding** → fast update, slow lookup. - **Pass-by-Value** → no alias, no caller-side effect. - **Pass-by-Reference** → alias possible, caller visible. - **Pass-by-Value-Result** → like by-ref, but copies on return; differs under aliasing. - **Pass-by-Name (Thunk)** → re-evaluates argument on *each* access. - **Pass-by-Need (Lazy)** → evaluates **once**, memoises result (Haskell). - **Display Table** → `Display[i]` = most recent AR at depth `i`; enables **O(1)** non-local access. - **Display maintenance** → save on call, restore on return; only the entry at the current depth changes. - **Closures** = (code pointer + environment reference) — essential for first-class functions. - **Stack depth bound** = max depth of activation tree = upper bound on simultaneously live ARs. - **Tail calls** can be optimised to reuse the current AR, breaking the "one AR per call" rule (important for functional languages). - **Common interview/KTU trick:** given nested procedures and a runtime call chain, ask for *static link* targets — always go to the *most recent* activation of the *lexical parent*. - **Engineering tip:** when debugging scope bugs, always print both the *static* and *dynamic* chain — the static chain reveals the *intended* binding, the dynamic chain reveals the *actual* call history.

topics.title: Procedure Definition and Activation
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECST758 - PROGRAMMING LANGUAGES
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Procedure Definition and Activation

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!NOTE]
> **Procedure Definition** is a syntactic unit that specifies the *identifier (name)*, the *formal parameters*, the *declarations of local entities*, and the *body (executable statements)* of a procedure. It introduces a new subroutine into the program text and binds a name to a parameterized sequence of actions.

> [!NOTE]
> **Procedure Activation** refers to the runtime mechanism that allocates a fresh *activation record (AR)* — also called a *stack frame* — on the *run-time stack* each time a procedure is *called (invoked)*, populates it with the actual arguments, machine state, return address, and storage for locals, and transfers control to the procedure body.

A procedure has two distinct lives:

1. **Static / Lexical Life** — Exists in the source code; defined once by the programmer.
2. **Dynamic / Activation Life** — Each *invocation* creates a new activation that lives temporarily on the call stack until the procedure returns.

The *point in execution* at which the activation begins is called the **activation point**. The point at which it terminates is the **return point** of that particular activation.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Restaurant Recipe

Imagine a printed *recipe card* in a kitchen:

- The **recipe card** is the *procedure definition* — it is fixed text, sits on the shelf, and is *not cooking anything by itself*.
- When the chef takes the card, gathers the listed ingredients (the *parameters*), and starts cooking — that is a *procedure activation*. Each time a customer orders the dish, a *new* activation of the same recipe happens.
- The chef writes down the *order number* (return address), the *kitchen counter they came from* (control link), the *ingredients* (parameters), and the *mixing bowls* (local variables) on a *chalkboard (activation record)* hung above the stove.
- When the dish is served, the chef erases the chalkboard (deallocation of the AR) and goes back to the order list (return).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Insight for Exams:** A *definition* is a compile-time, textual entity. An *activation* is a run-time, behavioral entity. One definition can spawn **N** activations, one for each call.

## 1.3 Standard KTU Terminology Mapping

| Term | KTU-Synonym | Meaning |
|---|---|---|
| Procedure | Subroutine / Function / Method | Parameterized named unit |
| Formal Parameter | Dummy Argument | Declared in procedure header |
| Actual Parameter | Actual Argument | Expression supplied at the call site |
| Activation Record | Stack Frame | Storage allocated per call |
| Calling Sequence | Prologue + Epilogue | Caller + Callee actions to invoke/return |
| Return Sequence | Epilogue | Actions on termination of call |
| Display | Static Link Array | Access to non-local variables |

---
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Anatomy of a Procedure Definition

A typical procedure definition (Pascal-style, used in Aho/Sethi/Ullman — the canonical PL textbook referenced for KTU Module 3) has these parts:

```
procedure  <name> ( <formal-parameter-list> );
   <local-declarations>
begin
   <body-statements>
end;
```

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Highlight:** The compiler builds a *symbol-table entry* for the procedure name containing: (i) the parameter types, (ii) the local types, (iii) the code address of the body, and (iv) the size of the activation record.

## 2.2 The Activation Record (Stack Frame)

An Activation Record is a contiguous block of memory allocated on the run-time stack when a procedure is called. Its structure is the *single most asked diagram* in KTU Module 3.

### 2.2.1 Standard Field Layout (top → bottom of stack frame)

| Field | Purpose | Set By | Used By |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Actual Parameters** | Storage for incoming arguments | Caller | Callee |
| **Return Value** | Place for the function's result | Callee | Caller |
| **Return Address** | Instruction to resume caller | Caller | Callee |
| **Dynamic Link (Control Link)** | Pointer to caller's AR | Caller | Callee |
| **Static Link (Access Link)** | Pointer to lexical parent's AR | Caller | Callee |
| **Saved Machine State** | Registers, status flags | Caller | Callee |
| **Local Variables** | Declared locals | Callee | Callee |
| **Temporaries** | Compiler-generated scratch | Callee | Callee |

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Master the following equations — they appear almost verbatim in KTU End-Semester Evaluation (ESE) questions.

$$
\text{Size}(\text{AR}_p) \;=\; \sum_{i=1}^{n} \text{Size}(\text{Parameter}_i) \;+\; \sum_{j=1}^{m} \text{Size}(\text{Local}_j) \;+\; \text{Size}(\text{Linkage}) \;+\; \text{Size}(\text{Temporaries})
$$

$$
\text{Depth of AR on stack} \;=\; 1 + \sum_{k} \mathbb{1}(\text{callee of } p \text{ is currently active})
$$

$$
\text{Number of Live Activations} \;=\; \text{Number of nodes in current activation tree}
$$

$$
\text{Memory}_{\text{stack peak}} \;=\; \sum_{p \in \text{path}(\text{main},\text{leaf})} \text{Size}(\text{AR}_p)
$$

Where:
- $\text{Size}(\text{Parameter}_i)$ = size in bytes of the $i^{th}$ parameter.
- $\text{Size}(\text{Linkage})$ = combined size of dynamic link + static link + return address (typically **8–24 bytes** on a 64-bit architecture).
- $\text{Size}(\text{Temporaries})$ = bytes for compiler-synthesized storage of intermediate expressions.
- $\mathbb{1}(\cdot)$ is the indicator function (equals 1 if the condition is true).

## 2.4 Why a Stack-Based Discipline?

Procedure activations follow **Last-In-First-Out (LIFO)** behavior because of two hard rules:

1. A procedure cannot return before all procedures *it* called have returned.
2. A call is well-nested — if $p$ calls $q$, then $q$ must finish before $p$ can finish.

This guarantees that the most recently activated procedure is always the first to finish, making the **run-time stack** the perfect data structure.

## 2.5 The Calling Sequence — Stepwise Logic

> [!NOTE]
> The *calling sequence* is split between **caller code** (executed before the CALL instruction) and **callee code** (executed at entry of the procedure). KTU expects you to remember this split.

### 2.5.1 Caller's Actions (Pre-Call)

1. **Evaluate actual parameters** and store them where the callee can find them (often in the caller's AR at fixed offsets, or pushed onto the stack).
2. **Save caller-save registers** (any register whose value the caller still needs).
3. **Push the return address** (the address of the instruction after the call).
4. **Push the dynamic link** — i.e., the current top-of-stack pointer (the *frame pointer of the caller*).
5. If static-scope rules apply, **push the static link** pointing to the AR of the lexically enclosing procedure.
6. Execute the **CALL** instruction — transfer control to the callee.

### 2.5.2 Callee's Actions (Prologue)

1. **Save callee-save registers** that will be modified.
2. **Allocate space** for locals and temporaries by adjusting the stack pointer (decrementing SP by the size of the AR).
3. Initialize locals if required.

### 2.5.3 Return Sequence (Epilogue)

1. Place the **return value** in the agreed location.
2. **Restore callee-save registers**.
3. **Restore FP (frame pointer)** from the saved dynamic link.
4. Execute **RETURN** — pop frame and jump to saved return address.

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The activation record model is the conceptual foundation of:
> - **Debugger stack traces** (e.g., `gdb`'s `bt` command).
> - **Exception handling / unwinding** (Java, C++, Rust).
> - **Coroutines and fibers** in modern OS schedulers.
> - **Just-In-Time (JIT) compilation** in JVM/CLR for escape analysis and stack allocation.
> - **Security exploits** like stack smashing and Return-Oriented Programming (ROP) — knowing AR layout is essential for cybersecurity.

---
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Worked Example — Activation Record Sizing

Consider the following Pascal-like procedure:

```
procedure P(x : integer; y : real);
   var
       a : integer;
       b : real;
       c : char;
   begin
       a := x + 1;
       b := y * 2.0;
       c := 'K';
   end;
```

Assume on a typical 32-bit architecture:
- `integer` = 4 bytes, `real` = 8 bytes, `char` = 1 byte (padded to 4), `address` = 4 bytes.

**Step 1:** Sum the parameter sizes.

$$
\sum \text{Size}(\text{Parameters}) = \text{Size}(x) + \text{Size}(y) = 4 + 8 = 12 \text{ bytes}
$$

**Step 2:** Sum the local sizes.

$$
\sum \text{Size}(\text{Locals}) = \text{Size}(a) + \text{Size}(b) + \text{Size}(c) = 4 + 8 + 4 = 16 \text{ bytes}
$$

(One byte of `c` is rounded to 4 due to word-alignment; many compilers pad.)

**Step 3:** Compute the linkage overhead (return address + dynamic link + static link = 3 × 4 = 12 bytes).

$$
\text{Size}(\text{Linkage}) = 3 \times 4 = 12 \text{ bytes}
$$

**Step 4:** Assume one compiler-generated temporary of 4 bytes for the expression `y * 2.0`.

$$
\text{Size}(\text{Temporaries}) = 4 \text{ bytes}
$$

**Step 5:** Apply the master formula.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Size}(\text{AR}_P) &= 12 + 16 + 12 + 4 \\
&= 44 \text{ bytes}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Answer:** The activation record of procedure **P** occupies **44 bytes** on the run-time stack.

---

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Action-by-Action During Activation

The following table consolidates the *who does what* sequence. This matrix is a **favourite 7-mark KTU question**.

| Step | Phase | Actor | Action | Stack Effect |
|------|-------|-------|--------|--------------|
| 1 | Pre-call | Caller | Evaluate actuals, store in temp slots | No net change yet |
| 2 | Pre-call | Caller | Save caller-save registers | $\downarrow$ (push) |
| 3 | Pre-call | Caller | Push return address | $\downarrow$ 4 bytes |
| 4 | Pre-call | Caller | Push dynamic link (old FP) | $\downarrow$ 4 bytes |
| 5 | Pre-call | Caller | Push static link (lexical parent) | $\downarrow$ 4 bytes |
| 6 | Transfer | CPU | Execute `CALL` instruction | — |
| 7 | Prologue | Callee | Save callee-save registers | $\downarrow$ |
| 8 | Prologue | Callee | Allocate locals & temporaries | $\downarrow$ (Size(AR)) |
| 9 | Prologue | Callee | Set new FP = current SP | — |
| 10 | Execute | Callee | Run body statements | No stack change |
| 11 | Epilogue | Callee | Place return value in agreed slot | — |
| 12 | Epilogue | Callee | Restore callee-save registers | $\uparrow$ |
| 13 | Epilogue | Callee | Restore old FP from dynamic link | — |
| 14 | Transfer | CPU | Execute `RETURN` | $\uparrow$ 12 bytes |
| 15 | Post-call | Caller | Retrieve return value | — |

Notation: $\downarrow$ = stack grows downward (toward lower addresses), $\uparrow$ = stack shrinks.

---

## 3.3 Activation Tree Derivation — Walk-Through

Consider this nested call program:

```
begin
   A( );
end.
```

```
procedure A;
begin
   B( );
   C( );
end;
```

```
procedure B;
begin
   D( );
end;
```

```
procedure C;
begin
   E( );
end;
```

```
procedure D;
begin
   (* no calls *)
end;
```

```
procedure E;
begin
   (* no calls *)
end;
```

### Step-by-step construction of the activation tree:

**Step 1:** The execution starts in the *main program*, creating activation $\text{A}_0$ (the anonymous main activation).

$$
\text{Root} = \text{A}_0
$$

**Step 2:** Main calls A, so activation $\text{A}_1$ becomes a child of $\text{A}_0$.

$$
\text{A}_0 \longrightarrow \text{A}_1
$$

**Step 3:** A calls B, so $\text{B}_1$ is a child of the *currently active* node, which is $\text{A}_1$.

$$
\text{A}_1 \longrightarrow \text{B}_1
$$

**Step 4:** B calls D, so $\text{D}_1$ is a child of $\text{B}_1$.

$$
\text{B}_1 \longrightarrow \text{D}_1
$$

**Step 5:** D finishes. We **return** to B; B finishes. We return to A. The next statement in A calls C.

$$
\text{A}_1 \longrightarrow \text{C}_1
$$

**Step 6:** C calls E, so $\text{E}_1$ is a child of $\text{C}_1$.

$$
\text{C}_1 \longrightarrow \text{E}_1
$$

**Step 7:** E finishes, C finishes, A finishes, main finishes.

The complete activation tree:

$$
\text{Tree} = \{\text{A}_0 \rightarrow \text{A}_1 \rightarrow \text{B}_1 \rightarrow \text{D}_1\} \;\; \cup \;\; \{\text{A}_1 \rightarrow \text{C}_1 \rightarrow \text{E}_1\}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Tip:** At any instant, the *currently active* activations are precisely the nodes on the path from the root to the most-recently-created leaf. Their ARs are the ones currently on the run-time stack.

---

## 3.4 Algebraic Property — Stack Depth

For an activation tree, the **maximum stack depth** equals the length of the longest root-to-leaf path.

$$
\text{StackDepth}_{\max} = \max_{\pi \in \text{Paths}} \left(\, \sum_{n \in \pi} \text{Size}(\text{AR}_n) \,\right)
$$

> [!WARNING]
> A common student error: confusing **stack depth (number of frames)** with **stack size (bytes)**. KTU questions often ask for one and the other; do not interchange them.

---

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Diagram — Activation Record Layout

The following Mermaid block renders a clean, exam-ready schematic of a single activation record. **All node IDs are alphanumeric-prefixed and contain no special characters**, complying with Mermaid v10 safety rules.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A0["TOP OF STACK Lower Address"] --> A1["ACTUAL PARAMETERS"]
    A1 --> A2["RETURN VALUE SLOT"]
    A2 --> A3["RETURN ADDRESS"]
    A3 --> A4["DYNAMIC LINK Control Link"]
    A4 --> A5["STATIC LINK Access Link"]
    A5 --> A6["SAVED MACHINE STATE"]
    A6 --> A7["LOCAL VARIABLES"]
    A7 --> A8["TEMPORARIES"]
    A8 --> A9["BOTTOM OF STACK Higher Address"]
    style A0 fill:#e6f3ff,stroke:#003366
    style A9 fill:#ffe6e6,stroke:#660000
```

> [!NOTE]
> In the conventional downward-growing stack, the **TOP** is at the lower address and the **BOTTOM** is at the higher address. Each `CALL` pushes a new frame at the TOP.

---

## 4.2 Mermaid Diagram — Activation Tree for the Worked Example

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    mainNode["A0 Main Program"] --> aNode["A1 Procedure A"]
    aNode --> bNode["B1 Procedure B"]
    bNode --> dNode["D1 Procedure D"]
    aNode --> cNode["C1 Procedure C"]
    cNode --> eNode["E1 Procedure E"]
    dNode -.returns.-> bNode
    bNode -.returns.-> aNode
    eNode -.returns.-> cNode
    cNode -.returns.-> aNode
    aNode -.returns.-> mainNode
    style mainNode fill:#fff2cc,stroke:#806000
    style aNode fill:#d5e8d4,stroke:#82b366
    style bNode fill:#dae8fc,stroke:#6c8ebf
    style dNode fill:#f8cecc,stroke:#b85450
    style cNode fill:#dae8fc,stroke:#6c8ebf
    style eNode fill:#f8cecc,stroke:#b85450
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The dashed edges represent *returns* and exist only to convey control flow. The solid edges form the **activation tree proper**.

---

## 4.3 Mermaid Diagram — Block-Level Calling Sequence Flow

This is the *block-level functional architecture* of the calling sequence, mapping how control and data flow between caller, callee, and the run-time stack.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph CALLER["CALLER CODE BLOCK"]
        C1["Step 1 Evaluate Actuals"]
        C2["Step 2 Save Caller Registers"]
        C3["Step 3 Push Return Address"]
        C4["Step 4 Push Dynamic Link"]
        C5["Step 5 Push Static Link"]
        C6["Step 6 Execute CALL Instruction"]
    end
    subgraph STACK["RUN TIME STACK MEMORY"]
        S1["AR of Caller Top of Stack"]
        S2["AR of Callee New Frame"]
    end
    subgraph CALLEE["CALLEE CODE BLOCK"]
        P1["Step 7 Save Callee Registers"]
        P2["Step 8 Allocate Locals and Temporaries"]
        P3["Step 9 Set New Frame Pointer"]
        P4["Step 10 Execute Body Statements"]
    end
    subgraph RETURN["RETURN SEQUENCE"]
        R1["Step 11 Place Return Value"]
        R2["Step 12 Restore Registers"]
        R3["Step 13 Restore Old Frame Pointer"]
        R4["Step 14 Execute RETURN Instruction"]
        R5["Step 15 Caller Resumes Execution"]
    end
    C1 --> C2 --> C3 --> C4 --> C5 --> C6
    C6 --> P1
    P1 --> P2 --> P3 --> P4
    P4 --> R1 --> R2 --> R3 --> R4 --> R5
    C6 -.allocates.-> S2
    R4 -.deallocates.-> S2
    style CALLER fill:#e1f5ff,stroke:#0277bd
    style CALLEE fill:#e8f5e9,stroke:#2e7d32
    style RETURN fill:#fff3e0,stroke:#ef6c00
    style STACK fill:#f3e5f5,stroke:#6a1b9a
```

> [!NOTE]
> The dashed edges show **physical stack effects** (allocation / deallocation of memory), while solid edges show **control flow**. This distinction is a KTU favourite for "explain with block diagram" questions.

---

## 4.4 Summary Table — Activation vs. Definition

| Aspect | Procedure Definition | Procedure Activation |
|---|---|---|
| When | Compile time | Run time |
| Where | Source code / symbol table | Run-time stack |
| Quantity | One per procedure name | One per call |
| Longevity | Entire program | Until the call returns |
| Contains | Syntax, semantics, parameter types | Data: params, locals, links |
| Created by | Programmer | Calling sequence |

---
