# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam — May 2024]`
**Define procedure activation. What is an activation record? List any four of its fields.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> [!NOTE]
> **Procedure Activation** is the runtime process of invoking a procedure, which involves allocating a new *activation record* on the run-time stack, transferring control to the procedure body, and later deallocating the frame upon return.

An **activation record (AR)**, also called a **stack frame**, is a contiguous block of memory on the call stack that holds all information needed for a single execution of a procedure. The four standard fields are:

1. **Actual Parameters** — values supplied at the call site.
2. **Return Address** — instruction in the caller to resume from.
3. **Dynamic Link** — pointer to the caller's AR.
4. **Local Variables** — storage for the procedure's local entities.

*(1 mark for definition of activation, 1 mark for AR definition, 1 mark for four fields.)*

---

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]`
**What is an activation tree? State any two properties.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

An **activation tree** is a directed tree in which each node represents a unique activation of a procedure, and the parent of a node represents the activation that invoked it. The root represents the activation of the main program.

**Properties:**
1. **LIFO Discipline:** A node remains on the tree as long as its descendants are active. A node is removed (along with its subtree) only after all its descendants have returned.
2. **Path-to-Leaf Property:** At any moment, the activations that are currently live are exactly those on the path from the root to the currently executing leaf. Their ARs are the ones on the run-time stack.

*(1.5 marks for definition, 1.5 marks for two properties.)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks)

> [!NOTE]
> As per the **KTU 2024 Scheme ESE pattern**, Part B questions carry 14 marks with **internal choice** between two alternatives. Both alternatives below are fully solved.

---

### **Question A `[KTU University Exam — June 2024]`**

**(a)** Explain in detail the various components of an activation record. Illustrate with a neat diagram. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Describe the calling sequence of a procedure with the actions performed by the caller and the callee. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution for Q.A(a) — Components of an Activation Record (7 marks)

An activation record is a structured block of memory allocated on the run-time stack whenever a procedure is called. Its components, from the top of the stack downward, are:

1. **Actual Parameters** *(1 mark)* — The values (or addresses) of the arguments supplied by the caller. The callee reads them at fixed offsets from its own frame pointer.

2. **Return Value Slot** *(0.5 mark)* — A reserved location where the callee places the result of a function. Its size depends on the return type (e.g., 4 bytes for `int`, 8 for `double`).

3. **Return Address** *(1 mark)* — The address of the instruction in the caller where execution must resume after the callee returns. Pushed by the caller's `CALL` instruction.

4. **Dynamic Link / Control Link** *(1 mark)* — A pointer to the AR of the caller. Used during return to restore the caller's frame pointer.

5. **Static Link / Access Link** *(1 mark)* — A pointer to the AR of the *lexical (static) parent* of the current procedure. Required to access non-local variables in languages with nested scopes (e.g., Pascal, Ada).

6. **Saved Machine State** *(0.5 mark)* — Contents of registers the callee will modify (callee-save convention) and condition codes.

7. **Local Variables** *(1 mark)* — Storage for the procedure's locally declared variables. Allocated at compile time at fixed offsets from the frame pointer.

8. **Temporaries** *(0.5 mark)* — Compiler-generated scratch storage for intermediate sub-expressions (e.g., the result of `a*b` before adding `c`).

9. **Diagram** *(0.5 mark)* — see the Mermaid diagram in Section 4.1 of these notes.

---

#### Model Solution for Q.A(b) — Calling Sequence (7 marks)

The calling sequence is the **coordinated set of actions** performed by the caller, the CPU, and the callee to set up, execute, and tear down a procedure invocation.

**Phase 1 — Caller's Pre-Call Actions** *(3 marks)*

1. **Argument evaluation:** The caller evaluates each actual parameter and stores it in a temp slot (often in the caller's AR).
2. **Register saving:** All caller-save registers whose values are still needed are pushed onto the stack.
3. **Push return address:** The address of the instruction after the `CALL` is pushed.
4. **Push dynamic link:** The current frame pointer (top of caller's AR) is saved as the dynamic link.
5. **Push static link** *(if statically scoped)*: Pointer to the lexical parent's AR is pushed.
6. The `CALL` machine instruction transfers control to the callee's code address.

**Phase 2 — Callee's Prologue** *(2 marks)*

1. **Save callee-save registers** that will be modified.
2. **Allocate storage** for locals and temporaries by decrementing the stack pointer.
3. **Set new frame pointer** (`FP = SP`).

**Phase 3 — Return (Epilogue)** *(2 marks)*

1. Place the return value in the agreed slot.
2. Restore callee-save registers.
3. Restore the old frame pointer from the dynamic link.
4. Execute `RETURN` — the CPU pops the return address and jumps back to the caller.
5. The caller resumes execution and may retrieve the return value.

> [!TIP]
> **Exam Tip:** Mention **who sets each field**. In the KTU marking scheme, the action-by-actor split (caller vs. callee) carries full marks.

---

### **Question B `[KTU University Exam — June 2024 — Internal Choice]`**

**(a)** Construct the activation tree for the following program and state the order in which activations terminate. **(7 marks)**

```
main( )
   { A( ); B( ); }

A( )
   { C( ); D( ); }

B( )
   { E( ); }

C( ), D( ), E( )   { /* leaf — no calls */ }
```

**(b)** Explain parameter passing mechanisms — *call by value, call by reference, call by name* — with examples. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution for Q.B(a) — Activation Tree (7 marks)

**Step-by-step construction** *(5 marks for tree + 2 marks for termination order)*:

1. Main calls A: $\text{main} \to \text{A}_1$.
2. A calls C: $\text{A}_1 \to \text{C}_1$. C finishes immediately.
3. A calls D: $\text{A}_1 \to \text{D}_1$. D finishes immediately.
4. A finishes; main now calls B: $\text{main} \to \text{B}_1$.
5. B calls E: $\text{B}_1 \to \text{E}_1$. E finishes; B finishes; main finishes.

**Activation Tree (rendered using ASCII for the answer sheet):**

```
            main
           /    \
         A1      B1
        /  \      \
      C1    D1     E1
```

**Order of termination** (LIFO, deepest-first):

$$
\text{C}_1 \;\to\; \text{D}_1 \;\to\; \text{A}_1 \;\to\; \text{E}_1 \;\to\; \text{B}_1 \;\to\; \text{main}
$$

**Justification:** The activation tree enforces strict LIFO discipline — a node finishes only after all its descendants have finished. Therefore, leaves $\text{C}_1$ and $\text{D}_1$ finish first (children of $\text{A}_1$), allowing $\text{A}_1$ to finish; similarly for the right subtree.

---

#### Model Solution for Q.B(b) — Parameter Passing Mechanisms (7 marks)

Parameter passing defines *how* actual arguments are bound to formal parameters.

**1. Call by Value** *(2 marks)*
- The actual parameter is *evaluated* and its *value* is copied into the formal parameter's storage in the callee's AR.
- The callee operates on a *local copy*; modifications do **not** affect the caller.
- Example (C, Pascal):

```
void swap(int a, int b) {
    int t = a; a = b; b = t;     /* does NOT swap caller's variables */
}
```

**2. Call by Reference** *(2.5 marks)*
- The *address* of the actual parameter is passed; the formal parameter becomes an *alias* of the actual.
- The callee can read and modify the caller's variable directly.
- Example (Pascal `var`, C++ reference, Fortran):

```
procedure swap(var a, b : integer);
   var t : integer;
begin
   t := a; a := b; b := t;       /* DOES swap caller's variables */
end;
```

**3. Call by Name** *(2.5 marks)*
- The actual parameter is *textually substituted* for the formal parameter in the callee's body — essentially macro-expansion.
- Re-evaluated **each time** the formal is used. Can produce surprising results (e.g., the famous *Jensen's Device*).
- Used in Algol 60; rare in modern languages but pedagogically important.
- Example (Algol 60):

```
procedure inc(i) := i := i + 1;
```
If `i` is a complex expression, it is re-evaluated at every use.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Comparison Table for Quick Recall:**

| Mechanism | What is passed | Caller variable modified? | Re-evaluation? |
|---|---|---|---|
| Value | Value (copy) | No | No |
| Reference | Address | Yes | No |
| Name | Re-evaluated text | Yes (if assigned) | Yes (each use) |

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Deduction Traps in KTU Exams:**
>
> 1. **Confusing "definition" with "activation"** — Definition is *static/compile-time*; activation is *dynamic/run-time*. Examiners deduct 1 mark if you mix these.
> 2. **Forgetting the action-by-actor split** — When describing the calling sequence, you MUST clearly state which actions are the *caller's* and which are the *callee's*. Vague answers like "the system pushes things onto the stack" lose 2–3 marks.
> 3. **Mixing static link and dynamic link** — Dynamic link points to the *caller*; static link points to the *lexical parent*. These are DIFFERENT in nested procedures. Examiners frequently test this distinction.
> 4. **Drawing the activation tree incorrectly** — Each node represents *one* activation, not the procedure itself. Multiple calls produce *multiple* nodes with subscripts (e.g., $\text{A}_1, \text{A}_2$).
> 5. **Forgetting the return value slot** — In a *function* (not just a procedure), the AR must reserve space for the return value; omitting this field costs a mark.
> 6. **Omitting the time-complexity justification** — When asked about stack depth, justify with the LIFO property of the activation tree.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist — Module 3: Procedure Definition and Activation**
>
> - **Procedure Definition** = *static*; exists in source code; introduces name, parameters, locals, and body.
> - **Procedure Activation** = *dynamic*; created at run-time for *each call*; lives until return.
> - **Activation Record (AR)** is a stack frame holding: actuals, return value, return address, dynamic link, static link, saved state, locals, temporaries.
> - **Size(AR) = Σ parameter sizes + Σ local sizes + linkage overhead + temporaries.**
> - **Dynamic Link** → points to **caller's** AR (run-time parent).
> - **Static Link** → points to **lexical parent's** AR (compile-time parent).
> - **Calling Sequence** is split into *caller's pre-call actions* and *callee's prologue*.
> - **Return Sequence** = *callee's epilogue + caller's post-call resumption*.
> - **Run-time stack** is LIFO because of nested, well-bracketed call structure.
> - **Activation Tree** — root is `main`; parent-child = call relationship; LIFO removal of subtrees.
> - **Path to current leaf** = the live activations = the contents of the stack.
> - **Three parameter-passing mechanisms:** value (copy), reference (address), name (textual re-substitution).
> - **Be precise about units** — distinguish *number of frames* from *bytes on stack*.
> - **One definition → many activations** (this single sentence has earned 2 marks in past KTU papers).
> - **Activation record layout** is the most diagram-heavy sub-topic — practice drawing it from memory.
> - **Activation tree construction** requires careful attention to *temporal order* of calls, not textual order.
